title: Penn State women’s lacrosse hosts Johns Hopkins for White Out
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After falling to then-No. 4 Northwestern Sunday, Penn State will have one last chance at a home victory.

The Nittany Lions will host No. 7 Johns Hopkins at 6 p.m. Thursday for the White Out.

Last year, the Blue Jays came out on top in both the regular season and postseason matchups, giving the blue and white something to fight for.

All-time matchups

While Johns Hopkins has had the upper hand over the last several years, the Nittany Lions hold a 17-12 all-time advantage in the rivalry.

After opening with five straight wins from the blue and white, the series went back and forth between both squads before the Blue Jays took dominant control. Since 2021, Johns Hopkins has won seven of the eight games played between the two teams.

In the previous season’s finale, the Nittany Lions fell 12-11 in overtime.

Attacker Erika Ho led the way for the blue and white, scoring four goals. However, it was Taylor Hoss who took the Blue Jays to overtime and Lacey Downey who scored the winning goal on her home turf.

A week later, both teams traveled to College Park, Maryland, as Johns Hopkins put on an even more dominant performance in the Big Ten quarterfinals.

Ho led the way again for the blue and white, but only had two goals this time around. On the other hand, six Blue Jays had two goals, leading to a 13-8 victory.

2026 Blue Jays

After earning a second round appearance in the 2025 NCAA Tournament, Johns Hopkins has continued its domination with a 12-3 record this year.

The Blue Jays' success is evident through six ranked wins this season, including a 13-6 victory over No. 6 Michigan on Sunday.

One of the biggest success stories out of Baltimore is senior Ava Angello, who leads the team and sits second in the Big Ten and sixth in the nation with 55 goals. Downey sits just behind with 41 goals, good for fifth in the conference.

Outside of the two leaders, Hoss is also back for her junior year, and currently sits third on the team with 63 points. With 14 starts, she’s scored 29 goals and 34 assists.

When the Blue Jays upset Michigan Sunday, Downey led the way with five goals.

Thursday’s matchup

While Johns Hopkins is the higher-ranked team heading into Thursday’s contest, both teams have had their fair share of struggles over the past few weeks.

For the Blue Jays, goalkeeper Ashley Langdon ranks at the bottom of the Big Ten with just a 37.6% save rate.

That could be an issue when the Nittany Lions host them, however, as three players have scored over 30 goals on the year.

Kelly MacKinney, Rocquette Allen and Payton Wainman have combined for 114 goals on the season, serving as a major part of Penn State’s high-scoring offense.

However, that offense could struggle against Johns Hopkins' dominant defense, which has caused 207 turnovers this year, 53 more than the next-highest team.

title: Penn State football draft profile | WR Kyron Hudson utilizes size as bigger receiver, run-blocker
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Kyron Hudson took a chance last offseason, transferring from USC with the hopes of making a bigger name for himself and securing his future.

But things didn’t work out the way he hoped in his final season. Hudson’s yard total saw nearly a 50% decline from his junior year as his yardage fell from 462 yards the year before to just 288 yards with Penn State.

Despite the underwhelming season, the 6-foot-1 receiver has valued his time at Penn State and the opportunity to play collegiate football.

“Man, I'm blessed,” Hudson said at the Penn State Pro Day. “I just want to thank God for the opportunity he gave me to live this dream out, and I'm living it.”

Following the season, Hudson trained in Naples, Florida, for over two months, seeking advice from NFL stars and USC lettermen Amon-Ra St. Brown and Drake London as he goes through the draft process.

While Hudson isn’t considered Penn State’s top receiver heading into the draft, he attacked pro day with a chance to improve his stock.

“I was super excited waking up out of my sleep, so excited just to get here,” Hudson said. “It's been a dream of mine, just to be here.”

Metrics

Height: 6-foot-1

Weight: 212 pounds

Bench press: 15 reps

Vertical jump: 30.5 inches

Broad jump: 9-foot-7

40-yard dash: 4.64 seconds

Pro shuttle: 4.50 seconds

3-cone drill: 7.47 seconds

Projections

Projected round drafted: Undrafted

Player comparison: Trent Sherfield Sr.

Scouting report

While Hudson has the height and hands to be successful in the NFL, his explosiveness and speed are blaring weaknesses in his skillset, as shown by his performance at the Penn State Pro Day.

Hudson’s broad jump and vertical jump would’ve ranked second-to-last among wide receivers at the NFL Scouting Combine, while his 40-yard dash time would’ve tied for last among receivers.

Despite these struggles, the 6-foot-1 receiver showed signs of success with his run-blocking, as he played a big role in both the pass and run game. In Penn State’s win against Nebraska this year, Hudson blocked a free rusher, allowing Andrew Rappleyea to take the ball in for a touchdown.

During his junior year at USC, Hudson proved to be a reliable target, catching every pass that was thrown at him. He entered his first season at Penn State with just a 2.7% career drop rate, although that number increased in 2025.

In his senior season with the Nittany Lions, Hudson only started six games with the Nittany Lions this season, a dropoff from the year before, when he started in nine games. Hudson finished fifth in total receptions for Penn State, but had an impressive 12.5 yards per reception.

Going into the draft, Hudson isn’t listed on PFF’s draft board, which highlights the top-450 prospects in the 2026 NFL Draft.

Best team fit: New York Giants

During his pro day, Hudson talked about meeting with multiple teams over the past few weeks, including the New York Giants.

The Giants could be a potential destination for Hudson given their wide receiver struggles in recent years.

During the 2025 season, the team's expected top wide receiver Malik Nabers went down with a season-ending injury after four games, leaving eventual quarterback Jaxson Dart without many weapons.

While Hudson likely wouldn't enter the league as a starting wide receiver, he could provide some depth to the Giants receiving corps that’s struggled in recent years.

title: Senior Column | What was that?
author: Jadzia Santiago | The Daily Collegian
description:
Dear undergrad,

What the hell was that?

As rhetorical (and pained) as that sounds, I’m genuinely asking. Now that they’re coming to a close, I have no category to put the last four years into.

Were these the best years of my life thus far? Yes. Were they also the worst? Yes!

I suppose I feel this way because there was never a dull moment, which meant that I was never bored. This got a bit overwhelming at times, but that wasn’t always a bad thing.

Sometimes, when I had some time alone after something happened and I could just stand there, panting under the immensity of it all, it was actually quite beautiful.

During those moments, I was always filled with gratitude, because I knew all that beauty couldn’t have come from just me. Who did it come from, you ask?

Well, let me tell you, and thank them along the way.

First, to everyone I spoke to for articles during my reporting days, thank you. It was an honor to hear your stories and share them with the world, and I’ll never stop being grateful for your trust.

To the Collegian, thank you for encouraging me to chase down these stories. It was grueling, humbling and the most worthwhile thing I’ve done in my young life thus far. I may not do it anymore, but I’d do it all over again.

Thank you for teaching me that I can do hard things. Oh, and thank you for my friendship with Julia Frederick, who can only be described as a light in the dark.

To Mercedes, editor-in-chief extraordinaire, I’ve been awed by you from the start. You’re the best journalist I know and I’m lucky to have met you and become your friend. Mark my words, the world will hear your name so often they’ll get sick of it, but I never will.

W.O.R.D.S., my Sisyphean boulder and favorite group of poets, it’s been an honor to teach and learn from you in equal measure. You’ve made every Wednesday worth it with your patience and resilience. I’ve loved being your president, and (for better or worse) I’ll never know what I did to deserve it.

Last and most of all, I thank my wonderful friends, without whom I wouldn’t be half the woman I am today. Thank you for the long nights, bonkers stories, the what-the-hell debriefs in elevators, bathrooms and various domiciles. I love you always.

And you, reader, one of the select few inhabitants of the void I shout into, what do I have to say to you?

Well, as you’ve probably gathered, my undergraduate career was cartoonishly chaotic, filled with grief and growth and has made me near-unrecognizable.

But as much as I joke about it, I’m not upset. In fact, I’m grateful for every moment I was knocked on my ass.

When I started at the Collegian and Penn State as a whole, I had no idea what I was doing, and I still don’t. But my time here’s taught me that I might never, and that’s okay.

Par for the course, actually. What I had, though, was belief.

Not in heroes or miracles (though I sometimes hoped for them during exams). Rather, I believed in people, in language and the power of those two things to change minds.

Over these years, I’ve seen that belief come to life through the people I’ve met and the things I’ve written and learned. I’ve come to understand myself as part of a collective whole, one created by the stories we share.

There are many theories as to why language — specifically narrative — exists, which I’ve gone over in my CAS major. They’re pretty sound (although I’m not sure of science’s opinion on that word), with exceptions and nuances.

And my English major posits just one more: language exists so that we can point to other human beings and say, Light. And I can point to you and say, Hope.

I was going to joke in this column that I’m graduating a total cynic and can only wish that you’ll be better than me. However, I realized that’d be too easy, and we don’t do easy around here.

The wonderful words I’ve heard and shared have made me realize that, despite my reservations and slight embarrassment to admit it, I have some faith in the world — mostly because I have faith in you.

Yes, yes, laugh at me, call me stupid or put me in a cringe compilation. I’m being sincere.

I need you to know that your story needs to be told, and that your words matter. Despite what many pseudo-intellectuals and AI proponents have been yelling about, humanity still has worthwhile things to say.

You have worthwhile things to say.

Sure, sometimes you’ll be knocked flat on your back with nowhere to turn — and many times, it’ll be your own stupid fault.

I mean, look at me and my college career. There are many things I regret. Maybe one day I’ll grow into also being sorry for them.

But I’ve kept my promises, disappeared from lives and places, gotten things done. So, there will be achievement, too. There’s goodness. There are things you can point to and say, Light, Hope.

I’d call it miraculous that we have sounds for those things, but really, it’s just human.

Thank you for hearing my and other people’s stories. I believe that yours might just change a mind one day, and I want you to tell it.

I love you, you delightful screwup. Go have the time of your life.

title: Senior Column | A love letter to my home
author: Veronica Figg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State has been home to me since the minute I first set foot on campus. I remember feeling an indescribable feeling when I stepped into the HUB-Robeson Center (HUB) at my admitted students day before my freshman year. I only knew that I felt like I belonged here.

I soon conceptualized this as feeling like I was home. Throughout my four years here, I’ve considered Penn State as much my home as my real home back with my parents. So much so that I would sometimes accidentally say I was going back “home” when I was going back to school after breaks.

What makes a place a home is the life you build within it. Penn State is home to me because of the relationships I’ve formed here and the activities I've gotten involved in. My freshman year roommate was randomized and became one of my best friends. I made many new friends through being a scholar in the Schreyer Honors College and also living in the Schreyer Honors College dorm. I also made friends through the organizations I joined. I was one of those college students who got involved as soon as possible, attending the infamous HUB lawn involvement fair as a first-semester freshman and putting my name on practically every club signup that interested me, which was a lot.

Three organizations were a large part of my life at Penn State. The one that defined me the most was my service sorority, Omega Phi Alpha. It was through my sorority that I found sisterhood and I’ve loved every minute I spent with my new member class and the rest of my sorority sisters. I will carry the friendships that I made in my sorority with me far beyond college.

Being in my sorority also opened up the opportunity for me to pursue a leadership role in the Panhellenic community at large. I was the vice president of Wellness for the Panhellenic Council, where I planned wellness events for the entire sorority community of over 4,000 girls. I loved being able to connect with sorority members outside of my own sisterhood. I was also a member of the Overall Recruitment Team and loved guiding new members through the sorority recruitment process to find their own sisterhoods.

I’m also a facilitator for Greeks Care, an organization within the Relaionship Violence Outreach, Intervention and Community Education Center at Penn State that educates the sorority and fraternity community about sexual assault prevention. As vice president of Wellness, I also founded the Sexual Violence Awareness and Prevention Board, a group made up of multiple campus organizations that come together to create and implement campus-wide programming for sexual assault awareness and prevention.

The board is my proudest achievement at Penn State. I’m proud that I’ve left my mark by working to end the epidemic that is sexual assault on college campuses, and I know that the students on the roundtable will continue that mission after my graduation.

I also had the opportunity to be on two newspapers throughout my time here. I started off at HerCampus, where I wrote lifestyle and career articles. I even earned recognition in 2023 for writing “Why Getting An Internship the Summer After Your First Year of College is a Good Idea.” This article won Best Career Feature for 2023 from HerCampus.

I started writing for the Collegian in my third year of college and started on news. I became the Collegian advice columnist on the opinion staff in my fourth-year during my first semester. In my last semester, I’m the food reviewer for the Collegian on lifestyle. I liked the variety of writing I’ve gotten to pursue on the different staffs and I’ve loved writing for the Collegian.

In a perfect ending to my time here, I was chosen by the Department of Sociology and Criminology as the criminology student marshal for the College of Liberal Arts. I’m honored to represent my major at commencement in May.

To current first-year, second-year and third-year students, I want to leave you with some advice, as that was my expertise earlier this year. Get involved. A place is a home if you make it one. Find friends, clubs and organizations that bring you joy; that’s what makes Penn State your home.

Lastly, to the incoming first-years, the Penn State Class of 2030, welcome home. If you weren’t football fans before, you will be now.

We Are.

With Love,

Veronica

Criminology Student Marshal

Penn State Class of 2026

title: Column | Exploring Penn State women’s lacrosse’s strengths, weaknesses ahead of postseason
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State has two games left of the regular season before the Big Ten Tournament kicks off next week.

Despite this, the Nittany Lions have struggled to create a consistent identity over the last few weeks, dominating a pair of lower-tier Big Ten opponents while looking almost non-existent at times against top-tier competition.

With an NCAA Tournament appearance dependent on the next stretch of games and Penn State’s conference tournament success, here’s a look at some current strengths and weaknesses for coach Kayla Treanor’s squad.

Strength: Offensive depth

In Penn State’s 19-9 win against Oregon last week, four players scored a hat trick, with three recording five or more points.

The trio of Kelly MacKinney, Rocquette Allen and Payton Wainman has been unstoppable over the past few weeks, scoring 39, 38 and 37 goals on the year, respectively.

Allen earned five freshman of the week honors this year as she’s recorded four five-goal games.

Outside of goal-scoring, junior Delaney Radin leads the team with 71 points, including a Big Ten-best 47 assists — the second most in school history.

In addition to those four high-scoring stars, Erika Ho has played a big role, earning a spot on the Tewaaraton Award Watch List. Despite being injured midway through the season, Ho has rebounded and recorded a total of 24 goals and 10 assists this year.

Weakness: Inconsistent performance

Penn State’s back-and-forth performance was best on display against Northwestern on Sunday.

After going up 2-0 against one of the best teams in the country, the Nittany Lions allowed seven straight goals from the Wildcats before scoring four straight for an attempted late-game comeback.

Following the game, Treanor said her team has to play full games and be consistent, especially on offense.

That same conversation was had earlier this season as the blue and white attempted to come back from an 11-4 deficit with a 7-3 second half, ultimately being too little, too late, against Princeton in March.

Strength: Goalie reliability

While Sydney Manning has been Penn State’s starter in all 15 games, Treanor has eyed her three backups at various times throughout the season.

Emily Bette, Arden Jansen and Makenna Gagliardi have combined for over the length of three games this season, as Jansen has been the primary backup.

Each of the three has posted save percentages above 50%, with Jansen leading the way with 16 saves this season.

For Manning, she posts the second-best save percentage in the Big Ten at 49.4%.

Weakness: Draw controls

Arguably one of the biggest issues facing the Nittany Lions revolves around draw controls, a weekly discussion in Treanor’s postgame media sessions.

While senior Emma Kelly has totaled 102 draw controls this season, she and the team have struggled in Penn State’s losses, limiting the opportunities the blue and white have had to score.

Northwestern had a 21-9 advantage on draw controls Sunday, highlighted by just two controls by Kelly. Instead, it was Ho who led the way with four draw controls, still far less than the Wildcats.

In the other three losses this season, the Nittany Lions have been out-drawn 49-26, proving to be a difference-maker in those big games.

title: Senior Column | Prepare for lift off
author: George Keene | The Daily Collegian
description:
My American father, raised in Philadelphia, and my Palestinian mother, raised in Jerusalem, grew up in two very different worlds. They encountered different challenges, celebrated different triumphs and started their life stories from opposite sides of the world.

In 2002, their paths crossed, and together, they took me on a journey that couldn't have been more perfect, a journey that has led me to where I am today.

A little over 22 years ago, I was born in the American Hospital of Dubai, approximately 6,980 miles from University Park, Pennsylvania. Just 15 months later my brother, Kevin, was born in May 2005, and 15 months after that my sister, Christina, joined the family.

After Dubai, we spent four years in Bulgaria before moving to Cairo, where I attended Cairo American College. Moving there during the revolution in the early 2010s as an 8-year-old was a shock to my system. Halfway through our stint in Egypt, my mother, brother, sister and I were evacuated back to the United States. We were separated from my father for about half a year, and it was challenging living in the States while my dad had to continue to work in a place filled with instability and violence. Nobody tells you how to navigate switching schools in the middle of the school year and how to deal with a family being apart.

A couple of years later, we moved to Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia, where I attended grades seven through nine. These three years at the International School of Kuala Lumpur changed the course of my life. I played sports, met people from around the world and learned several important life lessons, most importantly, the value of diversity.

In 2019, I moved to Nairobi, Kenya, where I spent half a school year at the International School of Kenya, before the COVID-19 pandemic sent us back to the United States again. We were separated from my father again, and we lived in a Residence Inn hotel in Crystal City, Virginia, before finally finding our next home in York, Pennsylvania.

In 2022, I stood on a stage in my high school gym and delivered a speech to my fellow high school graduates. In that speech, I shared that changing the world seems like a daunting task, but together, with each of us creating our own little waves, we could create a tsunami that could change the world. This remains true and is becoming more necessary than ever. Despite all that was happening, I believed there were better days ahead.

Six years later, here I am, writing this column in State College, Pennsylvania, and little has changed.

We continue to live in a world where diversity isn’t celebrated, but attacked. We live in a world where hate, war and violence overshadow love, diplomacy and peace. We stand idly by and watch injustice happen, we allow others to spread hate-filled rhetoric and we don’t stand up against evil.

Every graduate from every university across the world is about to embark on their own life story. Each of us is in charge of our own. Choose to write a story filled with happiness, kindness and love. Don’t be afraid of change, embrace diversity and savor the good moments and fight through the bad.

I’m so thankful to have attended Penn State York and University Park. I met so many people who have impacted me and pushed me further than I could have imagined. I’ve met people here who give me hope that the world can become a better place.

I want to thank Mila Sanina, Steve Reighard and Steve Sampsell for mentoring me, pushing me and guiding me as I find my own way.

I want to thank my friends and co-editors from The Daily Collegian, Zach Karpf, Andrew Deal, Emma Lessick, Teresa Phelan, Matt Cropp, Chris Eustler, Kaitlyn Murphy, Sam Simonic, Alex Osman, Evan Smith and AJ Piazza. The Collegian isn’t a special place because of what we do, but because of who we do it with.

To my closest friends, Owen Bricker, Kian Seifi, Sumit Ghimire, Gavin Wilson, Sean Brown and Ibrahim Hobrom, thank you for being there for me and pushing me to be a better person, student and friend.

To Zach Benzel, little did I know that when we met by coincidence over three years ago, you would become one of my best friends. You know how important family is to me, and how hard it is for me to be away from home. But you made it easier, because you became like family to me and someone I could always count on. There’s no other person I would’ve rather shared this experience with.

Sido and Tata, thank you for showing me what true strength looks like. You showed me that true strength comes from within, it’s resilience, standing up for what you believe in and never giving up. If it's possible to win the game we call life, there’s no greater winner in this world than you.

Baba and Mama, thank you so much for teaching me the importance of being kind, accepting, honest, hard-working and faithful. You’ve given me a lifetime worth of memories and experiences in 22 years, and I hope that one day I can make your lives as easy as you made mine. You were there to support me at every game, every academic event and every other moment that mattered to me. I could always count on seeing your two faces in the crowd, and Mama saying, “Hands up! Find your man!”

Kevin and Christina, I’m so grateful to be your big brother, and thank you for pushing me to always be better and stay true to myself. You guys make me want to be a good role model, and you guys don’t know how much I look up to you.

I don’t know what’s next or where I’ll be three months, six months or ten years from now. What I do know is that the experiences I’ve had at Penn State, the people I’ve met around the world and my family will help light the way.

MORE SENIOR COLUMNS

title: Penn State football draft profile | DT Alonzo Ford Jr. willing to do dirty work to make NFL
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Penn State hasn’t had many defensive tackles enter the NFL, but Alonzo Ford Jr. could add to that short number in the coming weeks.

The Nittany Lions have just three defensive tackles in the big leagues entering the 2026 NFL Draft, but that number could nearly double in two weeks.

Ford began his career at Old Dominion, tallying 72 total tackles across his two seasons with the Monarchs. He then made his way to Happy Valley for his final two seasons of collegiate action, seeing significantly less time in a much stronger defensive line room.

However, Ford made himself a viable option as a rotational defensive tackle and a consistent member on special teams.

“They like what I can do on the field and how I play aggressively,” Ford said at Penn State Pro Day. “Doing the dirty work nobody don’t want to do, and I take pride in doing that dirty work. So they like that.”

Metrics

Height: 6-foot-2

Weight: 293 pounds

Vertical jump: 27 inches

Broad jump: 8-foot-7 inches

Bench press: 24 reps

Projections

Projected draft round: Undrafted

Player comparison: Marcus Harris

Scouting report

While Ford never filled the stat sheet with Penn State, he was a consistent interior defensive lineman for the team over the past couple of seasons and started all but one game in 2025.

Ford notched 32 tackles across his two seasons on teams that were thought to be in contention for a national title. However, he didn’t record a single sack, but recorded an interception during Penn State’s game against FIU last year.

Those lower numbers came because of the rotation used over the past couple seasons. That rotation included likely draftee Zane Durant in addition to other defensive tackles around his size.

The Richmond, Virginia, native consistently added size during his time in college. In 2022, he was listed at 273 pounds, while 2025 had him at over 300 pounds.

Now listed at 293 pounds at Penn State’s Pro day, Ford felt he could “move a little more fluid” as a more undersized defensive tackle. With Ford’s willingness to do the dirty work and ability to play on special teams as a consistent member of the field goal blocking unit, he could carve out a role as a special teamer on a pro squad.

Best team fit: New York Jets

The New York Jets’ defensive line isn’t as strong as it’s been in previous years after trading Quinnen Williams to the Dallas Cowboys. They bolster a current defensive tackle group that would start T’Vondre Sweat and Harrison Phillips.

Ford had a visit with the Jets for a local day, already having that connection. While they have seven defensive tackles listed on the depth chart, it’s a position that is useful for depth and a spot that could work in special teams.

With the interior defensive line unit not having many proven moments, Ford could make a name for himself in training camp, allowing him to make the roster or be a member of the practice squad with a chance at elevation during the season.

title: Downtown State College meal deals that actually hit
author: Lexi Cannan | The Daily Collegian
description:
There are plenty of meal deals all over downtown State College — you just have to know when to go. From wing nights and happy hours to huge portions that somehow last two days, it’s very possible to eat well without spending your entire bank account.

Spending $15 on food and still being hungry 20 minutes later should honestly be illegal. Nothing is worse than opening the fridge an hour later like the meal never even happened. Some places don’t make their deals obvious, but once they’re found, they quickly become part of your weekly routine.

If saving money is the goal but good food is still a priority, these downtown spots have meal deals that are actually worth planning around.

Tadashi

First up is Tadashi, where lunch deals make sushi and ramen way more affordable.

Its lunch specials are the main deal to know, offering lower prices on popular items compared to dinner. Rolls, ramen and combo meals are cheaper during the day, which makes a big difference if sushi is usually seen as a “treat” meal.

Going during lunch hours is key here. The same meal that might feel expensive at night suddenly becomes much more manageable, which makes it easier to go more often without feeling like too much is being spent.

It’s also a good option when the usual rotation of pizza and fast food starts to get repetitive. The real deal is knowing when to go.

Yallah Taco

Yallah Taco is one of the easiest places downtown to spend less and still get a filling meal.

Their deals are built into the menu. Tacos start around $4 each, and buying multiple brings the price down even more. Burritos and bowls are around $10-$12 and are filling enough on their own.

They often have deals like discounted burritos or tacos, which makes it even better to time your visit right. The portions are good and the food is fast, making it an easy go-to.

Primanti Bros.

Next is Primanti Bros., where the deals actually make a difference. Their half-price happy hour means most drinks behind the bar are half off, which is tough to beat.

This makes it one of the best places to cut costs on food and drinks. They have cheap slices and drink specials, so it’s easy to stop in without spending too much. Even a small order actually feels like a good deal.

The sandwiches come packed with fries and coleslaw, so one order is basically a full meal. The portion alone makes it feel more worth it.

Cafe 210 West

If you’re looking for daily deals, Cafe 210 is a go-to spot downtown. There’s always a special, whether it’s drinks, apps or food deals like burger baskets.

Going on the right day can make a difference in price, especially when you’re with a big group splitting pitchers or appetizers. It’s the kind of place where planning ahead actually pays off.

Plaza Mexican Bar and Grill

It’s a great spot for deals, especially earlier in the evening at Plaza Mexican Bar and Grill. Happy hour runs Monday through Thursday from about 4-6 p.m., with cheap margaritas, beer and cocktails. It’s a simple way to keep the cost down.

Along with that, there are often daily specials depending on the day, including taco deals, combo meals and drink specials. These rotate, but they usually make it possible to get a full meal without spending too much.

It’s a good pick when something more sit-down is wanted, but still with deals that actually make it worth it.

Buffalo Wild Wings

Buffalo Wild Wings is all about deals. Their weekday happy hour has cheaper apps and drinks, so going earlier just makes more sense. They’re known for buy-one-get-one wings on Tuesdays (traditional) and Thursdays (boneless), which makes it cheaper.

Splitting food with friends makes the deal even better. It’s one of the easiest places to plan around a deal and actually see the difference in price.

Final Thoughts

Where you go matters, but when you go matters more. Between happy hours, daily deals and portions that last way too long, it becomes a lot easier to spend less. Once the good deals are found, everything else feels way too expensive for no reason.

At that point, paying full price just feels wrong.

title: ‘They are loved just as they are’ | Local churches provide inclusive community for queer students
author: Saxtyn Lutz | For the Collegian
description:
Many students at Penn State have likely come across the preachers who commonly stand outside the HUB-Robeson Center or at the Allen Street Gates, condemning those in the LGBTQ+ community to hell. However, they’ve also likely passed by the large pride flag over the entrance of Faith United Church of Christ (UCC) on the corner of College Avenue and Locust Lane.

Debates over whether queerness and faith can coexist are commonplace in modern dialogue. Local churches such as Faith UCC and the Wesley Foundation are aiming to provide safe spaces for queer Christians, in an effort to dispel the notion Christianity condemns those belonging to the LGBTQ+ community.

Faith UCC is currently led by its first openly queer minister, the Rev. Jes Kast. She became the settled pastor in 2019, which she said was a bold step for the church. This Sunday, Kast and her wife will be baptizing their baby at Faith UCC.

“It's one thing to fly the flag and say, ‘Oh, we're allies,’ but it's another thing to say, ‘No, our pastor is queer herself,’” Kast said.

Kast said society leads many students to believe that they cannot be both queer and Christian, which is something she often sees within newcomers at Faith UCC. She said it’s incredible to help students find peace with their identity and for them to no longer feel the need to defend themselves.

“They see a message of Jesus in the gospel that welcomes and loves all people,” Kast said. “‘Love your neighbor as yourself,’ and that is the work that we're called to do.”

Cam Mallet stumbled across Faith UCC during his search for a LGBTQ-friendly congregation.

“The number of people I know in our congregation who came to this church just because there was a pride flag out front is very large, and we can never take it down because it's our best recruiting tool,” Mallett, a sixth-year graduate student studying kinesiology, said.

He said his first time attending the church’s Sunday service was also his first time leaving a church feeling good. So, he kept going back.

“There was a very long period of my life where I couldn't imagine myself ever standing in front of a group of people and saying that I was gay, much less a faith community, and that everybody would still be willing to love and care for me after that,” Mallett said.

Emma Steinebronn, a member of Faith UCC, was also attracted to the church by the pride flag.

Steinebronn, a fifth-year graduate student studying physics, said there’s recently been a movement of biblical scholarship to focus on love. She said it’s understood within the church that love should come first in any way, shape or form and people should be allowed to love who they love.

“Many of us come from backgrounds where queerness and religion are incompatible,” Steinebronn said. “Many of us have found a home in Faith Church, because that is a place where not only queerness and faith are compatible, they’re honored. Because God made you who you are and part of that is your queerness.”

Mallet said by definition, queerness is being set apart from the normative standard. However, at Faith UCC, there are many young and old members of the congregation who identify with the LGBTQ+ community.

“Rather than feeling you're weird or this minority for being queer in our church, you're not in the minority,” Steinebronn said. “There's a lot of queer people and so it's just a very welcoming environment to be in.”

While the large pride flag outside of Faith UCC is a downtown staple, they aren’t the only church along College Avenue that’s openly accepting of LGBTQ+ students. Adjacent to Faith UCC, St. Paul’s United Methodist Church also welcomes and affirms those of every gender identity, gender expression and sexual orientation.

The Rev. Sarah Voigt of St. Paul’s United Methodist Church serves as director of the Wesley Foundation, a student organization connected with the church based in Abba Java Coffeehouse.

Years ago, it painted the rainbow pride flag on a large table in the middle of Abba Java. This was done as a pushback against anti-LGBTQ+ people who were spending a lot of time in that space. Despite initial backlash, according to Voigt, the Wesley community ensured the table remained.

“Our space tries to tell the story of who we are,” Voigt said.

Voigt said it’s important to show LGBTQ+ students they have an inclusive space because of the amount of harmful messaging that surrounds them.

“You walk down the street, and there's people who claim to be Christians who are yelling hate at people, making judgments, offering words of destruction and devastation,” Voigt said. “I want our student community to know that the messages they have often heard and continue to hear from so many different Christians is not the only story and they are loved just as they are.”

Ella Strickland said it’s hurtful to those in the queer community who are involved in Christianity because it drives them away from the religion. She said she feels as if she’s being judged based on who she associates herself with.

“Just because I'm gay doesn't mean I can't be a Christian,” Strickland, a third-year studying agricultural and extension education, said. “I think it's very alienating to assume that we can't coexist. Like, those two identities or those two parts of me can't be in conversation with each other.”

Strickland is Wesley Foundation’s event planner. She said the organization provides her with a sense of community that she hasn’t found anywhere else.

Every Wednesday at 6 p.m., the Wesley community gathers for a free meal in the Abba Java Coffeehouse. Strickland said she enjoys coming to these because she feels open to talk about herself without shielding her personality or sexuality.

“I can be who I am outside of the church, but when I come into this community, y'all say y'all don't care, but I feel like you're asking me to leave a part of myself at the door just to make everyone else feel more comfortable,” Strickland “That doesn't happen at Wesley.”

MORE CAMPUS COVERAGE

title: Leonhard Center staff launch website to explore AI applications in engineering
author: Jonathon Chiu | The Daily Collegian
description:
Since its founding in 190, the Leonhard Center for the Enhancement of Engineering Education has supported the evolution and expansion of the College of Engineering’s curriculum. Most recently, the center spearheaded the launch of a new website: AI for Engineering Education.

Launched on Feb. 4, the website features videos geared toward faculty and graduate teaching assistants (GTA) in the College of Engineering detailing artificial intelligence’s (AI) potential application in policy creation and teaching. The project is spearheaded by Ibukun Osunbunmi, who co-created the website in response to inquiries from faculty and as part of his wider initiative to explore artificial intelligence applications in the College of Engineering.

“AI is not primarily an educational tool,” Osunbunmi said. “It's a consumer-based tool that is forced to serve an educational purpose, and it's disrupting how we teach. It's disrupting how we learn.”

He said he felt the creation of videos for the website’s content was the best way to reach a broad audience and answer potential questions about how AI can be used in education The website is designed to allow for anyone, regardless of their AI proficiency, to be able to learn something new about AI, according to Osunbunmi.

“Whether you are a super high AI use person or you don't want to have anything to do with AI, the website is created in such a way that it can take care of you, wherever you are coming in from,” Osunbunmi said. “No matter how much you know, there's something new you could use.”

He said he plans to update the website based on visitor feedback and further observation. Furthermore, Osunbunmi said he wants to preserve student autonomy when using agentic, which is more autonomous than traditional AI models as it has the capacity to act independently, and brainstorm how to lessen students' usage of AI to cheat.

Bono Shih, who works alongside Osunbunmi in the Leonhard Center, said AI has rapidly evolved since December 2024, which is when they began working on the website initiative.

Though he can see other colleges pursuing a similar endeavour, he said he found it important to understand the different circumstances of how it can be used at Penn State.

“Because these video modules, especially Ibukun’s modules, are also for GTAs, we also talk about how they use AI for engineering research — it's really different from creative writing, for example,” Shih said. “AI is not value-free and AI is not context-free, but users need to be attentive to use cases and how approaches and how those AI uses have to align with the demands for certain knowledge about, for example, teaching.”

Shih said it's also important to speak with other faculty to gain alternate viewpoints on how to draft and implement AI policies that can be added to the website via future videos. He said his attendance of education conferences in 2025 helped him learn how faculty from other colleges have begun to include input from students when drafting AI policies.

“My initial perspective when I developed my own videos was that educators have a responsibility to think it through before they invite students to know about why we have to have that AI policy,” Shih said. “For students to recognize that AI policy because they are participants, no longer just students who follow the rules, is kind of a new philosophy that would be something I want to use.”

Sarah Zappe, the Leonhard Center’s director, said she saw the website as a breakthrough for faculty to improve their teaching. The website also coincides with a wider push to understand AI within the College of Engineering.

“The Leonhard Center every year funds projects from proposals written by faculty in the College of Engineering and almost all of them have something to do with AI, which is really interesting because in the past the topics varied a lot more,” Zappe said. “I just was looking at all the titles and it's, I would say, probably close to 80 to 90% are probably AI-related, so it's obviously a topic that people are interested in.”

She said the website comes at a time when learning how to leverage AI is necessary. People are inundated with the “doom and gloom” portrayal of AI and told critical thinking skills and creativity are at risk for students.

She said AI tools can be used to enhance learning rather than replace it.

“We don't really have a choice — AI is here,” Zappe said. “We have to figure out how to use it, so I think trying to aim for better is a better pathway than trying to shut it down completely, which is not realistic.”

MORE CAMPUS CONTENT

title: ‘The future is bright for us’ | Penn State softball freshmen continue to shine in victory over Bucknell
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State bounced back from its three-game losing streak in an emphatic way on Tuesday night, defeating Bucknell 11-3 in five innings.

The Nittany Lions offense hit on all cylinders at the right moment, as the team looked to regain some much-needed momentum before heading to Columbus, Ohio, for an important weekend series with Ohio State.

“This was a big night for us, we needed to get back into the 1-0 column, and, you know, I think we did a lot of things really well,” coach Clarisa Crowell said. “We just were able to put together a lot of quality at-bats, and we stole some bases here and there, so I really liked what the offense did.”

The blue and white finished the night with 14 total hits, eight of which went for extra bases, including three home runs that provided a major spark. Penn State built the bulk of its offensive momentum off a six-run third inning that shifted the tone of the game and featured three long balls, energizing the dugout.

“I think all year, you've really seen our team build off of each other's at-bats, and we talk about that all the time, even just in practice of using each at bat as research,” junior infielder Michela Barbanente said. “So I think even just today, like we saw from at-bat to at-bat, those adjustments being made and not having those back-to-backs, but then also, if somebody's successful, what are they doing.”

The Nittany Lions’ big offensive night was especially highlighted by its freshman bats, which continued to play a significant role in the team’s success. Breanna Hanik, Allison Oneacre, and Gabby Gradishar each turned in strong performances, all going 2-for-3 at the plate.

However, the production from rookies in the box is nothing new and has been key for the blue and white all season.

“The freshman class, they've obviously had a huge impact, just overall, on our team, and yes, their production tonight was awesome,” coach Crowell said. “ I think it just speaks volumes about just who they are as athletes, but they're just great kids, which makes it even better when you see kids like that performing, and the future is bright for us.”

Hanik was the most productive bat for the blue and white, recording four RBIs with a double and a three-run home run, additionally coming across to score three times.

Both of Oneacre’s hits were doubles, including one for an RBI. Gradishar’s lone RBI was the cherry on top of Penn State’s Tuesday night performance, as she recorded a sacrifice fly to walk-off the game in run-rule fashion.

“The thing is, like, it doesn't feel like we're youngsters, the upperclassmen just treat us, like we're all equal, and we're all one big family,” Hanik said. “I think that's what we all always build off of, because it’s just another day, just like with my girls.”

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's hockey blueliner Jarod Crespo inks ATO deal with San Jose Barracuda
author: Elliott Inscho | The Daily Collegian
description:
Another Nittany Lion will skate on professional ice.

Jarod Crespo has signed an Amateur Tryout Agreement with the San Jose Barracuda, the AHL affiliate of the top-flight San Jose Sharks.

The 6-foot defenseman skated in 37 games during Penn State’s 2025-2026 campaign, scoring five goals and notching 12 assists. Throughout his career with the blue and white, he appeared in 141 games, recording 42 points over four seasons.

Crespo, a Seven Lakes, North Carolina, native, will join Aiden Fink, Carter Schade, Charlie Cerrato, Matt DiMarsico and Kevin Reidler as Nittany Lions to sign a pro deal in recent weeks.

The Amateur Tryout Agreement allows Crespo to appear in AHL games for the Barracuda without losing his amateur status.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's volleyball middle blocker Rose earns third EIVA Defensive Player of the Week honor
author: Keira Albert | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State's Owen Rose earned his third EIVA Defensive Player of the Week title on Monday.

𝟳𝘅 𝙀𝙄𝙑𝘼 𝘿𝙚𝙛𝙚𝙣𝙨𝙞𝙫𝙚 𝙋𝙡𝙖𝙮𝙚𝙧 𝙤𝙛 𝙩𝙝𝙚 𝙒𝙚𝙚𝙠➡️ 𝟯 𝙩𝙝𝙞𝙨 𝙨𝙚𝙖𝙨𝙤𝙣🦁 Two five-set EIVA wins at Sacred Heart🦁 1.80 blocks/set 🚫🦁 11 blocks in Friday's win#WeAre pic.twitter.com/t6LocHRKEr— Penn State Men's Volleyball (@PennStateMVBALL) April 13, 2026

This marks the middle blocker's seventh time earning the honor throughout his career, and the eighth weekly award for Penn State from the EIVA this season.

Rose had been a major piece to the success of the Nittany Lions this season. The redshirt senior had 11 blocks on Friday against Sacred Heart and averaged 1.80 blocks per set.

His defensive contributions made all the difference, helping push the blue and white to two five-set wins in the EIVA.

With just two regular-season matches left this year, and both being against the in-conference George Mason squad, the blue and white look to keep their undefeated 10-0 streak alive within the EIVA.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball bounces back with run-rule win against Bucknell
author: Brielle Gari I The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State opened its ninth week of the season with an in-state rivalry against the Bucknell Bison on Tuesday. The Nittany Lions hosted their Country Night and first Dollar Dog Night.

The blue and white bats went off in their Tuesday Night Lights as they defeated the Bison in five innings with a final score of 11-3.

In the circle, sophomore right-hander Abigail Britton started things off for Penn State.

The Bison started off strong in the top inning with junior Sarah Zimmerman getting a single to left field, but the blue and white defense was able to shut down their momentum. Britton was able to get a strikeout and only allowed one hit.

Bucknell gave Zimmerman the start on the mound. Freshman outfielder Breanna Hanik hit the batter’s box and was able to work a walk. Starting momentum for Penn State as Zimmerman hit junior Brooke Klosowicz, a single from freshman Gabby Gradishar got Hanik home.

The Nittany Lions were able to break the scoreboard first, leading 1-0 going into the second.

Britton was successful at the top of the second as she kept the scoreboard clean and got her second strikeout of the game. The Penn State defense did commit an error, but they were able to finish the inning.

Zimmerman surrendered a walk that gave sophomore outfielder Natalie Lieto a free base to kick off the bottom of the second. Lieto was successful in stealing second. Junior outfielder Frankee Flesher also walked.

Lieto looked to steal third, but the Bison defense was able to catch her to get the out. Flesher was able to steal second and third successfully. Senior catcher Madison Seidel was the third Nittany Lion to walk in the second.

Penn State went back to the top of the order, as Hanik stepped up, and on her fourth pitch at the plate, she launched the seventh home run of her rookie season. Freshman infielder Allison Oneacre got a double that was all for the blue and white as they increased their lead to close out the second inning, 4-0.

Junior right-hander Bridget Nemeth entered the circle for the blue and white.

Nemeth got her first strikeout of the game and was able to maintain a clean scoreboard in the third.

Gradishar got things going in the bottom of the third with a single to right field. Junior Michela Barbanete kept the hitting energy alive as she stepped into the batter's box and homered to right field.

The Bison pitching staff made a change in the circle, giving freshman lefty Nina Barrouk a chance to close the gap.

On Lieto’s second pitch at-bat, she hit a homer to center field, increasing the blue and white’s lead 7-0 in the bottom of the third with only one out.

Seidel got a hit down right field. The rest of the bottom of the third inning was filled with doubles, doubles, and even more doubles for Penn State.

Scoring six runs in the third, the Nittany Lions' bats were hot as they continued their momentum throughout the lineup.

Bucknell made their third change in the circle with senior righty Mea Cosentino, as she was able to close out the third after six runs were scored. Penn State led 10-0 going into the fourth.

Junior infielder Brooke Popella started a rally for the Bison as she was able to hit a single off a full count. Junior infielder Molly Lawson followed up with a single, giving Bucknell two runners in scoring position with no outs.

Nemeth responded with her second strikeout of the game, but surrendering a walk gave the Bison bases loaded.

Sophomore Reagan Powell kept the Bucknell offense alive as she hit a single to center, getting two runs on the board. The blue and white defense was able to close out the inning.

The Bison trailed heading into the fifth, 10-2.

Consentino was able to hold down the Penn State offense, only giving up a single hit.

In the top of the fifth, the Nittany Lion bullpen made a switch, adding freshman lefty Brevyn Kellepouris to the circle.

Senior outfielder Haley Speicher kept things alive for the Bison offense as she singled up the middle to kick off the fifth inning. Popella doubled to right field, giving Speicher the ability to get another run on the board.

Kellepouris gave up her first walk of the game, but that was all as the defense was able to close out the top of the fifth. Penn State led 10-3.

Coach Clarisa Crowell changed the lineup to start the bottom of the fifth. Sophomore catcher Macy Chamberlin, on her first pitch at-bat, hit a double to get things going for the blue and white.

Senior catcher Jordyn Jochims made an appearance at the plate, getting a single to right field.

Freshman infielder Kalli Karwowski was able to get a single as well, and with bases loaded, Gradishar hit a fly ball to center that allowed Chamberlin the winning score.

Penn State defeated the Bucknell Bison 11-3.

Up Next

The blue and white continue from the diamond against Ohio State in Columbus at 6 p.m Friday.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State women's hockey earns commitment from forward Sydney Stoughton
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Jeff Kampersal is loading up.

Penn State locked up a commitment from 2009-born forward Sydney Stoughton, she announced via Instagram on Tuesday.

Stoughton currently plays for Lovell Academy U16 and recorded 19 points in 18 games. She was originally committed to Minnesota before flipping to the Nittany Lions.

The Mason, Ohio, native earned a spot on the U.S. World Juniors Classic U18 squad in 2024-25, tallying two assists in six games with a +2 net rating.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's lacrosse defenseman Alex Ross selected by New York Atlas in 2026 PLL Draft
author: AJ Piazza | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State had a player selected in the PLL Draft.

Alex Ross was drafted No. 8 overall by the New York Atlas in the first round of the 2026 PLL Draft.

ALEX ROSS WELCOME TO THE BULLS 🤘We have selected Defenseman Alex Ross with the 8th Overall Pick in the 2026 @PremierLacrosse College Draft. pic.twitter.com/19HqZrXnie— New York Atlas (@PLLAtlas) April 14, 2026

Ross has been a starter since his freshman year and has played a big part in both of the Nittany Lions' Final Four runs. The senior tallied 106 ground balls so far in his career, as well as 72 caused turnovers throughout his time in the blue and white.

Jeff Tambroni has been a big fan of Ross since he stepped foot on campus and especially spoke highly of Ross during media.

“Alex has certainly grown throughout his years here," Tambroni said. "I would even say significantly throughout his senior year, and he does not get too emotionally high or low. I think that’s a big piece of why he has been so successful for so long."

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s hockey alumni make their mark in the pros
author: Maggie Hardy | The Daily Collegian
description:
After Penn State’s season ended in the Regional Semifinals of the NCAA Tournament, it didn’t take long for much of the team’s top talent to get signed to the big leagues.

Some Nittany Lions signed with their drafted teams, while others were picked up as free agents, getting their long-awaited opportunity to play hockey at the professional level.

One Nittany Lion that has already made a sizeable pro impact is junior forward Aiden Fink.

After putting together three standout seasons at Penn State — including a Top-10 Hobey Baker finish, setting a single-season program points record and being named a CCM/AHCA Second-Team All-American — Fink signed an amateur tryout contract with the Nashville Predators.

The forward was taken by the Predators in the seventh round back in the 2023 NHL Draft, and first reported to the team’s AHL affiliate, the Milwaukee Admirals, to begin his pro career.

Fink’s impact was felt immediately, and, in just his second game with the Admirals, registered his first professional point on an assist — and then added three more in the same night.

After leaving the score sheet empty in his debut, Fink began a six-game point streak, including registering his first professional multi-goal effort Saturday, that caught the eye of the big leagues.

The Predators announced Monday they were signing Fink to a three-year, entry-level contract beginning this season, opening the door for him to make his NHL debut in the near future.

Fellow junior forward Matt DiMarsico is another Nittany Lion to have a strong start to his professional career.

DiMarsico registered a career-high 42 points this season, which was good for second on the team, including a team-leading 18 goals.

After going undrafted, DiMarsico began the offseason as a free agent, and it didn’t take long before the Colorado Avalanche inked him to a two-year, entry-level deal.

DiMarsico’s contract terms required him to play out the remainder of the season with the Avalanche’s AHL Affiliate, the Colorado Eagles, before he gets the opportunity for a call-up next season.

The Wexford, Pennsylvania, native registered his first professional goal on April 4, adding a third-period score to seal the Eagles’ 4-2 win.

In just five games with Colorado, DiMarsico was quick to make an impact, adding two assists for three points in his short pro career so far.

Fink and DiMarsico were quick to get their names on the scoresheet; however, several other Nittany Lions have inked professional contracts but have yet to slot into their respective lineups.

Sophomore forward Charlie Cerrato was another to join the system of his drafted team, signing a professional tryout with the Carolina Hurricanes’ AHL affiliate, the Chicago Wolves.

Cerrato was a dominant offensive talent in his two seasons with Penn State.

After being passed up twice in the NHL Draft, a standout freshman season that saw him lead all Big Ten rookies in every offensive category had NHL teams paying attention, leading to the Hurricanes taking Cerrato No. 49 overall last summer.

The forward was forced to miss a large part of the blue and white’s second half due to injury, but made his return to the lineup in the team’s last regular-season matchup. However, in Penn State’s final game, Cerrato looked to have suffered yet another injury after exiting early in the second period.

Cerrato has yet to slot into the Wolves’ lineup; however, he was seen participating in practice on Tuesday via the team’s social media. After recently clinching a spot in the Calder Cup Playoffs, Cerrato’s professional debut in Chicago could be on the horizon.

Sophomore goaltender Kevin Reidler was the first Nittany Lion to land a pro contract this offseason, after the Ottawa Senators — the team who drafted him back in 2022 — signed him to a two-year, entry-level deal.

Reidler played just one season with the blue and white, transferring from Nebraska-Omaha last offseason, and posted a solid .901 save percentage in 18 games.

Reidler has been assigned to the AHL and is awaiting his pro debut, as his contract requires him to report to the Senators’ affiliate, the Belleville Senators, to play out the rest of this season.

After finishing their NCAA careers, seniors Jarod Crespo and Carter Schade were two more additions to the Nittany Lion group with pro contracts.

Crespo, a top blueliner and one of Penn State’s alternate captains this year, signed an Amateur Tryout Agreement with the San Jose Barracuda, the AHL affiliate of the San Jose Sharks.

Schade, another Nittany Lion defenseman, landed a Standard Player Contract with the Reading Royals, the ECHL affiliate of the Philadelphia Flyers and Lehigh Valley Phantoms, to round out the pro group.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: ‘You can’t take it personal’ | RBs coach Savon Huggins brings tough coaching style
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Savon Huggins is a very vocal presence on the practice field. He can be heard shouting directions, praises and constructive criticism to his running backs, but that’s just Huggins’ way of showing his love for his players.

Huggins is intentional with his coaching style — he holds his room to a high standard and isn’t afraid to tell them something they might not want to hear. That could’ve been difficult for the players to adjust to, yet all three have appreciated the toughness and are continuing to come closer together as a running back group.

“I just always think like you got to have that wisdom of, when he's pushing you, he's just trying to make us better, and you can't take it personal,” Carson Hansen said. “He's just trying to make us better in the best versions of ourselves. So I really appreciate him for doing it, pushing us every single day. Because I don't think he really truly understands how much we appreciate it, but it is really appreciated.”

As Matt Campbell worked to fill out his coaching staff, the running back position remained open the longest and was the last position coach hire he thought he had to make before wide receiver coach Noah Pauley’s unexpected departure.

Campbell landed on Huggins, who was the Boston College running backs coach the last four seasons. Huggins is a former running back as well, spending his career at Rutgers from 2011-14.

Huggins’ tough coaching style actually comes from his days as a player, as he said Tuesday that as a former player, he knows “you want the truth.”

“You want honesty. Because how can you grow if you don't know what your gaps are?” Huggins said. “You're gonna know, and everybody else in the running back room is gonna know what your gaps are. We're not gonna hide it. Cam (Wallace) is gonna know what Carson's gaps are, and Carson's gonna know what people's gaps are, and everybody's gonna know what their strengths are, too, as well.”

Huggins also approaches film sessions a bit differently. He wants his players to not only point out mistakes or good reps of their own, but to speak up when they notice a teammate slipping up or making a nice play.

After a bit of adjusting, Huggins said he notices that his players now “take ownership” more than what is required of them, something that’s “very encouraging” to Penn State’s new running backs coach.

James Peoples is one running back that Huggins felt like needed an adjustment period and some heart-to-hearts, but Peoples has welcomed the advice and coaching just as his fellow teammates have.

“Coach Huggins, he's on my butt, and I appreciate him for that,” James Peoples said. And I think, of course, there's areas in my game where I feel like I can improve and the areas I'm good at, you know, everybody has that, but he's really been honing in on me and being like, ‘we got to improve on everything.’”

The running backs have also grown closer as they continue to get to know one another. Hansen said the group is “like a family now,” and that includes Huggins, who truly understands what it feels like to be one of them.

“I think instantaneously, when I sat down with each and one of these young men, we have a level of connection, because I legitimately have been them,” Huggins said. “So I see myself in them, and I think that allows us to have some relatability when it comes to, you know, that part of it, but it's been great.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State notebook | Softball coach Clarisa Crowell talks weekend performance, dollar dogs, Ohio State
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State is looking to bounce back after being swept by Northwestern in convincing fashion last weekend. The Nittany Lions wrap up their seven-game home stretch with a Tuesday night matchup against Bucknell, before heading to Ohio State

Coach Clarisa Crowell met with the media on Tuesday to discuss a tough series with the Wildcats and what’s yet to come for her team this upcoming week.

Dollar Dog Night

After not playing on its home dirt until late March, Penn State has gotten to stay in Happy Valley for two weeks and play in front of the Nittany Lion faithful.

However, playing at home is not just beneficial for local fans but for the players as well.

“I do think, from a rest and recovery standpoint, it's great to play at home, and not having to travel because it does take a toll on you,” Crowell said. “Enjoy playing in front of our fans, the energy I feel like in the stadium has been great, the fan support has been great.”

With the baseball team out of town, softball is taking over dollar dog duties, so fans can instead find the deal at Beard Field on Tuesday night.

“For us, it’s any way we can gain some fan support, I know it's been huge for baseball in the past few years,” Crowell said. “So we thought, you know what, why don't we go ahead and bring that tradition over to softball and so we'll see tonight.”

Pitching and defense

Northwestern got the best of Penn State's pitching staff this past weekend, totaling 38 runs and recording double-digit hits in each game.

The Nittany Lions owned a 3.73 ERA entering their series against the Wildcats, but that number now stands at 4.04.

“I mean analytically, our stuff was actually good, we just didn't execute our plan,” Crowell said. “When you put the ball over the white, or the ball catches too much white, that is a good hitting team, that makes you pay, and that's exactly what happened.”

The blue and white were particularly overwhelmed in a 17-3 loss on Sunday. Northwestern scored 10 runs in the sixth inning, and Crowell was forced to use all five arms available in the bullpen.

In this scenario, pitching and defense coincide, and there were times when the Penn State defense behind the pitchers lacked as well.

“The defense needs to play better, too, behind the pitchers. There were some times where we didn't make plays, and the floodgates opened,” Crowell said. “It was just a crazy weekend, you know, but it happens, that's the highs and the lows of the game.”

Prepping for the Buckeyes

The Nittany Lions will look to bounce back in conference play when they take on Ohio State in Columbus.

The Buckeyes bring a strong arm to the circle with freshman right-hander Jenna Molk. The Dover, Ohio, native has pitched 128 innings this season and owns a 3.28 ERA.

“Jenna Molk is a hard-throwing drop ball pitcher. Drop balls for us, I think that is a good thing because I think our kids do a great job with balls down in the zone,” Crowell said. “This past weekend, Marina Mason was more up spin. Down is, I don't want to say it's easy to hit, but it's easier to hit, especially with the way our kids swing the bat.”

The scouting report also has the Nittany Lions facing an Ohio State team whose .322 batting average ranks sixth in the Big Ten.

“Ohio State's a very good offensive team, always have been, they are their power-hitting team,” Crowell said. “We're going to have to put all three parts of the game together, and we're going to have to pitch, play defense, and score runs.”

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State RB Carson Hansen in ‘best shape of his life’ ahead of 2026 season
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Running back Carson Hansen increased his production each year while at Iowa State, and he could be in store for another step forward in 2026 with Penn State’s coaching staff offering praise for the veteran.

Following the Nittany Lions’ 10th practice of spring ball, Matt Campbell said Hansen’s growth has been “really big” for the squad. Hansen’s in the middle of a battle for the starting running back role against Quinton Martin Jr. and James Peoples, and his conditioning is standing out.

“He just got better and better and better,” Campbell said on Tuesday. “To watch him kind of come here physically, I think he's in the best shape of his life, which is awesome to see. I think he's really done a great job.”

Hansen has been with Campbell since he was a freshman in 2023. Campbell said Hansen had a third-down role that year before earning a starting role the last two seasons.

The 6-foot-2, 220-pounder ran for a career-high 952 yards on 188 attempts this past season, and he racked up 13 rushing touchdowns in 2024. Campbell mentioned Hansen as another Nittany Lion who’s going into their fourth year playing critical reps.

Now, as he’s showcasing his physicality in spring ball during his first offseason with Penn State, something Hansen credited to running backs coach Savon Huggins.

“Coach Huggins has done a great job of pressing that in the running back room,” Hansen said. “We're going to be the most conditioned team out there. You got to be as a running back, the play never ends in practice. It's like the guys are tackling you in a third period. That's just how it is as a running back, so you just got to be conditioned to the best of your ability.”

With the starting running back battle up in the air, Hansen is the lone back who has previously held a starting role. Peoples and Martin have both shown flashes of potential, but haven’t produced like Hansen.

As Hansen battles for the starting gig, Huggins said he’s demanding that he increase his conditioning level. Huggins has also noticed other qualities Hansen has shown during the spring.

“When Carson says he wants to do something, he's going to take detailed notes about it, and he'll go out there,” Huggins said. “It's encouraging to see the steps that he's taken and his overall football IQ has enhanced from the time I've gotten here till now, so that's one of the unique things.”

Hansen said it’s important not to take Huggins’ coaching personally, as he aims to develop his position group into the best version of itself. Hansen said he appreciates the work his position coach has done in pushing him and the rest of the running back room/

It’s all part of the work Hansen has put in this spring ahead of his first season with Penn State. While it’ll remain to be seen whether he sees another jump in production, he’s excited for his upcoming year as a Nittany Lion.

“The White Out game, the USC game, that's obviously going to be amazing,” Hansen said. “I haven't experienced 100,000 (fans) plus yet, so I think that's going to be really special, and I can't wait for that.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Report: Former Penn State men's hockey defenseman Cade Christenson transfers to Providence
author: Greg Finberg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Another Penn Stater has found a new home.

Defenseman Cade Christenson, who entered the transfer portal on Monday, has committed to Providence, according to Mark Divver of NHL.com.

Penn State D Cade Christenson headed to Providence College via the portal— Mark Divver (@MarkDivver) April 14, 2026

Christenson, a 6-foot-3 D-man, tallied five goals and two assists during his sophomore campaign. He departs Hockey Valley as a +33 with 16 points across two seasons.
