MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State DC D’Anton Lynn pouring confidence into defense with coaching style, character
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
All good defenses have to talk — D’Anton Lynn has made that clear.

So the defensive coordinator constantly reinforces what, three weeks ago, safeties coach Deon Broomfield called "obnoxious communication” to every single member of his defensive unit.

“It doesn't matter what sport you play, there's not a lot of things that are perfect in football, but communication has to be,” Lynn said. “All it takes is one guy to assume this, and it could be an explosive play. So we put a big emphasis on making sure we always communicate.”

Lynn knows what it takes to play defense at Penn State, having suited up as a cornerback from 2008-11, and being named an All-Big Ten honorable mention three times.

Lynn has taken what he learned as a Nittany Lion previously over to being a coach as he transitions from coaching the USC defense for two years.

“He's very hands-on. He's teaching. He's loud. He's vocal,” linebacker Kooper Ebel. “And then when we get on the field, he's extremely loud and vocal too. He breathes that confidence into us, and it carries over.”

Whether it’s the practice field or in the team meeting rooms, as either the whole roster, the defense, or in position groups, Lynn has made it clear that being loud and being heard is required for his group.

“We're staying on the same page and obnoxiously letting each other know. You can't be perfect on anything on the football field, but you can be perfect in your communication, your alignment, and then knowing your assignment,” linebacker Cael Brezina said. "So the first thing that comes with that is the communication. If everybody's on the same page, really good things are going to be able to happen.”

In order for the communication to operate like a well-oiled machine, Lynn has stressed that all defensive players have to know everyone’s role in the scheme.

If one unit isn’t doing its job, then the likelihood is the defense won’t succeed.

“It starts with trust, knowing that the guy next to you, the safety, whatever it may be, (defensive) line, they know what they're doing,” linebacker Caleb Bacon said. “Just trust in each other to make the right call, or make the right adjustments, and then making those (calls) loud and obnoxiously so everybody on the field can hear.”

Penn State is coming off a season where the defense didn’t live up to expectations under defensive coordinator Jim Knowles, who has since left for Tennessee. Knowles’ defense notably takes a few seasons to get acclimated to, and that was evident in the unit’s performance.

Lynn said the results have been positive so far with getting used to his playbook, but there’s still work to be done, which includes the communication aspect of his defense.

“I want them to really have a strong foundation, not just know what they're doing, but understand why,” Lynn said. “It helps this time of the year when you can really move slow, because we still have all June. We still have all camp."

Communication has been a key with Lynn, but that’s not the only aspect that has stood out about the Celina, Texas, native.

He’s been a leader of men, and has all the players and coaches bought in to what he’s about as a defensive coordinator and person.

“He's really, really humble,” running backs coach Savon Huggins said. “Unbelievable, high character, great leader and the young men respect him. You can see that in how they play.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State notebook | DC D’Anton Lynn talks slow installation, obnoxious communication
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
D’Anton Lynn has a track record to uphold, having previously played at Penn State and coached at the professional and collegiate level, most recently at USC.

For the first time this spring, Lynn met with the media to discuss the current state of the defense.

Lynn detailed his philosophy on installing and executing the defensive system, including the rotation of the green dot and the defense’s “obnoxious communication.”

Here’s what Penn State’s new defensive coordinator had to say.

Slow installation

As with any new coordinator, there’s a different system to learn for Penn State’s defensive players. To ensure that the players fully understand the concepts, and the meaning behind them, Lynn has taken measures to slow the pace.

"We're still in the process. We’ve tried to install slowly with the guys, equal amount of reps and different concepts, just to see. But the one thing you see, the guys fly around, the guys communicate, and the guys know a lot of ball,” Lynn said. “I want them to really have a strong foundation, not just know what they're doing, but understand why. But it helps this time of the year, when you can really move slow. Because we still have all June. We still have all camp."

Despite that, Lynn noted that many of the returning Penn State players are used to playing under multiple defensive coordinators in a short time-frame — Lynn is the Nittany Lions’ fourth defensive coordinator in five years.

"The one benefit that we have is we have guys who’ve played for a lot of different coordinators,” Lynn said. “There's some kids from Penn State; I'm their fourth coordinator. So they know a lot of ball, which is going to help speed things up."

Lynn didn’t name any specific standout players so far this spring, however, as he’s seen a mix of successes from the defense as a whole.

Green dot rotation

With Amare Campbell departing Penn State to Tennessee via the transfer portal this offseason, the green dot position is up in the air for the 2026 season.

Lynn is currently experimenting with who’s going to wear the green dot, having it rotate at practice between the linebackers before moving on to defensive backs.

“Short-term memory,” Lynn said. “There's a lot of ebbs and flows in the game and when that plays over, you need to be able to put that play to bed."

At USC last season, Lynn originally had linebackers and safeties wear the green dot, with the primary option being safety Kamari Ramsey. According to Lynn, it was similarly up in the air between the linebackers and defensive backs last spring as well.

“It really just comes down to what individual handles the green dot the best,” Lynn said.

Obnoxious communication

A Lynn original, the Penn State defenders are expected to utilize over-the-top, “obnoxious” communication to ensure that everyone on the team knows exactly what the plan is for every snap. This includes vocalizing and terminology, hand signals, sound cues and more to deliver a precise message on the field.

"We just put a big emphasis on just making sure we always communicate," Lynn said. "It's gotten better and better every day, but it's not where we want it to be yet. All it takes is one guy to assume this, and it could be an explosive play.”

And it’s not always about the defensive formation. It’s the location of every major offensive player on the field, including tight ends, confirming the context of the down, how much time is left on the clock and predictions on who the quarterback is targeting.

However, Lynn’s made it a point to preach the team like a hive mind, where every defender knows all of the available information.

"I just feel like all good defenses have to talk," Lynn said.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State notebook | Matt Campbell talks defensive line, Rocco Becht timeline
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Spring practice continued rolling along for Penn State with just five practices left until the Blue-White Practice.

Matt Campbell took to the podium in Lasch Building on Tuesday to talk about an injury update with Rocco Becht, the outlook on the defensive line and the team’s camaraderie.

Here’s what he had to say.

Rocco Becht update

Rocco Becht entered Penn State recovering from injuries to both of his shoulders. As the practices move along, Becht has been making advances, allowing him to participate more during drills and 7-on-7.

At the start of spring practices, transfer quarterback Connor Barry was the leader of the pack due to injuries from Becht and Alex Manske. However, Becht is working ahead of the initial schedule.

“He'll be ready to rock and roll,” Campbell said. “I think the fact that we've gotten this much out of him was way ahead of where we thought we would have been. So I think that's really big for him.”

While Becht has been rehabbing his injury, he’s worked with his skill position players to get them up to speed with the offense. Taylor Mouser was with Becht at Iowa State, and Becht’s leadership has been key to growing that connection with the wide receiver unit.

“Every day you pass here at four o’clock and he’s got the entire receiving core, tight end core, meeting with those guys and going over practice,” Campbell said.

Defensive line

A major emphasis in the transfer portal and retention of Penn State players was with the defensive line. Campbell added a balance of players who’ve seen a lot of reps, while also bringing back players who’ve shown flashes of potential.

The Nittany Lions brought in Alex McPherson from Colorado, who played in 10 games in his freshman campaign with the Buffalos, while two returners showed flashes in their respective first seasons in Happy Valley.

“I think it was that fine balance of seeing there was some really talented young players here,” Campbell said. “I think when you watch and see what Yvan (Kemajou) did, obviously, you had to go back a year and watch what Max Granville did, but you saw Max working in the weight room.”

While those players flashed, Iowa State transfer Ikenna Ezeogu has “maybe flashed every single day,” and true freshman Jackson Ford has continued “to grow and probably flash a bit sooner than maybe we would even known as a freshman.”

While the outside of the trenches has shown flashes, the inside has continued to prove that their growth and size could be a major factor in the Big Ten. The position group added from all over the country to grow the size of the position compared to what Penn State has had in recent years.

“So the physicality of the group,” Campbell said. “You better be right on every snap, or they're going to make you pay. And I think it's been really fun to watch that group just grow forward, and we'll need them to continue to grow forward.”

Camaraderie of team

One of the biggest obstacles for the 2026 team was meshing the team together. Penn State brought in 38 transfers and returned a slew of impact players from last year’s squad, meaning it’s taken some time for everyone to get to know each other.

“I think you’re building that every single day in the program, and you try to create that as much as you possibly can,” Campbell said.

No matter how much preparation is done in the offseason, it’s not necessarily guaranteed that the closeness of the team will show up on the field, as Campbell emphasized the squad’s first test of adversity will reveal how close the players have gotten.

“You’re going to have a bad quarter, you’re going to have bad moments and we’re going to find out then how close we have come together,” Campbell said. “We want to be tough. We certainly need to be disciplined, but the biggest thing we have to be is together, and if you’re together, you got a real shot.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Report: Penn State men’s basketball lands commitment from Miami (OH) transfer Brant Byers
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State is set to bring in a productive wing from the portal.

Miami (OH) transfer Brant Byers has committed to the Nittany Lions, per Johnathan Givony of Draft Express.

NEWS: Miami Ohio transfer Brant Byers has committed to Penn State, agent German Srulovich of @WEAVE tells DraftExpress.The Nittany Lions land a 6'8 forward and high-level shooter who hit 39% from 3 for a Miami Ohio team that went 32-2 and earned an at-large NCAA Tournament bid pic.twitter.com/bB9pgGInsO— Jonathan Givony (@DraftExpress) April 14, 2026

The 6-foot-8, 200-pounder was with the RedHawks for the last three seasons. After redshirting as a freshman in 2023-24, he’s garnered All-MAC recognition the past two years.

This past year, Byers was a starter for a Miami team that went undefeated during the regular season, averaging 14.2 points and 4.1 rebounds per game across 34 appearances. He shot 47.8% from the field and 39.2% from 3-point range, helping him earn second-team All-MAC honors.

As a redshirt freshman, Byers won the conference’s Freshman of the Year award in 2024-25. He averaged 8.4 points and 2.8 rebounds per game across 34 appearances, including one start.

The Chambersburg, Pennsylvania, native now returns to his home state with two years of eligibility remaining. As Penn State’s roster stands now, he’s a strong candidate to start at the three for the Nittany Lions next season.

Byers is Penn State’s second transfer portal addition this offseason, joining Central Connecticut State guard Jay Rodgers, who committed on Sunday. International prospects Roko Prkačin and François Wibaut are also set to become Nittany Lions next season along with incoming freshman Jamison White.

title: Senior Column | I did it my way
author: Julia Kushner | The Daily Collegian
description:
This semester has been one of reflection. An end is near (cue Sinatra’s “My Way”) and so is a new beginning.

And though I’ve been thinking of myself and my own past, present and future, I’ve also been thinking about a legacy to be left behind for those coming after me.

I, in all honesty, began working for the Collegian because I disagreed with it. I loved the idea of it, but I felt the execution was politically one-sided.

If no one else will cover conservatism accurately and no one else will voice a Republican opinion, I told myself, then I will. And what a gift that has proven to be.

It’s been tough. Sometimes I feel like I’m in the lion’s den. In many areas of my collegiate career, I’ve been the minority, oppositional view, and this comes with obvious challenges: rewrites, not being able to say what I want, how I want to and even loneliness.

But it has also come with its benefits: a strengthening of my own beliefs, an improvement in my writing and bridging divides, my future career, even though I’m getting an advertising degree, and the one thing I always wanted — for students on the opposite side of the aisle to simply read a differing opinion, even if it never changes their own.

There’s nothing sweeter than a peer coming to me and telling me they’ve read one of my pieces. This is especially true when I know of their left-leaning political persuasion, yet they’re still able and willing to engage with my work.

I don’t mean for this to be a self-promoting fluff piece. I mean for this to encourage those on campus who are of the most important (in my view) kind of diverse — intellectually.

Though college campuses in general and this one in particular often share very similar viewpoints, students aren’t a monolith.

I can guarantee that someone else feels the same way you do, and you shouldn’t be afraid to voice your opinion. Even, and especially, if it’s variable from the mean.

And though I often disagree with my own outlet’s coverage, the Collegian is a gift that keeps on giving.

In fact, my upcoming job will be at a news outlet in our nation’s capital, and I only got this exciting employment opportunity because of another outlet I’ve been working for, The College Fix, and I only got that job because my editor read The Daily Collegian.

What Divine providence!

And speaking of, though I could talk endlessly about writing, politics and newspapers, the greatest aspect of my collegiate career has been my finding G-d, my finding purpose and meaning.

Yes, I’m turning a newspaper reflection into a piece about G-d. Sue me!

I can’t, in good faith (pun unintended, but funny nonetheless), leave Penn State without sharing the most important thing in my life and the lives of many of my contemporaries in the one place where I have a voice.

I’ve done much soul searching with the help of many a rabbinical book and teacher, and I’ve come to the conclusion that my entire reason for writing is to share the truth. Not just the political truth, but the truth. The truth of G-d. I aim to infuse this underlying message into everything I write, successfully or not.

So many young people are desperate for meaning, for something bigger than themselves and their own little worlds. I know I was.

I may not be as influential or as memorable as the Willard Preacher, but I hope my couple hundred words can plant the seed.

I began college lost. I was confused about my place in the world, about why things happened to me in the past and what was in store for me in the future.

When I found Jewish philosophy, my life changed, and no, I’m not being hyperbolic.

I really mean that my entire life changed. My outlook on life changed and so did my actions.

I now begin each new day with gratitude for my Maker, for trusting me enough to return my soul to my body. I read more books than I did before, and my first activity each day is to read Torah (i.e. Old Testament) topics. I set myself up for a day of positivity and mitzvos (essentially, commanded good deeds).

I have drive; I have passion for life; I have meaning. I know why I’m here. G-d created me on purpose and He did so in order for me to make this world a place for Him through mitzvos.

I know that I am here at Penn State to share the truth, and that my summer job will allow me to do the same. And I know that I’m ultimately here to build a Jewish home and to be the wife of a G-d-fearing man and the mother of his Divinely ordained Jewish children.

I know who I am and why I’m here. I know G-d (as well as anyone can). I know His desires for me. I am fulfilled because of this. I’m happy because of this. I’m gracious because of this.

I simply am because of this. And you can be too.

It’s never too late or too early for a relationship with the One G-d. He’s waiting for you to reach out your hand and grasp ahold of His outstretched arm and come home to Him and His ideals.

I thank G-d for my time at Penn State (and at the Collegian), and I hope you will, too.

title: Celebrate Campus Pride Month with these 5 film recommendations
author: Alli Satterfield | The Daily Collegian
description:
April is Penn State’s Campus Pride Month and for many, with public celebration comes private reflection. This month, whether you need time to laugh, cry or think critically, there are endless LGBTQ+ centered movies to enjoy while you do so.

Here are some recommendations:

1. “Call Me By Your Name”

Based on the novel by Andre Aciman, this movie stars Timothée Chalamet and Armie Hammer. With 7.8/10 stars on IMDb, the film focuses on the love story of two young men as they discover their emotions and desire over the course of a summer together.

Reviewers said the film portrays their love innocently, without the concepts of guilt and shame typically woven into a queer love story, and they claim Chalamet’s performance is emotional and raw. While it doesn’t have a happy ending, the film is beautiful, emotional and ultimately human. “Call Me By Your Name” illustrates a truly real love story, and can be a compelling watch for anyone.

2. “Cabaret”

The classic musical “Cabaret” has multiple film adaptations, but my favorite is the 1993 TV movie featuring Alan Cumming. In this version, Cumming takes on the role of the Emcee, who creepily and beautifully hosts the audience of the Kit Kat Club, a place where everyone is welcome and celebrated in Berlin, despite the incoming Nazi regime.

The theme of resistance feels especially timely now, as communities are coming together in the face of widespread rollbacks of DEI initiatives and other challenges to inclusivity. The musical is also a reminder to not turn a blind eye to the suffering of others, even if their challenges don’t impact your own life. Life isn’t always a cabaret.

3. “Boy Erased”

Continuing with the rather melancholy theme, this film is about a young man sent to conversion therapy. The protagonist is outed to his parents and his father, a Baptist pastor, forces him to either attend a conversion camp or be ousted from his family. The film, which is based on a true story, shows a realistic tale of human connection and emotion.

Critics of the movie warn that some scenes can be brutal and violent, while also not portraying the protagonist’s pain deeply enough. Others argue this was done intentionally to show an authentic story instead of just forcing audiences to cry. Some critics also claim that the script makes viewers too sympathetic to parents wanting to change their child.

But, despite the varied opinions, the film was given an 80% by Rotten Tomatoes, so it’s probably worth the watch. You can decide for yourself.

4. “Bohemian Rhapsody”

I think this film is great. I understand the argument that we’ve seen enough biopics, but I would argue this one is worth watching. It chronicles the career of Queen frontman Freddie Mercury, who is considered by some in LGBTQ+ community as a queer icon. He openly dated men and women and pushed the boundaries of gender expression, given the time period he lived in. Some people attribute his ability to do so to his status as a rock star, and the film explores this, as well as his medical struggles toward the end of his life.

“Bohemian Rhapsody,” which takes its name from the notorious Queen song, ends with the Live Aid concert, and blends the movie’s fictional footage with firsthand videos of Queen at the event. I cried the first time I watched the film because it seemed to immortalize Freddie Mercury’s influence and show that, even though he has become an icon, his influence was and is so much larger than his sexuality.

5. “But I’m a Cheerleader”

And, now, for a happier film. “But I’m a Cheerleader” is, at this point, a cult classic — fans of the movie seem to absolutely love it. I could not count on my hands the number of times I was told to watch this movie before I finally did. It’s been recommended to me dozens of times, but I finally watched it last week.

It was so worth it.

I found the film to be complex, campy and funny, and I especially enjoyed the ending. If you want a lighter movie, this is the one for you.

Happy watching and, more importantly, Happy Pride.

MORE BLOGS

title: Derby: A Women’s Comedy Troupe shares laughs, memories
author: Lily Dorf | The Daily Collegian
description:
With a variety of clubs offered at Penn State, it’s hard to keep track of them all. One comedy troupe on campus, Derby, is made for women and nonbinary individuals to show off their comedic skills with unique skits, stand-up sets and more.

President Juliana Gray said her first experience with comedy was with Derby and at first, she wasn’t interested in joining the club.

“I actually found out through a close friend of mine who was in the club at the time,” Gray, a fourth-year majoring in psychology, said. “We got to talking and she said ‘You're so funny, you should join this comedy club,’ and I said ‘There's no way I'm gonna do that.’ Then, fast forward three years, all of my best friends are a part of Derby and I'm the president.”

She said a typical week-in-the-life of a Derby member starts on Mondays at 6:30 p.m. in the Borland building, where members get together to workshop writing skills, comedy sketches, stand-up skits and more. However, for show weeks, it's a little more intense.

“We usually have show week meetings, which is every day of the week leading up to the show,” Gray said. “That's just practicing all of our decided upon different sketches, stand-up, whatever we're deciding to do for that show.”

Gray described the group’s performances as “Saturday Night Live style comedy with stand-up included.” Each show has new material, with alumni shows recycling past sketches, games and more.

In Derby, everyone has a role and for members like Jenna Vincenti, they can have multiple to play at once. As event planner and informal script writer she puts her all into Derby, and said comedy has always had a special place in her heart.

“Comedy has always been a big part of my personal life,” Vincenti, a fourth-year studying English, said. “I've always loved making jokes with family and just kind of being the class clown. It's always been something I've liked doing.”

For Vincenti, being in Derby has helped her strengthen her comedy skills and writing skills.

“I'm also a creative writing minor, so I really like having this low-stakes creative freedom where I can just write a sketch with my friends and then we put it on, we all have a good time and we stage it,” Vincenti said.

In the club, members get the chance to perform and collaborate with other clubs and even some classes.

“We had collaborated with a telecom class, and it was their final project to film one of our shows and edit it,” Vincenti said. “They then filmed a professionally done sketch with us … It was so fun, and when you look back on it, it's incredible to see how well done it was. Being able to do things that maybe we wouldn't normally get to do by having access to that kind of technology … It just kind of lets us express ourselves out of the confines of what we had normally done.”

Annie Neary said she knew before she came to campus that she wanted to be a part of Derby.

“I was looking through the Discover page of clubs I potentially want to get involved in, and I saw Derby on the Discover page and I immediately knew, that's the club I want to be in,” Neary, a first-year studying elementary and early childhood education and special education, said. “I made a beeline at the involvement fair to join the GroupMe and sign up and everything.”

She said there’s more to Derby than just the comedy, and the members all have love and acceptance for each other.

“Not only is it an improv troupe, but it’s an improv troupe for women and nonbinary people,” Neary said. “It really gives a chance to uplift those voices, which are very often overshadowed and undervalued in the context of comedy. So, getting to share a space with people who have similar experiences and get to create comedy as a result of those experiences, I think is really special.”

MORE LIFESTYLE COVERAGE

title: Potential transfer targets to rebuild Penn State women’s basketball
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After Tanisha Wright was hired as the next coach for Penn State on March 19, the program experienced a large exodus with 10 players hitting the transfer portal.

While three of those players have committed to new schools, the Lady Lions have yet to earn any transfers of their own, leaving the team with no players on next year’s roster.

Here’s a look at some potential targets for the blue and white to start rebuilding its squad in Year 1 under Wright.

Victoria Rivera, Georgetown

Outside of Wright, the only other addition to the Lady Lions has been Penn State’s all-time leading scorer Kelly Mazzante as an assistant coach, who’s spent the last three years at Georgetown.

Two weeks before she was hired, the Hoyas’ leading scorer from last year, Victoria Rivera, hit the transfer portal.

With Mazzante now in Happy Valley, the Lady Lions could feel right at home for Rivera. Penn State needs some form of leadership and experience on its squad, and Rivera’s spent the last four years with Georgetown, including three years as a starter.

While she only played in nine games before sustaining a season-ending injury, Rivera averaged 9.4 points and 2.2 rebounds per game. In her last full season of action, she started 27 games the year before; the Houston native averaged 10.5 points, 3.2 rebounds and 1.7 assists per game.

Kaylene Smikle, Maryland

While former Maryland guard Kaylene Smikle likely wouldn’t experience an NCAA Tournament in her graduate year like she saw this year, she’d have a new start at Penn State following a season-ending injury.

Smikle went down with an injury on Dec. 3 after averaging 13.1 points to start the season.

The Bay Shore, New York, native has proven to be an elite scorer at every level, recording 42 3-pointers during her healthy junior season. That year, she averaged 17.9 points, 4.4 rebounds and 1.5 steals per game while starting all 33 contests.

If Smikle wants to stay in the Big Ten and be the premier player at a program — stepping out of the shadow of Oluchi Okananwa from last year — Happy Valley could be the best place to wrap up her collegiate career.

Fatima Diakhate, Pitt

After spending her junior season at Pitt, Fatima Diakhate is looking for a new home, possibly staying in the Keystone State.

If that’s the case, Diakhate could look to Penn State as her next school, helping kickstart the Wright era in Happy Valley with one year of collegiate eligibility remaining.

With the Panthers, the Dakar, Senegal, native averaged 9.4 points and 8.9 rebounds per game, and could be a solid replacement at center, standing at 6-foot-5.

Diakhate spent two years at Georgia before Pitt, and could follow the path of former Lady Lion Amiya Evans, who came from the Bulldogs to Happy Valley last season.

Taylor Feldman, Purdue

Another potential intra-conference transfer the Lady Lions could take advantage of is Taylor Feldman, who took a big jump from Northern Arizona to Purdue last season.

While the Tustin, California, native is another athlete who fell to injury during the 2025-26 season, she put on a solid showing through seven games, averaging 6.3 points, 2.4 rebounds and two assists per game.

With the Lumberjacks, she started 43 games in three seasons and averaged 16.4 points per game her junior season. Following an injury, Feldman could look to restart her Big Ten career with the Lady Lions next season.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: UHS, CSGD help students feel ‘seen, respected’ through gender-affirming care services
author: Vasco Chen I The Daily Collegian
description:
Amid shifting national discourse surrounding gender-affirming care across the United States, Penn State continues to offer services such as gender-affirming surgery, hormone replacement therapy, voice care training to all students and employees through Counseling and Psychological Services (CAPS) and University Health Services (UHS).

UHS has a gender diverse care team that specializes in providing gender-affirming care to students. The team consists of gender diverse coordinators from the Center of Sexual and Gender Diversity (CSGD), physicians from UHS, counselors from CAPS and nurses and student health insurance navigators from community health partners.

Tanja Hernandez said even if a student is already consulting with different medical providers, they’re free to talk through best practices with the team and transfer services if necessary.

“We do our best to make sure that students who go through any kind of gender-affirming care, whether that’s medical or mental health, are given an opportunity to speak with someone who’s specialized in this kind of care,” Hernandez, the gender diverse community and belonging coordinator at CSGD, said.

Hernandez said students with any questions about their insurances’ coverage of gender-affirming care should reach out to a health insurance navigator, who will help them review their benefits available through their insurance. Even if they’re not on the Student Health Insurance Plan (SHIP), they should still begin with the gender diverse care team.

“Because we operate under the Pennsylvania healthcare structure, even Medicaid and state insurance cover all of the cost for gender-affirming care,” Hernandez said. “For those who don’t have that co-pay provided, I work with students on filling out applications for grants and microgrants out of Eastern PA Trans Alliance and Point of Pride.”

Penn State is connected with other clinics across the nation who provide gender-affirming care through the Collegiate Gender Affirming Care Coalition, a network of healthcare providers dedicated to improving access to care for gender diverse students.

“We invite them to our campus every year, and we foster a connection with them so that we have a better understanding of their workload,” Hernandez said. “So, if a student is seeking a specific surgery and we know that a local provider is on a backlog of more than eight months, they might consider referring out to somewhere in Philadelphia or Harrisburg, somewhere that’s not quite as local but still within our network.”

Carla Prieto, the coordinator of sexual and gender diverse care at CAPS, said those partnerships are essential to ensuring the gender-affirming care services they offer are helpful to students.

“Having gender-affirming care resources on campus is fundamentally about student well-being, safety, and the ability to fully participate in university life, including reaching the fullest academic potential,” Prieto said

Although UHS only accepts student patients, the university health insurance for faculty and staff also includes gender-affirming care. Penn State also provides free voice training for students who are interested in changing the register, tone or pitch of their voice to align with how they wish to express themselves.

“When students feel seen, respected and supported in their identities, it directly impacts their mental health, academic success and sense of belonging,” Prieto said. “People tend to learn best when they don’t have to spend energy questioning whether they belong here.”

Gwynne Morris currently serves as a mentor for Queer Peers, which is a welcoming program that supports LGBTQ+ students as they navigate campus life and search for community.

“When I was a mentee last year, I would talk to (my mentor) about some emotional things, and they were obviously super cool and supportive,” Morris, a second-year studying political science, said. “And now, as a mentor, I'm also like that with the people who I've mentored.”

She said having these programs is really important because it protects diversity and makes Penn State more inclusive..

“The biggest thing that it has helped me with is referring me to the Student Legal Services,” Morris said. “I didn't pay anything to get my name changed in any way. They covered all of those costs completely, which was really, really amazing.”

In April 2025, Penn State Health stopped offering gender-affirming care for individuals under the age of 19 in compliance with federal law. Though services at Penn State continue, Prieto said it's hard to say if that will always be the case under the current administration.

“Supporting the resources above as an institution fosters a campus climate that is more inclusive, more connected, and more responsive to student needs,” Prieto said. “Supporting gender diverse students means supporting the whole student, and in doing so, strengthening the entire campus community.”

MORE CAMPUS CONTENT

title: Weekly Recap | Penn State softball suffers a series sweep to Northwestern
author: Brielle Gari I The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State was unsuccessful this past weekend as it hosted the Northwestern Wildcats for a Big Ten series weekend.

Losing all three games over the weekend, the blue and white haven’t faced this amount of tough defeats since its opening conference series against then-No. 18 Oregon. The Wildcats outscored the Nittany Lions 38-9.

After concluding its fifth consecutive Big Ten weekend series, the blue and white still sit in the middle of the conference standings with a 7-8 record against its opponents.

Bats go quiet

The biggest struggle the blue and white faced was the lack of production at the plate.

Over the course of the weekend, Penn State only got a total of nine hits. Sophomore Natalie Lieto was one of the most successful Nittany Lions, with a solo home run in the series opener.

Freshman Breanna Hanik had some success in the series finale, going 2-for-3.

Northwestern’s pitching staff gave up 20 walks throughout the weekend series, which was the most productivity in the batter's box for the blue and white’s offense, as its bats were quiet.

With no major hits from the lineup, coach Clarisa Crowell made a handful of changes during the weekend. Unfortunately, even with hopes to see some action, changes throughout the order didn’t help Penn State’s silence at the plate.

Pressure cracks through defense

In the first two games of the series, Northwestern tallied the scoreboard first. Penn State's defense was unable to come back after the momentum from the Wildcats’ lineup.

Five major errors across three games caused setbacks from the defense, which the blue and white never recovered from.

Long innings resulted not only from errors, but also from productive at-bats by Northwestern. In the series finale, it was able to get ten runs scored in the top of the sixth to solidify the series sweep.

Over the weekend, the Nittany Lions' bullpen was used heavily, as every pitcher on the roster made an appearance in the series.

Left-handed pitcher Brevyn Kellepouris was the first reliever of the weekend in the series opener. She had a total of 66 pitches across 4 1/3 innings, getting two strikes while allowing three runs and five hits.

Junior right-hander Bridget Nemeth got the start for Saturday's game and the losing decision, allowing ten hits and nine runs. Freshman right-handed pitcher Mackenzie Duncan closed things out, but recorded her worst performance so far of the season.

Starting Sunday’s series finale, sophomore right-hander Abigail Britton looked strong. She had the best performance in the circle over the weekend with four strikeouts.

Northwestern had a surge at the top of the fourth, scoring three runs. It went downhill from there as the Wildcats scored 10 runs in the sixth.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State football draft profile | WR Devonte Ross boasts offensive, special team experience
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After three seasons at Troy, wide receiver Devonte Ross transferred to Penn State to hopefully make the most of his final year at a Power Four school.

While Ross’ senior season was underwhelming compared to his junior year with the Trojans, he positioned himself as a starting wide receiver for the Nittany Lions.

With just 36 receptions for 501 yards and five touchdowns last season, the Cartersville, Georgia, native saw a major decline in his stats, but valued the opportunity to compete at a bigger program.

“It’s a blessing being in this position,” Ross said at Pro Day. “Penn State was able to help me showcase my ability to the rest of the world.”

Despite the underperforming season, Ross had several big moments throughout the year, including catching the game-tying touchdown against Oregon and eight receptions for 84 yards against Clemson in the Pinstripe Bowl.

“I’m not sure exactly how (NFL teams) view me,” Ross said. “I know some teams definitely like players who are versatile. I feel like that’s something that I can add to a team.”

Metrics

Height: 5-foot-9

Weight: 162 pounds

Vertical: 36 inches

Broad jump: 10-foot-8

40-yard dash: 4.45 seconds

Pro shuttle: 4.36 seconds

3-cone drill: 7.21 seconds

Projections

Projected round drafted: Undrafted

Player comparison: Brandon Powell

Scouting report

While undersized compared to the average NFL wide receiver, Ross’ best trait is his speed, and he showed that this season with a 13.9 yards per catch average.

Ross also served as a versatile option for Penn State and could be the same for NFL squads, as he led the team with 71 yards on punt returns, including a team-best 34-yard return.

It’s common for special teams to be the easiest way for rookies to see a big league field, and Ross provides plenty of experience in that realm having returned punts for both the Nittany Lions and Troy.

Ross was a second-team All-Sun Belt selection in 2024 as a return specialist after averaging 17 yards per return. He also earned first-team All-Sun Belt honors as a receiver.

With the Nittany Lions, Ross drew the nation’s eyes against Oregon, when he caught four receptions for 48 yards and two touchdowns, including the game-tying touchdown to bring the game to overtime.

Following his slower senior year campaign, Ross is ranked as the No. 370 prospect by PFF. In 2024 at Troy, Ross tallied 1,043 receiving yards, good for 19th nationally. He also had an elite performance against Iowa, recording 142 yards and three total touchdowns.

While Ross wasn’t invited to the NFL Scouting Combine, his broad jump from Penn State’s pro day would’ve ranked ninth among wide receivers at the combine, while his vertical jump would’ve ranked 17th.

Best team fit: Washington Commanders

The Washington Commanders are coming off an underwhelming season following a 2024 NFC Championship appearance, winning just five games as star quarterback Jayden Daniels was in and out with injuries.

A big area of struggle for the Commanders was their wide receiver room, missing two of their top-five targets during portions of the season. With top option Deebo Samuel now gone to free agency, Dan Quinn and company are looking for a new target for Daniels.

If the Commanders pick up Ross, he could be the third former Penn State receiver to join Washington in recent years, as Jahan Dotson and Mitchell Tinsley both spent time there in recent years.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Recruiting roundup | Peter Bourque visit, Midwest commitments highlight Penn State recruiting action
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
After a slow start on the recruiting trail, Penn State gained some steam by picking up a handful of commitments in the 2027 class, and that trend continued this week.

Here’s what happened in Week 3 of spring ball recruiting.

Peter Bourque visit

Penn State had a top 2027 quarterback target visit Wednesday in 4-star Peter Bourque, who ranks as the No. 76 player nationally, No. 6 quarterback and No. 1 player in Massachusetts.

The Marion native, who plays for Tabor Academy, was a top target under the old staff but ended up committing to Michigan. He reopened his recruitment in February, backing off his pledge following the firing of head coach Sherrone Moore and hiring of Kyle Whittingham from Utah.

Despite it being a new staff, Bourque remains a priority target for the Nittany Lions. Campbell, offensive coordinator Taylor Mouser, quarterbacks coach Jake Waters and running backs coach Savon Huggins visited him in January.

Bourque, along with 4-star Will Mencl, who visited last week, are the top targets on the quarterback board. Bourque has an upcoming visit to Virginia Tech planned, reuniting him with James Franklin and ex-quarterbacks coach Danny O’Brien. A reported offer from Notre Dame could add the program to the mix as well. Georgia is another school to monitor.

Penn State has to fight Oregon and Auburn for Mencl. Mencl has an upcoming visit to Oregon scheduled and has already seen Auburn. Penn State likely would’ve been the favorite if 4-star Jake Nawrot committed to the Ducks, but he instead pledged to Kentucky.

Both Bourque and Mencl also had interest from Miami, but the Hurricanes recently earned a commitment from 4-star Israel Abrams, which likely skews Bourque and Mencl away from ending up in Coral Gables.

Midwest recruiting spree

Penn State landed a trio of 2027 recruits this past week, all of whom hail from the Midwest: 4-star tight end Cooper Terwilliger, 4-star wide receiver Landon Blum and 3-star linebacker Blake Betton. Tewilliger is from South Dakota, Blum is from Iowa and Betton is from Minnesota.

All three were former targets by Matt Campbell and the rest of the Iowa State staff that followed Campbell to Penn State. It’s another example of players trusting prior relationships, committing to a school with limited visits and not being from the nearby area.

As Campbell and company adjust to a new recruiting footprint, they’ve made it clear they’re going back to familiar territory. This will likely be a trend in the early stages as they work to build relationships in Pennsylvania, the DMV and the Mid-Atlantic.

Despite wanting to recruit the six-to-eight-hour radius that Campbell highlighted at his introductory presser, the expanded recruiting terrain will more than likely be a factor, as there’s a level of comfort in recruiting the Midwest.

Through both the 2026 and 2027 recruiting classes, Penn State has landed pledges from 13 different states — South Dakota, Iowa, Minnesota, Colorado, Texas, Florida, Virginia, Ohio, Illinois and Georgia. Four commits have come from Nebraska and New Jersey, while two have come from Pennsylvania.

Notable visitors

One notable visitor has a connection to the current roster in 4-star 2027 linebacker Case Alexander, the brother of tight end Cooper Alexander and son of former 10-year NFL tight end Stephen Alexander. During his visit, Cooper took him fishing.

“I think he's super talented. He can honestly play tight end in college if he wanted to,” Cooper said last week of Case. “He has the skill set and the mindset too, but he'll be a defensive player because of how physical he is and the mindset he has and what he brings to the table. He's a great player.”

There were a pair of notable 2027 running backs that visited in 4-star Tre Segarra, who scheduled an official visit, per Anna Adams of 247Sports, and 3-star Kelvin Morrison. Penn State has put itself in a favorable position for them, and for 4-star running backs Aiden Gibson and Da’Jon Talley-Rhoades.

Penn State had its first official visitor in town in 4-star 2027 interior offensive lineman Carter Jones, who visited in January, which also made him the first recruit to visit when the Campbell regime started. He has official visits scheduled for Georgia, Virginia Tech, Clemson and Tenneesse.

Other notable 2027 visitors included 4-star cornerback Kei’Shjuan Telfair, who’s a name to watch for a commitment this spring, 4-star wide receiver Matthew Gregory, 4-star defensive lineman David Folorunsho and 3-star defensive lineman Aniti Paiva.

MORE FOOTBALL CONTENT

title: Penn State football draft profile | P Riley Thompson brings versatility, reliability
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Riley Thompson was beaten out by Gabe Nwosu for the starting punter role in 2025, but contributed as the Nittany Lions’ primary holder, appearing in all 13 games in the 2025 season.

He aided kicker Ryan Barker in posting a 94.7% field goal percentage, the highest conversion rate in program history, and a 97.8% extra point conversion percentage.

However, the Melbourne, Australia, native and former Nittany Lion is searching to take his punting capabilities to the professional scale.

Although Thompson doesn’t share the same raw power that Nwosu has, Thompson brings reliability to the table and many NFL teams would benefit from that consistency in their depth. Despite that, it’s likely that Thompson will go undrafted.

“You’ve got to be consistent,” Thompson said at pro day. “It's just another step up. You always start off at being the best in your area, wherever you go, and then the higher you get up, the better people get. You’ve got to be your best when your best is required."

Metrics

Height: 6-foot-2

Weight: 217 pounds

Projections

Projected round drafted: Undrafted

Player comparison: Tory Taylor

Scouting report

Thompson’s primary offering to an NFL roster is his reliability, having near identical single-season statistics throughout his two years as the Nittany Lions’ starting punter. Coupled with solid results as a holder for Barker, Thompson can play both roles when needed.

His previous experience with Australian Rules Football, where kicking is a necessary skill, allowed him to smoothly transition to American football when Florida Atlantic gave him a chance.

Thompson booted 61 punts with the Owls, averaging 45.4 yards per punt with 26 landing inside the 20-yard line — a career high. He was named a Freshman All-American by both College Football News and The Athletic.

Across his career, Thompson totalled 60 punts inside the 20-yard line and 20 inside the 10-yard line. Thompson’s 44.2-yard punt average during his time at Penn State is the third-highest all-time in program history.

In 2023, Thompson’s 48.8-yard punt average was the second-highest in a single season all-time for the Nittany Lions. His career-long punt was 63 yards against Boise State in the 2024 College Football Playoff quarterfinals.

Although he wasn’t Penn State’s starting punter in 2025, Thompson still had a solid career in the blue and white, and his success as a holder could be valuable to NFL squads.

Best team fit: Minnesota Vikings

The Vikings lost starting punter Ryan Wright to the New Orleans Saints in a sudden free agent twist, responding by signing four-time Pro Bowl punter Johnny Hekker from the Tennessee Titans.

Hekker has been steadily declining due to his age, but he still boasts a 46.8-yard career punt average and career-long 78-yard boot. He earned invites to the Pro Bowl game in 2013 and made three consecutive appearances from 2015-17.

Although Thompson might not be ready to start in the NFL, age is on his side, and inking a contract with the Vikings could allow him to grow in time for when Hekker calls it a career. As an undrafted free agent, it wouldn’t hurt to have Thompson added to Minnesota’s depth chart.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Paperbacks for Pride | 5 books for Campus Pride Month
author: Avery Pelletier I The Daily Collegian
description:
For so many of us, books have taught us about the world from the first day we learned to read. In many cases, they’re the closest any of us get to experiencing the perspective of another person.

While many college students have a list of required reading for their classes, there’s still immense value in extracurricular reading to continue to learn about the experiences of others.

In honor of Campus Pride Month, celebrated at Penn State in April, here’s a list of five LGBTQ+ centered books. Recommended by professors here at Penn State, these books cover a variety of times and genres that could make a reader go from crying to laughing.

“Collective Amnesia” by Koleka Putuma

In her collection of heartbreaking, yet unapologetic poems known as “Collective Amnesia,” South African author Koleka Putuma highlights her own life experiences as a queer woman in a predominantly Christian country.

Split into three parts, “Inherited Memory,” “Buried Memory” and "Post Memory,” are collections of poems that may be an emotionally difficult read, but are necessary to remind us all of the experiences many LGBTQ+ people continue to go through.

This recommendation comes from Dr. Gabeba Baderoon, an associate professor of women’s, gender and sexuality studies, African studies and comparative literature. She described the book as a “startlingly brilliant and engrossing collection” that portrays the experience some may go through around the world.

Baderoon said this is a collection frequently incorporated into university curricula around the world, including here at Penn State.

“Real Queer America: LGBT Stories from Red States” by Samantha Allen

Published in 2019, the nonfiction story “Real Queer America: LGBT Stories From Red States,” written by American author Samantha Allen, is a unique take on the storytelling of the experiences of many Americans.

Based on her six-week-long road trip through the United States, Allen not only highlights the experiences of different queer Americans growing up in areas that don’t traditionally support them, but also shares her own experience as a transgender woman from a Mormon family.

Jenifer Wagner-Lawlor, professor and acting head of the Department of Women’s, Gender and Sexuality Studies, recommended this book and said she’s personally taught it before.

“Woman on the Edge of Time” by Marge Piercy

In this sci-fi novel, American author Marge Piercy focuses on the experience of the main character, Connie Ramos, a Mexican-American woman experiencing homelessness, as she sees the two different possible realities of the future.

While this book is not nonfiction or specifically focused on the reality of modern-day queer people, the storyline navigates challenges around gender and sexuality in a future dystopia. It not only engages readers, but requires them to reflect on today’s reality.

Dr. Susanne M. Klausen, professor of women’s, gender and sexuality studies and African studies, recommended this book and personally deems it a “classic” for the genre.

“Raving” by McKenzie Wark

As the title says, this book may attract readers with its themes about partying. However, the experiences shared in this short, yet engaging nonfiction piece by Australian writer McKenzie Wark will be what keep readers engaged.

Focusing on the underground transgender and queer rave scene in New York, Wark goes beyond just sharing the stories that she experienced as a transgender woman in these communities. At the heart of this book is a story about connection and fluid identity.

This book was also recommended by Wagner-Lawlor, and it’s one she has also taught before.

“Close to the Knives: A Memoir of Disintegration” by David Wojnarowicz

In this collection of short essays, American writer and activist David Wojnarowicz shared more than just the stories of his personal life, but also captured the overall despair and anger many Americans, especially those in the LGBTQ+ community, felt during the AIDS epidemic.

While incredibly vulnerable and heartbreaking, this memoir is a must-read for modern readers to understand the pain that many, including Wojnarowicz, faced during this time.

This story was recommended by Kevin Bell, an associate professor of English, and he described it as a “scabrously poetic, ferociously beautiful collection of essays.”

MORE BLOGS

title: Column | Becoming Jonah: Finding freedom in the truth
author: Rebecca Fein | The Daily Collegian
description:
With scarlet lipstick applied sloppily to my lips, fake diamonds drooping from my ears and hanging from my neck and pink and purple tutus stacked upon my waist, I was a girly-girl through and through, a sharp contrast from my then “sister” Jonah.

Not to lead with the cliché troupe “they weren’t like other girls,” but when it came to

Jonah, that simply was the case.

The curly-headed, gaming-loving, baggy clothes-wearing “girl” would fight my mother at the sight of mascara, wince at the thought of a dress and heels and shook their head “no” at lilac and fuchsia.

This behavior within Jonah was always overlooked because you don’t need to like dressing up, shopping or manicures to be a “girl,” so Jonah was always considered a quirky tomboy, more interested in the Mets pre-season training and electric green than the latest trends and fashion.

It wasn’t until Jonah’s sophomore year of high school that “tomboy” wasn’t enough to describe their true identity. In light of this, Jonah pulled aside my mom one evening and came out to her as bisexual, and my parents were in full support.

It was done. My older sibling was finally living a life of truth, or so we thought.

After coming out for the first time, Jonah began exploring their sexuality. They dated women, chopped their hair to their shoulders and even started shopping in the men’s section for shorts, as that was the section they were more comfortable in.

When Thanksgiving of Jonah’s freshman year of college rolled around, they decided another change was in order.

It was when Jonah came home from college for the very first time that I remember lying in bed together while they updated me on everything college. While I was sharing whatever was happening to me at the time in the treacherous tenth grade.

It was then that Jonah asked if they could tell me something important.

I replied, “Of course,” my eagerness was apparent. “I go by Jonah now. Also, I am non-binary,” they expressed.

After about an hour of me asking questions, I’d finally understood. Jonah was never a “girly-girl” as a child because they were never a girl at all. Coming out as bisexual was a temporary transitional phase, while they figured out their real spot in the LGBTQ.

Non-binary is when a person doesn’t identify as either a girl or a boy. It’s a person who’s living in the “in-between.” A person who doesn’t use “she/her” or “he/him” pronouns but “they/them” to stay androgynous.

I was the first person Jonah had told. Jonah was Jonah to them and me, but nobody else.

Jonah trusted me, they confided in me and I vowed to stay loyal to them and support them through allyship. At the end of the day, blood makes you related, love makes you family and my love for Jonah goes farther than distance and lasts longer than time.

After coming out to me for the second time, they were ready to share it with the world and soon, everybody was introduced to Jonah.

I’ve never seen Jonah happier. Through my 19 years of watching them grow and change and their 23 years of self-reflection and development, this stage of Jonah was the brightest one.

After all, your authenticity is your power, not your flaw.

MORE COLUMNS

title: Weekly recap | Penn State baseball splits results through four games
author: Zach Schroder | For the Collegian
description:
With four games this past week, Penn State split the difference, winning the first two and losing the back half. One game was held at Medlar Field before the trip to Columbus. The blue and white are now 10-23 overall and 4-11 in conference play.

The Nittany Lions first outing was a single game at home against Fairleigh Dickinson. After a couple of strong innings and with the support of Dollar Dog Night, victory was earned 11-5 against a solid FDU team, who are now 21-12 overall and 15-3 in conference games. Michael Anderson, Bryce Molinaro and Joey DeMucci all had two runs and combined for three RBIs.

The blue and white then headed west for their three-game series against the Buckeyes. Friday afternoon's game looked like Penn State was going to go down without a fight, but the bats found their marks in the end. Three runs in both the seventh and eighth innings led the team to a comeback victory and its fourth conference win of the season. Anderson and Kevin Karstetter led the resurgence, as each had three hits and three RBIs.

The Nittany Lions second outing against the scarlet and gray was less exciting. A well-pitched and fielded game by both teams led to a low-scoring game, which Penn State would ultimately lose 3-2. Penn State almost came back again, but Ohio State pitcher Ryan Zamaro shut it down. Jayden Davis and Avery Smith both had two hits, and Spencer Barnett made notable plays from third base.

The third matchup got away from the blue and white quickly, going down 6-2 in just the second inning. Runs in the sixth and eighth innings tightened the score, but the Buckeyes ' lead proved too large. Anderson and Davis continued their nice play through the weekend; each had three hits. Ohio State would win 15-10 and win the series overall 2-1.

Highlighting the Nittany Lions' pitching this week was Ben Hudson. After a rocky start with a couple of bruised batters, Hudson held the Buckeyes, giving his offense a chance to stay in the game. Robert Brown III, Diamond Loosli and Kyle Emmons were also utilized when needed.

Penn State now looks toward a neutral site game on Wednesday against West Virginia before getting back into conference play with a three-game series starting Friday against Rutgers at home in Lubrano Park.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball welcomes Bucknell for Country Night and Dollar Dog Night
author: Brielle Gari I The Daily Collegian
description:
After Penn State’s month-long conference play, it is set to host the Bucknell Bison for Country Night and for a Dollar Dog Night.

The Nittany Lions fell short after three straight losses to Big Ten rival Northwestern, facing a tough weekend at home. The blue and white look to bounce back in front of a home crowd.

Having a 26-15 overall season, Penn State is set for its 42nd game of the season against the Bison.

In series history, the Nittany Lions have dominated Bucknell, leading 35-9. Tuesday night's game will be the 45th meeting between the programs.

Facing a non-conference in-state opponent, the Bison are at the bottom of the Patriot League with a 3-6 conference record. Bucknell has had a tough season, with only six wins.

Junior outfielder Sarah Zimmerman has led the Bison with a .342 batting average and is tied with freshman catcher Barbara Coppola with two home runs on the season.

Bucknell has struggled throughout the season, especially with their lack of production at the plate. Only having three players on the roster with a batting average above .300.

In the circle for the Bison, freshman right-handed pitcher Avery Powers leads with 62 strikeouts in her rookie season. The Tampa, Florida, native has a season high of 13 strikeouts in a single game.

The third hitter in the .300 batting average range is junior infielder Brooke Popella. She leads Bucknell with 31 hits in her 2026 campaign. She was named to the Second Team All-Patriot League during her sophomore season.

With 76 hits, 46 RBIs and 16 doubles on the season, Popella has been a main factor in the Bison’s lineup.

The Bucknell roster features a mix of veterans and young players, with six freshmen and six sophomores.

A Route 45 rivalry as the Bison look to upset the Nittany Lions again in the 2019 season. This nonconference match-up will be an interesting one as the blue and white just suffered a Big Ten conference sweep.

Penn State has two pitchers in the top-15 pitchers by ERA in the Big Ten, junior McKenna Young and sophomore Abigail Britton.

On the offense side, junior Brooke Klosowicz leads the blue and white with her .373 batting average. With 14 home runs in her rookie season, freshman infielder Allison Oneacre. Freshman Breanna Hanik has been a major contributor to Penn State’s success, posting a .337 batting average.

The blue and white look for redemption after a long weekend of little action at the plate and long innings. Only having a full day without a game, their matchup against Bucknell is a perfect opportunity to see how coach Clarisa Crowell’s squad reacts to tough losses.

First pitch is set for Tuesday, April 14th at 6. p.m at Nittany Lion Softball Park at Beard Field.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball prepares for single-game road clash against West Virginia
author: Ashley Felsberg I The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State is set to face No. 15, West Virginia, on Wednesday at 5:30 pm at Meritus Park in Hagerstown, Maryland. The blue and white will look to improve their record upon Rutgers’ arrival on Friday for their three-game series.

The Nittany Lions are coming off two recent losses against Ohio State and are now 10-23 on the season. With only 19 games left to play, these late-season matches are crucial with a Big Ten Tournament spot on the line.

Penn State was supposed to have faced the Mountaineers back in mid-March, but the game was canceled due to inclement weather, making last year the last time the two teams met. Penn State won that game 3-2.

Throughout the 2025 game, Butash pitched 4.2 scoreless innings, holding the Mountaineers to two runs. This win also became the Nittany Lions’ first ranked win in two years.

Jack Porter earned the Nittany Lions their first run, hitting his seventh home run on the season. Bryce Molinaro was responsible for the second and third runs after his single sent Porter and Paxton King home.

Going into this upcoming game, Bryce Molinaro will be one to watch at home plate. Molinaro has nine home runs on the season and 26 RBI’s.

Along with Molinaro, Michael Anderson has put up a strong front on the blue and white offense, batting .380, with 15 home runs, 40 RBIs.

West Virginia has a 24-8 record this season, coming off a 10-2 win against Texas Tech and winning two of its three-game series this past weekend.

Sophomore Chansen Cole pitched for eight innings straight, only letting up a total of two runs and seven hits, while striking out four batters.

Aside from Cole, graduate student Sean Smith has been putting up a strong offensive front, totaling seven hits on the weekend at Dan Law Field at Rip Griffin Park. He helped the Mountaineers on Friday with two RBIs and on Sunday with two RBIs and two runs.

History is on Penn State’s side, by a small margin. The Nittany Lions have won 74 times, while the Mountaineers have won 45 times. However, within the last ten matches, the wins are split.

West Virginia will pose a challenge for the blue and white, as coach Gambino’s squad has struggled to produce heavy offensive patterns.

On the contrary, West Virginia has also let some teams this season capitalize at the plate and put up large numbers, Georgia Southern, UCF, BYU all scoring 10 runs and ASU scoring 14.

In just six games played, the forward tallied three goals and seven assists to help the Admirals clinch a Calder Cup Playoff berth.

title: Penn State women's gymnastics' Ava Piedrahita enters the transfer portal for final year
author: Sami Altmire | The Daily Collegian
description:
Senior Ava Piedrahita is leaving Penn State to continue her academic and athletic career elsewhere for her final year of eligibility.

Piedrahita leaves the Nittany Lions after a successful season with the team and individually. The Gaithersburg, Maryland native has spent the last four seasons with the blue and white, and is a standout all-around gymnast.

Carrying multiple 9's on vault, bars, and floor, Piedrahita was a constant for the Nittany Lions. Piedrahita also earned All-American honors on vault after her first collegiate season, and has continued to succeed on that apparatus.

Piedrahita is also a Senior Second Team All-Big Ten member, and at the 2026 NCAA Tempe Regionals she was a large point contributor to Penn State's overall scores.

title: Former Penn State women's basketball assistant coach Bob Dunn hired as Cleveland State head coach
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Another part of Penn State's program has found a new home.

Former assistant coach Bob Dunn was hired as the next head coach at Cleveland State on Monday.

Dunn spent one year with the Lady Lions after a year as an assistant coach with the Vikings.

Cleveland State is coming off a 26-10 record last season, including an appearance in the WNIT Great 8.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Recruiting roundup | Penn State men’s basketball lands 1st transfer commitment, hosts another visitor
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Through Day 7 of the transfer portal being open, Penn State has hosted a pair of visitors, made its first addition Sunday afternoon and expressed interest to two other prospects.

This all comes as the Nittany Lions are trying to construct a roster that has already lost seven players to the portal. With all that turnover, Penn State still has up to six spots available as the roster stands now.

So, here’s all the recent recruiting news for the Nittany Lions.

Jay Rodgers commits

Central Connecticut State transfer Jay Rodgers became the first portal prospect to commit to Penn State, joining international players Roko Prkačin and François Wibaut and incoming freshman Jamison White as the current offseason additions the Nittany Lions are set to bring in for next season.

Rodgers immediately fills a need for Penn State’s roster, which lacked a point guard before his commitment. He averaged 6.9 assists per game this past season, so he slots in as the starting point guard as it stands now.

The 6-foot-3, 185-pounder also brings a veteran presence with him to Happy Valley. The 24-year-old has played college basketball since the 2020-21 season at New Orleans before making a stop in JUCO at Cochise College in 2021-22 prior to his four-year stint at Central Connecticut State.

Rodgers was on a visit to Penn State this past weekend, and he’ll have one more year of eligibility to use with the Nittany Lions.

Another visitor

Rodgers wasn’t the only transfer portal prospect Penn State hosted this past weekend, as IUP guard Dallis Dillard did as well. Dillard is coming off a standout year, earning second-team All-America honors by the Division II Conference Commissioners Association.

Dillard averaged 20.6 points, 5.8 rebounds, 4.7 assists and 2.5 steals per game across 32 starts for IUP in 2025-26. He’s another veteran prospect as he began his career in 2021-22 with the Crimson Hawks.

Dillard, who shot 46.8% from the field and 37.8% from 3-point range, seems like he’d fit Penn State more as a two guard when considering the team’s current needs. With Rodgers at point guard, the Nittany Lions still need a starting shooting guard, so Dillard could slot in there.

Other players linked to Penn State

Penn State has reached out to two more transfer portal prospects in UAB guard Chance Westry and Buffalo guard Daniel Freitag as well.

This isn’t the first time Mike Rhoades and company are interested in Westry, as they recruited him when he was in the portal ahead of the 2023-24 season. Westry was a 247Sports composite 4-star and the No. 39 player nationally in the 2022 class, and he’s a native of Harrisburg, Pennsylvania.

Westry had a breakout year for UAB this past season, averaging 15.5 points, 3.8 rebounds and 5.5 assists per game across 32 appearances. He earned second-team All-AAC honors following his year.

Similarly, Freitag also burst onto the scene at Buffalo, averaging 19.8 points, 4.3 rebounds, 3.8 assists and 1.3 steals per game across 23 appearances in 2025-26. He garnered an All-MAC honorable mention as well.

Freitag has some Big Ten experience as he was at Wisconsin for his freshman year in 2024-25. He averaged 2.1 minutes per game across 14 appearances with the Badgers, and he was a 4-star recruit in the 2024 class.
