Kortovich said he’s helped the new tight end additions adjust to playing and living at Penn State. After practice No. 8 out of 15 in the spring, he thinks the blend of returnees and transfers has gone well.

"It's been great since day one. They came in and right away were already blending as a group,” Kortovich said. “Like over time, just these relationships keep building and building, especially now that we're playing football, we're out there together on the field, which has been building tremendously.”

With tight ends Benjamin Brahmer, Gabe Burkle and Cooper Alexander already familiar with Mouser’s scheme from their days at Iowa State, Kortovich said they’ve been helping him learn the offense. He said it’s been “pretty cool to see their perspectives” during his first offseason with Mouser, who’s also the Nittany Lions’ tight ends coach.

But Kortovich has goals for himself this spring along with learning the offense and he prepares for his redshirt freshman year.

“Just pretty much get as comfortable as I can with the offense and just keep improving my game one by one,” Kortovich said. “Then also just building bonds with these guys, it's gonna be really special in the season, so just continue to do that.”

MORE FOOTBALL CONTENT

title: Center Brock Riker impressing this spring as Dominic Rulli works to return from injury
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State’s staff has been high on Brock Riker since they saw his name in the transfer portal. Offensive line coach Ryan Clanton said in February that he thought Riker “was the best center in the world.”

Riker received offers from Alabama, Oklahoma, Illinois, South Carolina and more while he was in the portal this offseason, but he decided to make Penn State his next home despite Dominic Rulli deciding to stay in hopes of earning the center job.

Now, the two are competing for the starting spot, but with Rulli sidelined this spring, Riker has been taking advantage of the extra reps.

“(Riker’s) probably been one of the biggest surprises to me,” offensive coordinator Taylor Mouser said. “We put a lot on the center, from being able to ID in the run game and slide protection, you know, wherever it needs to go, his ability to go and get it started (is great). That's always been the engine that's kind of run our offense. When we've got a really good center, the offense has been good.”

For Penn State, center has always been a consistent position with the next guy in waiting. Juice Scruggs played himself into being a second-round draft pick before Hunter Nourzad took over. After Nourzad departed for the NFL, Nick Dawkins finally got his chance and was the starter for the last two seasons.

Rulli was supposed to be next in line, as he’s entering his fifth year at Penn State after years of special teams play, but Riker is making a serious case for the job and has been taking every first-team center rep.

“I think Brock's taken advantage of every day,” Matt Campbell said. “He's really talented. He's a guy that, when you talk game reps, he certainly got great game reps. He got competitive reps to be the center and start as many games as he's played. You see a maturity in his game reps. And I think you've seen a physical growth for him.”

Riker came to Penn State at around 270 pounds, according to Campbell, and is now sitting around 295 pounds with the expectation of hitting 305 before fall camp. His ability to move despite the added weight has stuck out to Campbell this spring.

But Rulli isn’t giving up the starting center spot without a fight. He said at the end of February that he wants to compete and earn the position, as he originally joined Penn State as a walk-on and has “earned pretty much everything” he’s gotten.

Being unable to practice so far this spring has made it tough for Rulli to compete with Riker, but Campbell said Rulli’s doing “everything in his power to practice.”

“Dom's been in a tough spot. He's one of the toughest kids that I've been around,” Campbell said. “I think Dom is one of those guys that's so trusted already in our football program because of his mannerisms, his actions, I feel like he's been a great leader from the time that we've stepped on campus here.”

Campbell added that his biggest goal for Rulli right now is to get him healthy, and that nothing is set in stone right now regarding the center battle despite the strong impressions Riker’s made so far this spring.

“Obviously, as we get to fall camp, that's where some of these competitions will start to unfold themselves,” Campbell said. “And Dom, he's been a great leader, even with Brock and that whole line room of just as we install, being able to be a great communicator with everybody.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball welcomes Northwestern, hoping to keep Big Ten win streak alive
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
After starting Big Ten play 1-5, Penn State has found its footing. The Nittany Lions have swept their last two series against Rutgers and Maryland, and will look to keep the momentum when they welcome Northwestern this weekend.

Historically, the Wildcats have overwhelmed the blue and white, leading the all-time series 56-15, and sweeping the two-game series last season. As for Penn State, the Nittany Lions haven’t defeated the Wildcats since a two-game set in May of 2011.

The blue and white rank in the top half of the conference with a 7-5 record. Northwestern falls towards the bottom, owning a 5-7 record in the Big Ten.

The Wildcats lost 2-of-3 games against Michigan last weekend and dropped their matchup against DePaul on Wednesday before heading to Happy Valley.

Offensively, Northwestern posts a .285 team batting average, with only Illinois and Michigan State having lower averages in the conference. The Nittany Lions’ .286 team batting average ranks 12th in the conference.

Emma Raye is the Wildcats’ strongest slugger, leading the lineup with a .350 batting average, .690 slugging percentage and 35 RBIs. The Superior, Wisconsin, native also leads Northwestern with 16 extra-base hits, including eight home runs.

Another key part of the Wildcats’ offense is sophomore Kaylie Avvisato. The Leesburg, Virginia, native owns a .327 batting average, and her 31 RBIs are second on the team. Avvisato is a major threat on the basepaths with a team-high five steals on six attempts.

Penn State’s strong offensive duo consists of infielders Allison Oneacre and Brooke Klosowicz. Both are top-10 in the conference when it comes to RBIs. Oneacre’s 49 RBIs are four shy of the single-season record set by Klosowicz just last year.

Klosowicz is also nearing her own record with 42 RBIs on the season. The Johnsburg, Illinois, native leads the Nittany Lions with a .386 batting average, followed by Oneacre’s .367.

The blue and white lineup faces a strong Northwestern pitching staff that owns a 3.73 ERA.

Freshman Marina Mason is the go-to arm for the Wildcats. Mason’s 2.47 ERA is fifth in the Big Ten, and her 100 strikeouts are tied for sixth. The Brentwood, Tennessee, native has pitched 90.2 innings this season; the second-most innings pitched on the Northwestern staff is Riley Grudzielanek’s 40.2.

This series has potential for a pitchers' duel with Penn State posting a 3.37 ERA, the third lowest in the conference.

The Nittany Lions also have a freshman who’s top-five in the Big Ten in ERA, thanks to Mackenzie Duncan’s 2.24.

McKenna Young’s 2.73 ERA is 10th in the conference, making Penn State the only team with multiple arms in the top-10 in ERA.

Duncan is also the team's strikeout leader at 59, and Bridget Nemeth has seen the most action with 52.2 innings pitched.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Report: Penn State men’s basketball 7-footer Justin Houser enters transfer portal
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
One of Penn State’s 7-footers is departing the program.

Freshman Justin Houser is entering the transfer portal with three years of eligibility remaining, according to Dushawn London of 247Sports.

Penn State big man Justin Houser is entering the transfer portal per his agent Ken Jackson of Sky Limit Sports pic.twitter.com/G5Nb4b7VW6— Dushawn London (@DushawnLondon1) April 9, 2026

The Nittany Lions’ original plan for Houser was to potentially redshirt and develop behind 7-footer Yanic Konan Niederhauser. Instead, as Konan Niederhauser left for the 2025 NBA draft, Houser was thrust into a depth role that saw him burn his redshirt.

Houser appeared in 18 games this season, averaging 1.4 points and 1.1 rebounds per game with 42.9% shooting from the field. He showed to be willing to shoot the 3-point ball, knocking down 3-of-9 3-pointers.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Decision to stay at Penn State was an ‘easy choice’ for special teams coordinator Justin Lustig
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
The Penn State special teams saw a successful 2025 campaign.

As a result of that, special teams coordinator Justin Lustig became a priority for Matt Campbell to retain on his first staff in Happy Valley. Despite the mass coaching turnover, Lustig stayed true to the Nittany Lions.

“It starts with my family, personally. Growing up in Pennsylvania, my wife is from Pennsylvania, family and friends, I went to college an hour down the road (at Bucknell),” Lustig said. “This is home, and Penn State is Penn State.”

While the choice seemed clear for the Erie native, that didn’t mean other schools would come calling.

After a season where Gabe Nwosu averaged 46 yards per punt, Ryan Barker went 18-for-19 on field goals and defensive end Dani Dennis-Sutton blocked three punts, there would naturally be suitors for Lustig.

“There's a lot that came up,” Lustig said. “I mean, I was a free agent, so you get some calls and you have to weigh things out. At the end of the day it was an easy choice to stay.”

The 2025 season wasn’t an easy one for the Nittany Lions, as they failed to live up to preseason national championship aspirations.

It was an unfamiliar experience for Lustig, saying, “the adversity in the middle of the season was a challenge.”

“Never been put in that type of adversity as a coach,” Lustig said. Learning how to deal with that was a challenge, but something I think I've grown from.”

Other than Barker returning, Lustig’s room is completely revamped from a year ago for his third season in Happy Valley.

Mississippi State transfer Nathan Tiyce projects to be the punter. JMU kickoff specialist Cristiano Rosa will likely take on that role. Blaise Sokach-Minnick is the favorite to take over for Tyler Duzasnky at long snapper. Backup kicker Matthew Parker is currently the No. 2 punter, with the role likely going to freshman Lucas Tenbrock when he enrolls in the summer.

With all the new faces and players expected to take on elevated roles, Lustig has been handling it smoothly.

“He’s so attention-to-detail oriented. He knows how good you can be, and he's trying to make you reach that level every single practice,” Tiyce said. “He feels every single rep should feel like the Super Bowl, and he's putting pressure on you, and he's only doing it because he cares about you so much.”

It may look different inside the Lasch Building, but Lustig is seeing strides with the team chemistry as Penn State looks to turn around a season from a year ago that the special teams coordinator wasn’t expecting.

“I think the connectivity of the staff and the players has been outstanding,” Lustig said. “And we knew that was going to be potentially a challenge, especially with the players, but I feel like we're in a great spot with that.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Report: Former Penn State men's basketball forward Kebba Njie enters transfer portal
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
A member of the 2022-23 Penn State team will be in search of a new school.

Notre Dame forward Kebba Njie has entered the transfer portal following three years with the Fighting Irish, according to Pete Nakos of On3.

NEW: Notre Dame forward Kebba Njie has entered the NCAA transfer portal, @PeteNakos reports.https://t.co/G7gO8X9lN0 pic.twitter.com/WWueUFA5Ip— Transfer Portal (@TransferPortal) April 9, 2026

Njie made 17 appearances in 2025-26 before enduring a season-ending injury. He was averaging 2.5 points and 3.4 rebounds per game before suffering his injury.

Last year, Njie declared for the 2025 NBA draft, but he withdrew his name so he could return to Notre Dame for his senior season. Njie has played in 114 games throughout his career, averaging 4.2 points and 4.6 rebounds per game.

He followed former Penn State head coach Micah Shrewsberry to Notre Dame after the coaching change in Happy Valley in March 2023.

At Penn State in 2022-23, Njie started 26 games as a freshman on the Nittany Lions, who reached the second round of the NCAA Tournament that season. He averaged 3.4 points and 3.5 rebounds per game, and he's the fourth-highest rated recruit in Penn State history by the 247Sports composite.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State notebook | Matt Campbell gives praise to Taylor Mouser halfway through spring ball
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Matt Campbell was playing defense during drills along with others on the coaching staff as the offense worked out on Penn State’s practice fields Thursday.

Afterward, Campbell came to his post-practice press conference and told the media to not ask any questions about his defensive performance so he could give insight into the Nittany Lions as they are roughly halfway through spring ball.

Here’s more of what Campbell said.

Taylor Mouser

Taylor Mouser first joined Campbell’s staff after being a graduate assistant at Toledo in 2015 and stayed with him at Iowa State. Now, Mouser is the offensive coordinator in Campbell’s first staff at Penn State this year.

Campbell gave praise to Mouser as he’s getting started as the leader of the Nittany Lions’ offense, describing he’s had an “interesting journey.”

“I've always been a huge fan of promoting from within, and especially people that come in and do a great job,” Campbell said. “Taylor, he's curious. He's a great connector, and you kind of just saw every responsibility that we gave him, he elevated any of those areas and started out a lot, if it was in recruiting at Toledo and really his first year at Iowa State, we would give a lot of credit.”

Campbell gave credit to Mouser for identifying quarterback Brock Purdy and running back Breece Hall in the recruiting process, both of whom enjoyed productive careers at Iowa State and now in the NFL.

In addition to finding talent in high school, Campbell highlighted Mouser’s ability to develop. Campbell said multiple Iowa State alums attributed Mouser’s tutelage for why they could accomplish as players.

After former Iowa State offensive coordinator Nathan Scheelhaase left for the NFL following the 2023 season, Mouser got the opportunity to land his first offensive coordinator gig in 2024.

“It was a pretty easy decision on who deserved the right to take over the reins of the offense,” Campbell said. “What we did in (2024) and (2025) offensively, 24, an awesome year, and then 25 we had a new group of guys that kind of surrounded Rocco (Becht), and I thought we did a really great job.”

Halfway through spring

Penn State had practice No. 8 of spring ball Thursday, which is just past the halfway mark of 15 total spring practices. Campbell talked about the purpose of spring ball and what he’s expecting of his players.

“The goal of spring practice is, how do we practice?” Campbell said. “What does it look like to show up every day with great consistency? We're asking a lot of our guys in this five-week block of spring practice. We really are, from continuing to gain strength in the weight room to putting in two brand new systems on the offensive, defensive side of the football.”

With the Campbell era starting this year, the Nittany Lions went through turnover with the roster filled with returnees, transfers and freshmen. Mouser is installing his scheme for the first time at Penn State, while defensive coordinator D’Anton Lynn is doing the same for the defense.

With the Blue-White Practice set for April 25, Campbell said there’s still “great room to grow” until then.

“Just showing up isn't good,” Campbell said. “How you study every day, how you prepare, how you take care of your body, all those things have really been our emphasis. As I kind of talked to our guys at the midway point, we talked about it yesterday, is can we, individually and collectively, create momentum for ourselves leaving spring practice.”

Linebackers

Campbell assessed his linebacker room so far through spring ball during Thursday's presser. Notably, Tony Rojas is out for the spring due to injury, but Campbell highlighted the trio of Iowa State transfers in Kooper Ebel, Cael Brezina and Caleb Bacon.

“We've been fortunate because I think those three linebackers that came with us from Iowa State are guys that have played a ton,” Campbell said.

Ebel, Brezina and Bacon have played a combined 89 games in their careers. All three of them were key pieces in the linebacker rotation for the Cyclones last season, including Ebel and Bacon starting in all 12 games.

But other than the veterans in the position group, Campbell mentioned Cam Smith and Alex Tatsch.

“Cam is a young guy that's really flashed,” Campbell said. “He's athletic, he can run, and so we're super excited about that group, and you're not even talking about, obviously, my admiration for Alex Tatsch and what I've seen on video, and how I've watched him work.”

Smith is entering his redshirt freshman year after only appearing in one game last season. Tatsch, meanwhile, burned his redshirt in 2025 as he played in six games, tallying 14 tackles.

Campbell has already given high praise to Tatsch before, saying he’s got a chance to be one of the all-time great linebackers in Penn State history during a press conference in February.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Practice observations | Penn State QB Rocco Becht sees expanded snaps in buildup to Blue-White
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
On the shriek of the whistle, defensive tackle Alijah Carnell exploded from his downward stance, lifted both his hands in the air side-by-side and forcefully pushed the tackling dummy.

The State College weather brought a beautiful day on the Penn State practice field for the eighth spring session under Matt Campbell. The sun was shining, and for the just over midway point of spring ball, vibes seemed high.

After starting with stretching, the team split into their position groups. Familiar faces from last year’s squad popped in to watch practice, including running back Nick Singleton, linebacker Dom DeLuca, long snapper Tyler Duzansky.

The defensive line worked with tackling dummies, the linebackers worked on agility drills, the running backs corralled tosses for outside runs, the tight ends went through a gauntlet before hauling in passes and bouncing off a yoga ball and the quarterbacks worked on hitting pass-catchers on quick routes.

When the offense joined together to work on routes on air, there were a few noticeable developments on the injury front.

Rocco Becht saw an expanded role working with the first-team unit, backing up Matt Campbell’s statement from a few weeks ago, where he said the quarterback was ahead of schedule. During the last viewing session of practice for the media, Becht didn’t take part in the routes on air portion of practice.

Joining him as new additions to the routes on air portion of practice were wide receivers Chase Sowell and Brett Eskildsen. The wideout tandem has been limited in spring ball, but they took part in first-team reps wearing the purple non-contact jerseys that all the quarterbacks don.

In regard to personnel, the wideouts were joined by Koby Howard and running back Carson Hansen for the first team to take the field. The starting offensive line was Malachi Goodman at left tackle, Trevor Buhr at left guard, Brock Riker at center, Cooper Cousins at right guard and Garrett Sexton at left tackle.

All three tight ends in Benjamin Brahmer, Cooper Alexander and Finn Furmanek worked with every offensive team due to injuries in the room. Brian Kortovich was absent from practice, but later spoke to the media.

Andrew Rappleyea, who is out for spring ball, was seen at practice without a boot on his right foot or a motorized scooter, which he had at Penn State Pro Day. Gabe Burkle, who is also out for spring ball, wasn’t seen.

As far as injuries, wide receiver Peter Gonzalez and defensive tackle Siale Taupaki wore a lighter blue practice jersey and partook in drills after mainly rehabbing during the previous viewing session.

Names that stayed the same in terms of missing practice or were rehabbing are linebacker Tony Rojas, defensive end Max Granville, quarterback Alex Manske, offensive tackle Anthony Donkoh, safety Jeremiah Cooper, linebacker Alex Tatsch, defensive tackle De’Andre Cook and wide receiver Ethan Black.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Local DJ Josh Kondé wants to make ‘timeless’ music
author: Ashlyn Kafer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Skateboarders, videographers, vintage car owners and dancers swarmed Oliv Highland’s parking lot on Friday, prompted by their love of local DJ Josh Kondé’s music. Donning clothing reminiscent of 2016, the group served as background characters in a promotional video for Kondé's upcoming song “Ain’t Shi.”

Mere hours before, Kondé signed with Swedish record label Electronic Future. The accomplishment had the fourth-year studying telecommunications and media industries, brimming with energy as he filmed the video, kicking off the start of a new journey in his music career.

Kondé's DJing journey began the summer after his sophomore year, inspired by Lucas Dimyan, his fraternity Alpha Phi Delta’s (APD) former DJ. Damien taught him the introductory skills and from there he was able to land a job DJing in Maryland.

“My mom found some random Facebook group thing where they were going to teach people how to DJ for free,” Kondé said. “So I just show up to this random place an hour away by myself. Most people left after like 40 minutes but I ended up staying and screwing around and started DJing with his five different ports he had there, and I ended up making his kids dance, and he was like 'no you actually know what you're doing, let me give you a job.'"

Victoria Colucci, one of the participants in Kondé’s music video, said he has a knack for connecting audiences through sound.

“I feel that, if anything, the DJ themselves or whoever is performing sets the scene for what the night is going to be,” Colucci, a fourth-year studying marketing, said. “I have known Josh ever since he was DJing at APD, which is his frat, and I will say that every time he came on, the room literally lit up.”

After DJing at his fraternity more frequently, Kondé realized if he were to pursue DJing after college, he wouldn’t want to get stuck exclusively playing “Top 40” hits. Instead, he said he’s more interested in festivals or music-centered events where he can experiment with niche genres.

He transitioned into engineering his own shows, so he could avoid getting stuck playing popular party music, which led to Kondé headlining a show at Superior Ingredients in New York City.

“I’m moving more toward festivals and music events where it’s about the music and not just about a party,” Kondé said. “But to get your way up to being the headliner at a festival, you need to make sure your music perfectly fits in one of the stage’s categories. Still, sometimes, I’ll just let the song turn into whatever it wants to be.”

DJing has connected Kondé to many new people and experiences, though juggling professional aspirations in the music industry and creating music himself as a full-time student has proved to be challenging.

Despite the challenges, the experiences and opportunities have made the struggles worth the effort. Kondé opened for DaBaby in the Bryce Jordan Center and Quavo at LightsUP, performed before the National Act at THON and soon will take the stage at Mojofest, a touring music festival happening in April.

Hykina Cominskie, who’s previously seen him play several sets and helped to create his music video as well, said Kondé adjusts sets to match the audience and curate the vibe of the room.

“He’s very diverse and will perform with multiple types of DJs or play multiple types of sounds,” Cominskie, a third-year studying graphic design and engineering design and innovation, said. “I’ve heard him DJ Latin night and I’ve also heard him at THON. So it’s pretty awesome to hear him go from both of his styles. He’s definitely very good at keeping the vibe and I feel like he’s always smiling behind things, so that makes me feel like I can smile as well.”

As Kondé progresses in his career and looks to his future, he plans to work in the music industry. He hopes to bring back the hype of older house tracks and invite new elements into the scene to set himself apart from other DJs.

“I’m trying to combine pop with real vocalists,” Kondé said. “While everyone’s using samples of other things, I’m getting new stuff from scratch with vocalists I find all over the U.S., combining the elements that I really like from indie with the emotional drops of EDM to turn them into a cohesive piece.”

As his time at Penn State nears its end, Kondé said his love of music has made the challenge and hardship bearable.

“Working with vocalists takes forever, and I have to make all the words from scratch and every single individual inflection of any of their notes is another thing I have to think about,” Kondé said. “It’s harder, but that’s what I want to get into. I want to make something that’s timeless.”

MORE LIFESTYLE CONTENT

title: Penn State men’s volleyball continues road trip against Sacred Heart for penultimate series showdown
author: Chase Mitchell | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State has been on a tear lately, winning five straight matches after suffering three straight losses on the West Coast.

Now, the blue and white have one more road test against a struggling EIVA team for its penultimate series of the season.

The Nittany Lions take on 10-11 Sacred Heart in Fairfield, Connecticut, this weekend, with the first serve at 7 p.m. Friday, while returning to the court for a 3 p.m. start Saturday.

Bring out the brooms

Penn State’s five consecutive wins have come in convincing fashion. Since its 3-1 victory over Ohio State in Pittsburgh in late March, the blue and white picked up four straight 3-0 wins en route to series sweeps over NJIT and Charleston.

On the other end of the court, the Pioneers, struggling in conference play, were victims of a 3-0 defeat to the Golden Eagles back in February, and lost both matches in its George Mason series with the same score.

With Sacred Heart coming off back-to-back close wins over EIVA bottom-dweller Princeton and Penn State plowing through its competition as of late, something’s gotta give when the two programs looking to build on their momentum butt heads this weekend.

Weekly award winners

The Nittany Lions sit atop the EIVA standings, and have some of the conference’s best players to show why.

Recently, the blue and white’s outside hitter Sean Harvey and middle blocker Owen Rose were awarded with EIVA Offensive Player of the Week and EIVA Defensive Player of the Week awards, respectively.

The honors came after Penn State’s three straight wins over ranked foes Ohio State and NJIT where Harvey notched 39 kills in that stretch while also recording 12 digs and an ace to accompany a .411 hitting percentage.

In that same stretch, Rose, the Long Island native, tallied 15 kills, 16 blocks and four aces to round out a .619 hitting percentage.

Harvey’s .316 season-long hitting percentage puts the redshirt sophomore at the top of the EIVA for that statistic, but sits third in the conference in total kills, with 263 on the year.

Not only does Rose also sit atop the conference in blocks, with 111 on the season, but he also leads the NCAA with that mark, averaging 1.44 blocks per set – also the highest in the country.

Team stats

Penn State comes in at the No. 1 spot in four of the EIVA’s statistical categories, while Sacred Heart is the best in just one category. The Pioneers also sit dead last in another.

The Nittany Lions are the kings of blocks in the conference, averaging 2.85 per set. Mark Pavlik’s squad’s 1.5 service aces per set also lead the EIVA. On average, the blue and white’s opponents record 7.36 digs per set and have a .210 hitting percentage, both the fewest in the conference.

Sacred Heart’s opponents tally 1.99 blocks per set on average, the fewest in the EIVA. Oppositely, the Pioneers’ opponents average 9.38 digs per set, which is the worst in the conference.

In many categories, both units sit next to each other right around the middle of the competition. However, Penn State still has the edge in most statistical categories and could prove to be too explosive an offensive unit for Sacred Heart, which surrenders just north of 12 kills per set.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Super Gay 5K, Lavender Recognition | How to  commemorate campus Pride
author: Neel Ved | The Daily Collegian
description:
April kicks off campus Pride Month and for those looking to celebrate, there are plenty of events around campus.

Below are some events that might interest you.

Saturday, April 11

Super Gay 5K

At 9 a.m., the Center for Sexual and Gender Diversity will be hosting their annual Super Gay 5k at the HUB Pollock Plaza. Anyone is invited to come to this event whether they’ll be running, walking or just supporting their friends along the way.

Any experience level is welcome as a runner or walker. Registration for this event is required.

Wednesday, April 15

Pride in Every Field: A STEM Mixer with Rainbow Networks and Allies

From 12:30 to 2 p.m., at the Noontime Lounge in the HUB-Robeson Center (HUB) the Eberly College of Science, College of Information Science and Technology, College of Engineering, College of Earth and Mineral Sciences, College of Agricultural Sciences and College of Health and Human Development will be having a relaxed afternoon of bonding, community and joining forces among the LGBTQIA+ community members, Rainbow Networks and allies.

Anyone is welcome to attend this event. Drinks and refreshments will be provided.Those interested in attending must register by 5 p.m. on Monday, April 13.

LGBTQ+ in STEM Leading the Way: Perspectives on Identity

From 6 to 8 p.m. the Eberly College of Science, College of Information Science and Technology, College of Engineering, College of Earth and Mineral Sciences and College of Health and Human Development will be hosting an interactive discussion, panel and performance on the crossroads of STEM identity and queerness. This event will feature the STEM community members of Opulence.

Anyone is welcome to attend this event in Room 114 of the McAllister Building. Dinner will be provided once registered.

To receive dinner people must register for this event by 5 p.m. Friday, April 10.

Tuesday, April 21

Pride Prom: Under the Sea

From 6 to 9 p.m. in Heritage Hall at the HUB-Robeson Center (HUB) there will be an evening of commemoration, bonding and community at the annual Pride Prom hosted by the Center for Sexual and Gender Diversity. There will be music, dancing and self-expression in an inclusive and supportive environment.

The theme for this year is “Under the Sea” and it will feature ocean-inspired decor. Anyone attending this event is welcome to come in whatever they feel most comfortable and interpretive dress is encouraged.

Friday, April 24

Lavender Recognition

From 6 to 9 p.m., the annual Lavender Recognition will be taking place in Alumni Hall.

Lavender Recognition is a ceremony that commemorates graduating students and their allies, honoring their accomplishments, authority and contributions to the campus community in a nurturing and affirming environment.

The event will have a formal program and shared meal meant to create an environment of reflection, celebration and connection among graduates, friends and members of the community.

The dress code is business formal or cocktail attire, graduates are welcome to dress in any way that makes them feel like themselves.

Registration for this event is required.

Sunday, April 26

Get OUTside for Pride

From 11:30 a.m. to 5:30 p.m., as classes start to come to a close and finals week approaches, the Center for Sexual and Gender Studies has teamed up with Campus Recreation - Outdoor Adventures and Counseling and Psychological Services to spend the day at Stone Valley Recreation Area.

There will be activities to take a break from academic stress and spend time in nature through mindful reflection, outdoor adventure activities, and community connection.

Registration for this event is required, free food will also be provided.

MORE CAMPUS COVERAGE

title: Penn State football draft profile | Punter Gabe Nwosu offers size, versatility
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
Week 1 against Nevada, Gabe Nwosu surprisingly earned the starting job at punter over two-year starter Riley Thompson.

Nwosu being elevated panned out for Penn State, as he booted the ball 38 times in his lone year in the role, averaging 46 yards per punt. He had a season-high of 68 yards, pinning the ball inside the opponent's 20-yard line 15 times.

In addition to taking over punting duties as a redshirt senior, Nwosu was a three-year starter as the kickoff specialist, holding the job down for the last 42 games.

The Derwood, Maryland, native is not only a physically intriguing specialist with his 6-foot-6, 313-pound frame, but also an interesting player off the field with his interests in cello and soccer. He’ll also graduate in the spring with his MBA in supply chain management from the Smeal College of Business.

Nwosu will likely go undrafted, but he’s a more than appealing option to be picked up after the draft concludes or earn a training camp invite.

“You always dream about, like, 'can I go down the road and fast forward eight, nine years later, in this position,’” Nwosu said at Penn State Pro Day. “It’s a true, true blessing. There's a lot of hard work that's gone into it.”

Metrics

Height: 6-foot-6

Weight: 313 pounds

Projections

Projected round drafted: Undrafted

Player comparison: Pressley Harvin III

Scouting report

The biggest trait that Nwosu brings to NFL teams is his position versatility as a specialist.

Most of his experience comes as a kickoff specialist, where in addition to being the starter the last three seasons, he appeared in eight games in 2022 in the gig after redshirting the year prior. This past campaign 36 of his 74 kickoffs were touchbacks.

As a punter, what particularly stands out about Nwosu is his “A ball,” which is when a punt maximizes distance and hang time. This was particularly evident when Nwosu performed at Pro Day, sending balls deep on the practice field in windy and snowy conditions, having the ball float in the air on all of his attempts, one of which went for 60 yards.

While Nwosu has never attempted a field goal, he was viewed to be an emergency option for longer-range tries last season, occasionally being seen on the sidelines warming up for kicks greater than roughly 55 yards and even connecting from 65 yards during last fall camp.

Nwosu was also selected for the 2025 edition of The Athletic’s “Freaks List,” written annually by Bruce Feldman. According to Feldman, Nwosu has squatted 525 pounds, benched 365 pounds, had a vertical jump of 31.5 inches, logged a broad jump of 9-foot-9 and ran a 4.86 40-yard dash.

In September, Nwosu was compared to former Penn State punter Jordan Stout by James Franklin, who carved out a four-year stint with the Baltimore Ravens before following head coach John Harbaugh when he took the same role with the New York Giants, which made Stout the highest paid punter in the NFL with his three-year deal worth $12.3 million.

If Nwosu can live up to what Stout has accomplished so far in the NFL, he’ll carve out a solid professional career for himself.

Best team fit: Baltimore Ravens

With Stout’s departure, that leaves the door open for Nwosu to join the Ravens, making it a homecoming for the Maryland native.

Following Stout leaving for New York, Baltimore didn’t have a punter on the roster until Monday, where Luke Elzinga was signed after going undrafted from Oklahoma in the 2025 NFL Draft.

Elzinga’s lone experience in the professional ranks was a rookie minicamp invite from the Tennessee Titans last year. Due to his limited experience, and no other punter currently being on the roster, this gives Nwosu an opportunity to capitalize if he were given the opportunity.

Ravens General Manager Eric DeCosta has had his fair share of former Nittany Lions on the roster since he was hired in 2019, which includes Stout, defensive ends Adisa Isaac and Odafe Oweh, quarterback Trace McSorley and cornerback Amani Oruwariye. DeCosta’s daughter, Jane, graduated from Penn State last year.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Off the record | Concerned Carrie
author: Miss Behave I The Daily Collegian
description:
Editor’s Note: Off the record is a weekly column written by a third-year Collegian staffer writing under an alias. It aims to promote a space for honest conversations about love, sex and relationships. Those interested in sharing their experiences or submitting a topic are invited to do so through this anonymous form.

Dearest reader,

Well, it looks like we only have about three weeks left of the semester. It’s crazy how fast time flies by when you’re having fun and other things (get your head out of the gutter). I mean, avoiding responsibilities, rewatching comfort shows instead of doing assignments and somehow acquiring a new personality trait every time the weather hits 65 degrees.

I don’t know about you, but I’ve reached the point in the semester where my planner is more of a suggestion and my sleep schedule is hanging on by a very thin thread. But alas, we must persist.

As always, I want to thank you, my loyal, spicy little reader, for writing to me. Concerned Carrie is our host this week and I’m here to help her navigate the delicate balance between being a supportive bestie and a boyfriend critic.

Now, let’s talk about besties and boyfriends.

Friendship is meant to be a safe space. A place where honesty meets empathy and where someone can come as they are without judgment. The conversations? Raw, unfiltered, and in my case, probably incriminating enough that if they were ever recorded, my bestie and I would be serving some time behind bars.

But what happens when your best friend is in a relationship that you know isn’t right for them? Or you feel as though they deserve better than what they currently have.

First, I think it’s important to acknowledge that it’s tricky. It can be hard to know when your words cross the line between honest concern and unwanted interference. You don’t want to alienate your friend, yet staying silent can feel like watching someone you love settle for less than they deserve.

This is where something really important comes in. You have to know your friendship. Some friendships are built on a foundation of brutal honesty. Like “I love you but what you just did was incredibly disrespectful,” and it lands as love. Other friendships may need a softer approach, a little more cushioning, and a little less “he’s the human equivalent of a naked mole rat.”

At the end of the day, though, it's important to remember the goal isn’t to “fix” their relationship. It’s to make sure your friend knows you love and support them, no matter what.

There’s also this very persistent (and very annoying) myth that being a good friend means being a full-time cheerleader for every decision your friend makes. Don’t believe it, because real friendships are bigger than that. It’s about showing up, even when your opinion might be uncomfortable. Make sure your friend knows you have their back, even if they decide to stay with someone you don’t think is right for them.

And being honest doesn’t mean you have to criticize every move their partner makes. It can be as simple as expressing concern for your friend's well-being: “I see how much effort you put in, and I just want to make sure you’re happy and feel valued.”

Let them know you notice their needs are not being met and that you care enough to speak up. Also, supporting your friend doesn’t always have to mean supporting the relationship. It means supporting them as an individual. Their happiness, growth and their sense of self. You can dislike the boyfriend all you want and still show up with snacks, hugs and unwavering loyalty. Two things can be true.

The most important thing is that your friend feels safe in your honesty and unconditional support. Because here’s the thing, they might not listen to you. Not now, maybe not ever. Love and romantic feelings have a way of making people cling to potential rather than reality.

Your role isn’t to dictate or judge their choices. It’s to be the kind of friend who reminds them they’re loved, valued and never alone, no matter who they’re dating.

Honestly, relationships are messy, friendships are messy and boundaries can blur. But if your friend knows without a doubt that there’s genuine care and you’re coming from a place of love, then you’ve done your job. The rest is up to them.

They’re the driver of their own story, you’re just a passenger along for the ride. And you aren’t the one navigating with the GPS either. You have to trust your friend knows their direction and when to make the right turns. And if they don’t hit the brakes in time and crash … well, you’re there to help them get back up.

After all, a best friend has the power to heal a broken heart she didn’t break.

Yours truly,

Miss Behave

MORE COLUMNS

title: ‘It's like you've had a tail the entire time’ | Flock cameras on campus raise privacy, transparency concerns
author: Jonathon Chiu | The Daily Collegian
description:
Correction: A previous version of this article called Bob Smetana a DeFlock employee. People who work for the organization are volunteers, not employees. We've updated Smetana's title to reflect that. The Daily Collegian apologizes for this error.

Garin Roelofs and his friends often talk about mass surveillance, about how license plate readers across the country feed vehicle tracking data to police and private companies. For Roelofs, a first-year student in the Division of Undergraduate Studies, these conversations were abstract, until a few months after he arrived at Penn State — when he learned that he’d been passing this same technology every day on his way to class.

Penn State never announced when and where these cameras were installed. After the university denied his Right-To-Know requests for more information, Roefols began his own investigation. Now, his push to learn more about the devices has widened into a broader debate over campus surveillance and a lack of transparency from University Police & Public Safety (UPPS).

The cameras belong to Flock Safety, a technology company that develops camera, audio and investigative tools to help communities address public safety incidents, according to its website. The company is best known for its solar-powered license plate readers which use artificial intelligence to scan and catalog vehicles that pass by. At least 4,800 law enforcement agencies across the U.S. have used the readers since 2017, when the company was founded.

Flock’s interconnected network gives its users the ability to access data from cameras across the country – meaning any police department or other entity using Flock could look through Penn State’s own Flock camera footage. Penn State can do the same.

After searching through public records requests made to other law enforcement agencies, Roelofs found that Penn State has accessed 6,319 other Flock networks since Nov. 26, including the Town of Tonawanda Police Department in New York. A review of a New York Freedom of Information Law request for the department’s audit logs showed that Penn State had accessed their systems once during October and twice during November, citing testing purposes.

What we know — and what we don’t

According to DeFlock, an advocacy group monitoring the deployment of the cameras around the world, there are 24 reported Flock camera locations on Penn State’s campus. The Daily Collegian visited all reported locations to verify the presence of Flock cameras.

Penn State isn’t the only college campus where Flock cameras have been installed. At Temple University in Philadelphia, the cameras have helped the campus police department arrest suspects in several criminal cases, according to The Temple News. In August, the student newspaper reported that the university has an online portal detailing where data from campus cameras are being shared.

But Penn State’s agreement with Flock also comes as many law enforcement agencies are ending their contracts over privacy concerns. The deployment of Flock cameras nationwide has been met with controversy due to concerns from both the general public and activist groups, like the American Civil Liberties Union.

Jacqueline Sheader, UPPS’s information officer, said the Flock cameras are part of a “limited, time-bound pilot.” She said the cameras are located in positions along University Park’s perimeter that are consistent with UPPS Policy 7.12, which was put into effect on Aug. 27, 2025 and is not listed on the department’s website.

“The pilot supports defined law‑enforcement purposes, including stolen‑vehicle recovery, missing‑person cases and investigative leads,” Sheader said. “It is limited in scope and duration, with no contract or cost during the testing period.”

Sheader did not address the Collegian’s questions regarding when the pilot started and how long it will last, as well as specifics about the aforementioned policy and our request to view it. She also didn’t respond to questions about whether the cameras will be removed after the pilot ends or if their usage will be expanded.

She said access to the system is restricted to “trained, authorized UPPS personnel” and system usage also being logged and audited.

However, Penn State’s partial exemption from Pennsylvania’s Right-to-Know Law means that UPPS records, which would include the audit logs and license plate reader data collected from the cameras, are not available for public viewing.

Students, experts raise privacy concerns

Several local governments and law enforcement agencies across the U.S. have terminated their contracts with the company, often in response to concerns about potential data use by federal agencies such as Immigrations and Customs Enforcement (ICE), as reported by the San Francisco Chronicle and several ABC affiliates.

The company had planned to partner with Amazon’s Ring, which specializes in home security cameras, but ultimately canceled the deal following public backlash about privacy concerns. These concerns had been exacerbated by a Superbowl ad promoting Ring’s new AI-powered feature that could help locate lost dogs by scanning footage from neighborhood cameras.

Flock’s policy concerning the license plate readers allows the company to disclose data to law enforcement, government officials and third parties if they deem necessary. The policy also says the company uses the data to teach its AI model.

Ryan Faddis expressed his concerns about the cameras during a State College Borough Council meeting on Jan. 12, stating the council needs to protect the privacy of residents who drive by the cameras as some of them face public roadways like College Avenue and Atherton Street.

“This affects everybody in the surrounding area that is driving through those intersections, so that's why I went to (the council) first,” Faddis, a graduate student studying biochemistry, microbiology and molecular biology, said.

Faddis said he believes that the majority of people are ignoring Flock cameras on campus due to familiarity with traditional closed-circuit television cameras (CCTV), which have their feeds restricted from outside access.

“If you walk by a normal CCTV camera, you have the expectation that you are being recorded but that recording is not being transmitted somewhere where somebody on the other side of the country could access it. Whereas now these Flock cameras, because they're all connected, you almost have a police escort,” Faddis said. “If you're going on a road trip, they perfectly document your entire road trip path and it's like you've had a tail the entire time, and so I think it's important for people to realize that these aren't just your stereotypical or generic CCTV cameras.”

Faddis said he believes students should be aware of the cameras and is shocked that its use hasn’t been publicly disclosed by Penn State.

Roelofs, who created a website called The Panopticon to analyze campus surveillance, said pushing Penn State to remove the cameras will be a difficult process, but he believes it is possible.

“More than 30 localities dropped Flock after asking one question: Who else can see our data?” Roelofs said, citing an NPR article. “Penn State is harder to move than a city government. The state-related exemption blocks Pennsylvania Right-to-Know requests, so you cannot force disclosure. You have to go through the faculty senate, student government, public forums, media and the Board of Trustees.”

Sarah Hartman-Caverly, one of the co-creators of the Digital Shred Privacy Literacy Toolkit, said she and her colleague, Alexandria Chisholm, were made aware of Flock Safety following concerns its cameras could be used by law enforcement agencies to track the movements of women seeking abortions following the 2022 overturning of Roe v. Wade, according to reports from the Guardian and NBC Chicago. This prompted them both to begin investigating Flock.

She said the presence of Flock cameras contributes to an economy of surveillance capitalism, where an individual’s online activity is gathered by private companies for profit.

“As students, you obviously have some choice in which college and university you might attend … but once you choose Penn State University Park, all of these other choices downstream from there are made for you,” Hartman-Caverly said. “You have to use Microsoft 365 products. You have to use the Canvas learning management system. During COVID, you are uploading immunization records into a salesforce portal and, if you are going to be physically present on campus, you have to be subjected to Flock surveillance.”

Devices which fall under the term “luxury surveillance,” such as smartphones, wearable devices, dash cams and consumer surveillance devices such as Ring cameras, are also growing increasingly popular.

“These are all things that we've adopted into our lives because we perceive that they provide some kind of benefit or some kind of connection to essential goods and services but they come bundled with all of these surveillance capitalism practices and technologies,” Hartman-Caverly said.

While being surveilled in public is a foregone conclusion, DeFlock volunteer Bob Smetana said this doesn’t give companies such as Flock Safety permission to build a profile on someone based on the data gained from tracking someone’s movements and interactions.

For Smetana, it’s important that the public has the ability to monitor how their information is being accessed and used by law enforcement agencies.

“If the public can tell that their license plate has been searched 400 times by the same officer, that's not an issue. That's what accountability looks like,” Smetana said. “If we don't know that, what good are the audit logs?”

MORE INVESTIGATIONS

title: UPUA swears in Hogan, Lopez at 21st general assembly's first meeting
author: Kiera Ginn | The Daily Collegian
description:
The University Park Undergraduate Association (UPUA) swore in President Kat Hogan and Vice President Maria Lopez on Wednesday during the 21st general assembly’s first meeting.

Ayden Meise was elected speaker and James Dugary was elected as vice speaker.

Chairs for each of UPUA’s committees were also elected. Anthony Khuc was elected as chair of Academic Affairs; David Washabaugh as chair of Campus Operations; Avery Landis as chair of Government and Community Relations; Megan Soto-Benitez as chair of Justice and Equity; and Chase Allen as chair of Student Engagement.

Alison Tawil was sworn in as finance director. Shreya Budakoti was sworn in as chief of staff and Henry Wagner as secretary.

These confirmations come one week after UPUA’s executive election on April 1, where Hogan and Lopez won with 1,853 votes.

MORE CAMPUS CONTENT

title: No. 21 Penn State women’s lacrosse set to face Oregon
author: Aeryn Ajot | For the Collegian
description:
Last weekend, Penn State ended its losing streak and beat Ohio State, 18-9, moving them up in the polls to No. 21.

The Ohio State game proved to be essential to helping the Nittany Lions 9-3 losing streak. Now 10-3, Happy Valley’s record is recovering and the team is proving to be fierce competitors

Unlike the Nittany Lions, the Ducks have continued to accumulate a five-week losing streak and have been struggling throughout the 2026 season, with an overall record of 6-7.

Both having fought mutual opponents, with varying results, from the outside, his game seems to be an easy sweep for the blue and white. However, with the Ducks, you never know who will make the final blow.

Shared opponents

In comparison to other schools, the first competition against Oregon was last year, with a 19-10 win for the blue and white. With a lack of history, there is little to compare, but they have shared many opponents this season.

During the 2026 season, both schools have competed against SDSU, Maryland, Ohio State and Michigan.

When going down to which competitors they have each lost and won two, a prediction of this Thursday game can easily be made.

In order of who Penn State competed first against, both teams first matched up against SDSU. Penn State made an easy victory with an 18-6 win on Aztec territory, while Oregon had to fight for the 12-10 win.

Next, both teams competed against No. 6 Michigan in late March. The Nittany Lions and Ducks fell against the Wolverines, with Penn State losing 6-11 and Oregon losing 16-3.

No. 3 Maryland, once again, hurt both teams and added a loss to their records; with the blue and white narrowly losing with a score of 13-14, while the Ducks lost dramatically with a score of 7-15.

No. 25 Ohio State proved to be the team that separated Penn State and Oregon from identical results. In these matchups, the Nittany Lions proved to have ease against a lower-ranked competitor, with an easy 18-9 victory over the Buckeyes last weekend. While the Ducks had a tragic fate of a 2-20 loss in Columbus last week.

In games where Penn State had a wide margin of success against its competitors, like against SDSU and Ohio State, Oregon differed. Against SDSU, they won by a mere couple of goals and against Ohio State, it suffered a devastating loss by over 15 points.

Similarly, against Maryland, a high-ranked opponent, both teams lost but by severely different margins. Penn State lost by a single point in a tough matchup, while Maryland doubled the points against them in the match against Oregon, 7-15.

Although both teams had similar results, the scores are proof in the pudding that the game this Thursday will fall in favor of Happy Valley.

Thursday fate

Penn State has finally lost its losing streak and regained momentum since the Ohio State game. They’re ready to take on more schools and help reestablish themselves as a top national competitor.

Predictions show that Penn State is sitting pretty for Thursday, but time will only tell if Oregon has a few tricks up its sleeves.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball prepares for series against Ohio State
author: Ava Aguiar | The Daily Collegian
description:
Coming off an electric dollar dog night win against Fairleigh Dickinson, Penn State will hit the road to take on its historic rival, Ohio State, in a three-game series matchup this weekend.

The Nittany Lions faced tough competition this past weekend against a top-three Big Ten team, Nebraska, and were swept in the series. However, besides a dominant win by the Cornhuskers in game one, the blue and white held its own, losing only by one run in Game 2, 8-7 and by two in the final game to go 8-6.

The team also had a strong performance in its game last night, winning 11-5 against FDU. Players like Bryce Molinaro, Michael Anderson and Spencer Barnett have consistently been the top offensive contributors for the team. They will play a key part in their upcoming games against the Buckeyes.

The two teams haven’t faced off since 2023, but have both had an even number of wins over the other over the last five years. Penn State won its last game 15-3 at home in Medlar Field at Lubrano Park.

Going into the weekend, tensions are high as Ohio State sits at 17-14 overall and 7-5 in conference play, sitting near the top of the list, whereas Penn State is one spot from being at the bottom. Ohio State comes in with a well-rounded offensive team, with most players batting averages coming in the .300 range or just under, along with high slugging percentages.

Senior Buckeye Henry Kaczmar will be one to look out for with a .333 batting average and a .558 slugging average, whereas Penn State relies on its top hitters to put runs on the board.

Penn State’s pitching had a good night last night with Frankie Sanchez only letting up one run on one hit, one walk and a strikeout before the team had six strikeouts in the final five innings of the game before defeating FDU.

If Penn State wants to have a chance in these games against Ohio State’s offense, pitchers like Sanchez and Ethan Bauerschmidt are going to have to be ready to have a good day every day to pose a threat to the team’s consistent hitters.

Even though their season was off to a rocky start, the team has been showing strong performances against big teams like Nebraska and Illinois, which could propel them to bring a win back to Happy Valley.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State track and field's Ajani Dwyer earns Big Ten honor
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
For the second time in a week, Penn State's Ajani Dwyer is making headlines.

Dwyer was named the Big Ten Men's Track Athlete of the Week on Wednesday after his performance at the Pepsi Florida Relays.

BACK-TO-BACK🤯🔗 https://t.co/7xyHHA3wXK#WeAre | #PSUTF pic.twitter.com/Ai4E98y73i— Penn State Track & Field/Cross Country (@PennStateTFXC) April 8, 2026

The Sewell, New Jersey, native ran a record-breaking 10.06 seconds in the 100-meter on Friday, setting the top mark in the NCAA.

A week prior, he ran an unofficial school record at the Knights Invite, running 9.96 seconds in the 100m.

MORE TRACK AND FIELD COVERAGE

title: Benkovic Building remains partially closed after 'small' fire Wednesday morning
author: Grace Levy | The Daily Collegian
description:
Alpha Fire Company responded to a “small, contained” fire on the second floor of the Benkovic Building on Wednesday morning, UPPS' Public Information Officer Jacqueline Sheader confirmed in an email.

She said the fire occurred in a laboratory hood, which is a ventilated space that protects people from chemical fumes, vapors and particulate matter generated by experiments.

The second floor and other areas of the building remain closed, but it has been partially re-opened for use.

"There were no injuries and no ongoing safety concerns," Sheader said.

The Benkovic Building, previously known as the Chemistry Building, is home to over 85 chemistry research labs and the Nuclear Magnetic Resonance Facility.

MORE CAMPUS CONTENT

title: Penn State football draft profile | WR Liam Clifford's shot in NFL hinges on versatility
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
For the first time since the 2017 season, Penn State won’t have a Clifford brother on the team as wide receiver Liam Clifford is headed for the next step of his career.

Clifford declared for the 2026 NFL draft on Jan. 2 following a five-year stint as a Nittany Lion, but he could have long-shot odds to land an undrafted free agent deal.

Nevertheless, he participated at Penn State Pro Day, where his older brother Sean Clifford, who played quarterback in Happy Valley from 2017-22, was there to support him. Following his workout, he explained how he believes NFL teams are evaluating him.

“Overall, just being a guy ever since I've been at Penn State, being a guy who can plug in anywhere at receiver, playing all four positions across the board, outside in those slots,” Clifford said. “At the end of the year, even switching the quarterback and being able to kind of be a next man up wherever I need to be. Even on special teams, too.”

Metrics

Height: 6-foot-1

Weight: 200 pounds

Bench press: 19 reps

Vertical jump: 33 inches

Broad jump: 9-foot-3

40-yard dash: 4.57 seconds

Pro shuttle: 4.36 seconds

3-cone drill: 7.29 seconds

Projections

Projected draft round: Undrafted

Player comparison: Trey Palmer

Scouting report

Clifford came to Penn State as a 247Sports composite 3-star in 2021, redshirting his freshman year. He saw more playing time as his career went on, but he never churned out much production, finishing with 42 catches for 530 yards and a touchdown across 52 games played.

The Cincinnati native saw 367 snaps in the slot and 137 snaps out wide throughout his career, according to PFF. His most notable performance came in the 2024 season, when he had three catches for 107 yards in a win against UCLA.

Clifford entered the 2025 season as one of the veterans in the wide receiver group at Penn State, but he ended with three catches for 25 yards. Additionally, his season took an unexpected turn when he was thrown into the quarterback depth chart after multiple injuries in the room.

Clifford had two passing attempts, and both were incomplete. With the team’s need for him as a backup quarterback, he was only used sparingly at his original position.

Best team fit: San Francisco 49ers

Due to how his college career went, there likely won’t be too many offers on the table from NFL teams for Clifford, if any. However, if there’s one team that could be a fit for Clifford, it’s the San Francisco 49ers.

Head coach Kyle Shanahan prioritizes a run-first offense, so the 49ers’ wide receivers have to be willing to run block. With Clifford’s message at pro day highlighting his ability to be a next man up at wherever he’s needed, he could be a solid fit in Shanahan’s scheme.

With his lack of getting mentioned in various mock drafts, it seems like it’s going to be an uphill battle for Clifford to land a spot on a practice squad. But if he were to get an opportunity with the 49ers, his position versatility could be enough to secure a job.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State women's volleyball promotes coaches Brian Toron, Megan Hodge Easy to associate head coaches
author: Liana Tiamzon | For the Collegian
description:
Penn State has elevated its staff, announcing two new staff promotions.

Brian Toron and Megan Hodge Easy have been promoted to associate head coach roles, the program announced. Toron was also named recruiting coordinator, holding two roles on the staff.

Happy to announce that both Brian Toron and Megan Hodge Easy have been elevated to Associate Head Coach positions! 📰 https://t.co/6ufu5ZBbC4#WeAre pic.twitter.com/Adz4NJvCF0— Penn State Women’s Volleyball (@PennStateVBALL) April 8, 2026

Both promotions came after they played key roles in the Nittany Lions’ recent conference success, including a national championship season in 2024.

Hodge Easy, known to be one of the most decorated players in program history, won three national titles as a student athlete from 2007 to 2009. Since joining the coaching staff she’s helped the blue and white remain among the nation's top teams, as well as contributing to its latest championship run.

Toron has been a hidden weapon in the team's offensive development and recruiting efforts. He assisted Penn State to a dominant championship season while working closely with the program's setters and attack system.

Their new promoted jobs as associate head coach title reflect increased leadership and responsibilities, including recruiting and overall program direction.

The Nittany Lions’ move reinforces its commitment to retaining key contributors following its championship success.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State notebook | coach Clarisa Crowell talks upcoming Northwestern series, teams consistency
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State has been on a roll as of late, winning its last six Big Ten games. The Nittany Lions welcome Northwestern this weekend with a chance to continue their strong momentum.

Coach Clarisa Crowell spoke to the media on Wednesday, discussing the team's upcoming series and what she’s seen from her squad as of late.

Scouting the Wildcats

Northwestern is 17-18 with a 5-7 Big Ten record, but these numbers are deceiving. The Wildcats had a strong nonconference schedule and five of their conference losses are to teams that are ranked nationally.

“I don't think their numbers are indicative of how good of a team that they are, because they've played a really tough schedule,” Crowell said. “If you look at all of their nonconference games, I mean, they're competing against the best of the best.”

Northwestern is highlighted by a strong arm in the circle, freshman Marina Mason. The Brentwood, Tennessee, native ranks top-10 in the conference in both ERA and strikeouts.

“I watch her throw, and she's a pretty versatile pitcher, like she can throw up, she can throw down, she spins the ball really well, and she has an effective off speed pitch,” Crowell said. “When you have a pitcher that can throw just vertically, both up and down, and an off speed, that's a hard pitcher, but she's averaging at least one strike out a game.”

The Wildcats have a veteran lineup and they’re defined by more than just pitching. The blue and white are prepped to hit on all cylinders against a tough Northwestern squad.

“They're going to be a team that they're going to hit for power and they got some lefty slappers in the lineup as well. They're going to run well, they're going to be athletic, so they're going to be a very good team,” Crowell said. “We're going to have to execute at a high level to take down Northwestern because when you have a good pitcher and you can swing it, those teams can be tough to beat.”

Consistency and flow

After getting off to a slow start in conference play, Penn State has been on a roll its last two Big Ten series, Things are beginning to flow nicely for the Nittany Lions and they remain consistent game-by-game, win or lose.

“I think that one of the best things about our group is they're just, they're very consistent in their play, regardless of result,” Crowell said. “That 1-0 mentality, it's just embedded in our kids, so whether we win, whether we lose, the mindset is always, how do we go 1-0 today.”

Take for example, before its series against Maryland, Penn State dropped a heartbreaking 8-7 loss to Pittsburgh. The Nittany Lions bounced back in a strong way to sweep the Terrapins.

“I think, if anything, when we don't go 1-0, like last Tuesday was a tough game for us against Pittsburgh, but I think it just motivates them and fuels them to be even better,” Crowell said. “Our kids, overall, do a really good job of just being consistent with how they handle any outcome, and I do think failure just motivates them to be even better.”

Fueled by freshmen

One of the biggest stories of Penn State’s 2026 season has been the ability for its rookies to perform at a high level.

“They've been a big part of our success and first and foremost, our freshman class, they're not just getting the job done on the field, but they're exceptional young women,” Crowell said. “Those kids, before they set foot on campus, were bought into Penn State, and what we're doing here, and I think when you have that buy in piece, it just helps with production out on the field.”

Allison Oneacre has been one of the team's most consistent bats as well as one of the top hitters in the Big Ten. Mackenzie Duncan is the Nittany Lions ERA and strikeout leader, and Brevyn Kellepouris has made strides in the circle as well.

“They've been great for the culture of our program, and obviously they're having a tremendous impact on the field,” Crowell said. “In big ways, small ways, on the field, off the field, they've certainly been a big part of our team this year.”

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s basketball guard Dominick Stewart enters transfer portal
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
A second-year Penn State player is departing the program.

Guard Dominick Stewart will enter the transfer portal with two years of eligibility remaining, according to Sam Kayser of League Ready.

NEWS: Penn State guard Dominick Stewart will enter the transfer portal, he told @LeagueRDY.The 6-foot-5 guard out of Ocean Springs, Mississippi spent the last two seasons at Penn State. Started 24 out of 32 games this season.Averaged 6.6PPG, 2.2RPG and 1.6APG in 2025-26. pic.twitter.com/yDipqHg0nv— Sam Kayser (@KayserHoops) April 8, 2026

After being a depth piece on the 2024-25 team, Stewart became a consistent starter for Penn State this season. He averaged 6.6 points, 2.2 rebounds and 1.6 assists per game across 32 appearances, including 24 starts.

As a defender, Stewart was key in containing Iowa guard Bennett Stirtz during a 71-69 win over the Hawkeyes on Feb. 28, which prompted praise from Mike Rhoades.

“He's our most physical perimeter defender, and with his length and how he could cover ground,” Rhoades said on March 2. “With the experience and getting all the reps guarding good players, he's getting better and better guarding and defending and understanding how to guard different players, honoring the scouting report.”

Offensively, Stewart is regarded as a shooter, and he shot 34.8% from 3-point range this season. His best performance came in a win over New Haven in November, shooting 6-of-8 from 3-point range to finish with a career-high 18 points.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s basketball standout Kayden Mingo enters transfer portal
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
The cornerstone of Penn State’s roster is leaving the team.

Guard Kayden Mingo has entered the transfer portal, according to Jonathan Givony of Draft Express, with three seasons of eligibility remaining.

NEWS: Penn State freshman Kayden Mingo will enter the transfer portal, his agency Excel Sports tells DraftExpress. Mingo started every game in the Big Ten, averaging 13.7 points, 4.3 assists, and 2.1 steals. Will be a top PG target nationally. pic.twitter.com/aNJwgBU265— Jonathan Givony (@DraftExpress) April 8, 2026

Mingo was the Nittany Lions’ starting point guard this season, averaging 13.7 points, 3.5 rebounds, 4.3 assists and 2.1 steals per game with 47.7% shooting from the field and 24.4% shooting from 3-point range across 28 appearances.

Mingo’s younger brother, Dylan Mingo, is a 5-star prospect in the 2026 class and committed to UNC. He gave his reaction to his brother choosing the Tar Heels earlier this year.

“I'm happy for my brother and just super, super happy for him,” Mingo said on Feb. 18. “We're a blessed family to have all three of us (brothers) going Division I, and I'm very glad he made his decision. I'm happy that he's happy with his decision.”

Throughout the 2025-26 season, Mingo flashed his potential with Penn State. He won Big Ten Freshman of the Week on Dec. 1 after logging eight steals against Sacred Heart, and he’s had other performances like a season-high 24 points versus Oregon.

Mingo has a physical playstyle that drives into the paint to score baskets. He drained the game-winning bucket in a 77-75 win over Minnesota on Feb. 1 after driving into the paint.

The Farmingdale, New York, native is Penn State’s highest-rated recruit of all time. He was a 247Sports composite 4-star and the No. 37 player nationally in the 2025 class.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: 5 books Penn State graduates should read
author: Alli Satterfield | The Daily Collegian
description:
Lots of thoughts are swirling around in the minds of Penn State’s graduating class as their time at Penn State draws near. With so many questions to be answered — where you’ll work, where you’ll live — it’s hard to focus on anything else in the time leading up to graduation.

But if you have some free time post-graduation, here are some reading recommendations to help you feel more prepared or comforted in this new phase of your life.

1. “You Are a Badass: How to Stop Doubting Your Greatness and Start Living an Awesome Life” by Jen Sincero

This is a self-help book, and I’m aware this simple fact may already be turning some readers away. However, I ignored my self-help-book-ick and actually really enjoyed this.

Sincero has an unapologetic writing style that’s honest and empowering. Throughout the book, she emphasizes things you can actually do to help change your mindset, and I found that super helpful.

“You Are a Badass” is one of the things that helped build my self-confidence in my personal life and career, and it’s great for recent graduates. It can help you design the life that you want to live yourself.

2. “Retire Before Mom and Dad: The Simple Numbers Behind a Lifetime of Financial Freedom” by Rob Berger

Though I haven’t read this book yet, my dad raves about it. It’s a guidebook for financial literacy and independence, focused on efficiently building wealth. It wouldn’t necessarily be my cup of tea (although my father will probably force me to read it eventually), but it offers important lessons in budgeting and saving.

As you move into the workforce, you’ll probably have a larger paycheck than you’re used to seeing. This book provides advice on how to handle that, a lesson worth learning for recent graduates.

3. “Beautiful World, Where Are You” by Sally Rooney

Graduation is honestly a time filled with expectations. You expect to get your diploma, land a job, move to a new city and, ultimately, be successful. But sometimes we don’t meet our own expectations because of life’s curveballs, and that’s okay.

That’s the lesson “Beautiful World, Where Are You” taught me. It follows a few young people navigating their lives and relationships. Even though it’s a work of fiction, these characters feel real, and they’re reminders that you have the authority to create the life you want.

It’s a great reminder for everyone, but it may be especially necessary if you’re feeling behind as graduation approaches.

4. “The Alchemist” by Paulo Coelho

This novel chronicles the journey of a young man looking for his calling. I think it’s a great read for anyone looking for purpose, or who thinks they’ve already found what they’re meant to do. It’s a reassuring story that the universe is orchestrating things well.

For me, it reiterates the idea that everything is always working out. Recent grads may not feel like that’s a true statement, so this novel may be a necessary reminder.

5. “Tuesdays with Morrie: An Old Man, a Young Man, and Life’s Greatest Lesson” by Mitch Albom

While it’s a tear-jerker, this memoir is a lesson on not taking any day or experience for granted, and to recognize how beautiful life can be.

It perfectly intertwines the lives of a mentor and former student. Any reader becomes one of Morrie’s students in recognizing that learning doesn’t stop after you toss your cap.

Congratulations on all of your accomplishments and happy reading.

MORE BLOGS

title: Column | Thread of fates
author: Annelise Hanson | The Daily Collegian
description:
I’ve always been aware of endings in time. I worry about the future in the present while trying to make sense of the past. With that came the hyperawareness of mortality and my mourning of future moments. I try to plan my years in advance so I’m careful not to look back in regret for not trying something new.

Perhaps it’s the Fates or Morai with their unseen hands, older than the cosmos, with more power than the gods; spinning, measuring, cutting my thread of life as they see fit. Then what’s life but the illusion of control?

One night this question sank into my brain while I was on veering out the Acropolis glowing against a dark sky on my balcony, drunk on melancholy, each moment existing on a timeline I will never return to.

I cannot be everywhere I want to be all at once. This time last week, I was staring at the Eiffel Tower in Paris, and the week before I was at a techno club in Germany. A week before that, I was examining the night sky in the Sahara desert.

Night is when I become closest to that truth, and the thread of my life itself. Carrying the unknown with me in my marrow, fragments of myself dissolving into each place, only the feeling of being alive within them.

I become acutely aware that I cannot be everywhere I long to be, cannot hold onto every version of myself that exists in different places, different moments. Everything is fleeting. Everything is already, in some sense, gone. I can’t just transport to the places I love on a whim.

There’s a moment, sometimes, when I wake from sleep but haven’t fully returned to myself. A liminal space, half-dream, half-consciousness, where I feel my heartbeat with startling clarity. In that space, I’m deeply aware that everything I am — every thought, every memory, every desire — exists within something as fragile as bone and breath. That all of my being fits within a skull and a ribcage.

Your mind betrays you when you're young, while your body is in prime condition. Once you’re old, your body betrays you and your mind is the wiser. I plan my life years in advance because I wish to outmaneuver impermanence, as if careful intention might keep me from missing the moments that matter most.

Maybe that’s why I’ve always loved Athena. She was born not from the body, but from the mind, emerging fully formed from the head of Zeus, after a splitting headache where he asked Hephaestus to open his skull to relieve the pressure.

There’s something profound in that image, a goddess not yet known, possessing wisdom that cannot be suppressed even by the most powerful god, life forcing itself into existence despite prophecy.

To be born from inevitability and to arrive ready, armored, alive. Athena became the protector of Athens, a city that still stands as proof that ideas are immortal, that something of us can endure.

And yet even that endurance isn't infinite. The Fates still hold the thread. Which brings me to xenia, the ancient Greek belief that any stranger could be a god in disguise, that hospitality was a moral obligation. To welcome the unknown was to honor the divine. To reject it was to risk wrath.

Now, I see our humanity ending at the borders of our nation. Families separated, strangers turned away, people reduced to where they are from instead of who they are.

Lest they be angels or gods in disguise, if they arrived today, unrecognized, would we offer warmth, or would we deny them entry?
