Can you be the master of your fate, or is it in the hands of the fates during the brief moment of existence?

I’m reminded of a monologue from the 2004 movie “Troy:” “I'll tell you a secret. Something they don't teach you in your temple. The gods envy us. They envy us because we're mortal, because any moment might be our last. Everything is more beautiful because we are doomed. You will never be lovelier than you are now. We will never be here again.”

Mortality is the very condition that gives human life its beauty, urgency and meaning, and it’s something even the gods, in their endless existence, can never experience. Athens, in a way, gave me peace that life is a dance, and I must not grieve the past or obsessively plan the future.

Life comes from you.

MORE COLUMNS

title: How to stay focused before ‘summer fever’ hits
author: Lexi Cannan | The Daily Collegian
description:
The second it hits 60 degrees at Penn State, it’s over. Mentally, you’re no longer a student — you’re just a person who deserves to sit outside and “reset” for six hours.

Suddenly, everything feels optional. Going to class is a maybe, homework can wait and sitting on the HUB-Robeson Center lawn doing absolutely nothing becomes your top priority. You open Canvas once, close it immediately and somehow convince yourself that the mentality of “being outside is good for you” counts as productivity.

Here’s how to stay focused before “summer fever,” or the nerves before summer break, completely take over your life.

Accept that you’re not built for this weather

You’re not going to be your most productive self when it’s sunny and everyone you know is outside — it’s just facts. No one’s grinding a 10-page paper while their friends are lying in the grass listening to music.

You’re not going to magically get everything done, so lower your expectations. Tell yourself you’ll do one assignment, maybe two if you’re feeling brave. Even opening your laptop and actually starting is a win at this point.

Once you start, it’s weirdly easier to keep going. Next thing you know, you’ve done way more than you planned.

Stop lying to yourself about outdoor studying

You’re not studying outside — you’re people-watching with a laptop open. You go out there with good intentions, but within 10 minutes your screen is too bright, your music is too distracting and now you’re fully invested in random strangers’ conversations.

If you actually need to get work done, go inside and romanticize it if you have to. Pretend you’re in your “productive era,” light a candle, buy a coffee — anything to make it feel less painful.

Do the easiest thing first

When your brain is already halfway to summer break, the last thing you want to do is start something hard — opening a big assignment feels like emotional damage.

Start with the easiest thing you have, something quick you can finish fast without thinking too hard.

It gives you a small win, and honestly, that’s all it takes. You finish one thing and suddenly you’re like, “Wait, I can do this.” Now, you’re answering emails, finishing another assignment, maybe even feeling productive for the first time all week.

From there, you’re kind of on a roll, or at least it feels like it, which counts.

Give yourself a scheduled “outside break”

If you try to ignore how nice it is out, you’ll just end up staring at your screen and thinking about being outside.

Instead, plan for it. Tell yourself, “If I finish this, I can go outside,” and suddenly you’re not doing homework — you’re earning outdoor time like it’s a reward system.

It actually works, because you start typing faster, answering things quicker and becoming weirdly efficient just to go sit outside and do absolutely nothing.

Once you finally do go outside, it feels better because you’re not sitting there half-stressed and half-guilty — you’re just vibing, knowing you at least did something.

Put your phone in another room

You don’t need to check the weather app again — it’s still nice out. Nothing has changed in the last three minutes.

Your phone is the biggest distraction, especially when all you see are people posting outside. One quick check turns into 20 minutes of scrolling, and suddenly you’re watching someone you barely know play spikeball on the HUB lawn like it’s your full-time job.

If your phone is next to you, you’re going to pick it up, so put it in another room — not face down or on silent — but actually out of reach. It’s painful at first and you’ll definitely think about it, maybe even get up to check it, but once it’s gone, you start focusing because there’s nothing else to do. Just like that, you’ve done more work in 30 minutes than you did in the last three hours outside.

Set a time limit and actually stick to it

You don’t need to sit there for hours pretending to get things done. That’s how you end up staring at your screen, typing one sentence and needing a break.

Give yourself a set amount of time, like 30 or 45 minutes, and just lock in. It feels way less painful when you know it’s not forever.

Once the timer goes off, you’re free. Go outside, check your phone, stare at nothing — whatever you want. It turns your work into something you can survive instead of something that ruins your day.

Half the time, you’ll keep going anyway because you’re already there, which is honestly shocking but we’ll take it.

MORE BLOGS

title: Satire | How to waste time before scheduling classes at midnight
author: Talia Rittenhouse | The Daily Collegian
description:
Everyone’s most dreaded time of year has returned, as students need to schedule courses for the upcoming fall semester.

The time ticks more slowly than usual while waiting for midnight to arrive on your scheduled enrollment date. Students stay up late, biting their fingernails, nervous to see if they’ll get their desired classes.

Well isn’t that so exciting? Waiting in anxiety and fear of not getting your perfectly curated schedule — my dream date. That being said, let’s discuss some more time-consuming activities to do instead of picking at our nails while wasting time away, leading up to midnight.

Deep clean your entire room

Trust me, you’ve got time, and I’m sure your room could use some extra help as the semester comes to a close.

So, it’s time to clean — and I’m talking manually picking pieces of hair and dust out of your carpet, kind of clean. To waste even more time, try picking out the dust particles with tweezers. That’ll surely speed up the time for you.

You can fold every single piece of clothing you own twice and still have time to do more.

So after folding your laundry, you should organize each drawer in your room, read every card you’ve ever received, test out old hand lotions and rearrange your furniture.

But still, you’ll have more time.

Run a 5k

So now, at 10 p.m. it’s the perfect time to run a 5k.

Go tie up your sneakers, put on a workout outfit and get on trekking.

A 5k may not even be long enough, maybe a 10k could do the trick, as you have many hours to wait and stress about your schedule.

In all seriousness, a workout or a run would be helpful, since it releases endorphins and eliminates stress, so this may actually be the cheat code to enrollment day.

Recreate your schedule

As the hours get later and later, you lose hope — so it’s time to recreate your entire schedule and your future life, for that matter.

What if the “what-if report” has failed you, or the schedule builder or every other student at Penn State has filled the seats in your selected courses? This is everyone’s worst nightmare.

As this impending doom comes upon you, try to calm yourself by going in circles, looking through the available class options and adding more to your enrollment cart. You've got to prepare your backup plan.

Now, this may be stressful but it’s necessary, and honestly, what’s some more stress on top of the nervousness you’re already feeling?

Read the dictionary

If none of these are resonating with you, maybe some reading of the English dictionary will help. You have many words to read through and a lot of time to kill, so you can run out the clock and gain some new vocabulary — it’s a win-win.

If you already read through the dictionary last semester when scheduling your current classes, then it’s time to move on to another language — try French, Spanish or German.

If you’re still sitting with a few hours to go, re-read these pages out loud to your roommates. I’m sure they’ll be eager to share this newfound knowledge with you in the late hours of the night.

Doomscroll on LinkedIn

Why not stress yourself out more and search for internships or jobs on LinkedIn?

Sounds like the best time, right? You can just add more stress to what you’re already experiencing. But, hey, at least you won’t have to stress out two times in the same week about these topics.

So, while you wait to solidify your course schedule, let’s work on solidifying your entire future. The internship that brings you to your job, or the job that brings you to money — this sounds so relaxing.

Well, sorry for all of that excitement, but I’m sure these will be your number one picks for killing time. Good luck with scheduling classes!

MORE BLOGS

title: ‘We need to be like Marie’ | Marie-Eloise Leclair turns heads in successful Penn State track season
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
As senior Marie-Eloise Leclair crossed the finish line for the 100-meters at the Knights Challenge, the time of 11.08 seconds flashed across the scoreboard.

While the time would’ve warranted a school record, a +4.8 m/s wind advantage nullified the time from record consideration. However, it was a step in the right direction for Leclair in her first race as a Nittany Lion.

“It was just a pat on the back to be like, ‘Okay, I’m in the right place,’” Leclair told The Daily Collegian. “In terms of excitement, because it’s so early, it wasn’t what I was expecting.”

A little over a year ago, Leclair was competing in Division II at Simon Fraser University in British Columbia, Canada. Not only did she have the opportunity to become one of the greatest athletes in Division II, but she also represented her country on the world stage.

Leclair competed at the Paris 2024 Olympic Games in the women’s 4x100m relay for Canada, helping her squad to finish with a time of 42.50 seconds.

“She’s been on our radar for a few years now,” Glenroy Gilbert, the head coach of the Canadian Olympic track and field team, told the Collegian. “Her relay skills have really come into terms of being an athlete that can run the straightaway, run the curve and take and pass the baton with her left hand, so she’s very valuable.”

Just a few months after three top-five finishes at the NCAA Division II Indoor Track and Field Championships, Leclair began exploring the transfer portal for her final season.

“After four years, even though I was doing really well, I felt I had taken everything I could from this environment and was starting to outgrow it a little bit,” Leclair said. “When (Brandon Rizzo) accepted to have a call, I thought I could use it as practice because Penn State wasn’t really on my radar.”

Despite Leclair’s early hesitance about Happy Valley, her and Rizzo instantly clicked, and she moved to Penn State to wrap up her collegiate career.

“We want the same things, and Marie emphasizes how much she wants it,” Rizzo told the Collegian. “Her mindset is just different, and I think it’s a beautiful thing that we just clicked together so well. Our goals are the same, and we really want to win badly.”

While Leclair didn’t compete in the blue and white until the outdoor season in March 2026, she headed back to the world stage shortly after arriving at Penn State, where she helped her 4x100m squad set a national record in 42.38 seconds at the World Athletics Championships.

“I remember when I made my first national team, everyone was wondering what (Simon Fraser) was,” Leclair said. “I was like, ‘We’re both here, so whoever was talking trash to me, we’re on the same national team at the same meet.”

While Leclair didn’t compete for Penn State during the indoor season, she had the opportunity to run unattached and compete in the same meets as the Nittany Lions.

There, she ran several top marks that would’ve been school records if she was competing for Penn State, including a 7.34 in the 60-meter at the Penn State National Open. While she experienced this success, her training was behind due to the world championships.

“Believe it or not, watching her run 7.32, I thought we were ahead of the curve and thought it was amazing,” Rizzo said.

As Leclair continued her training and grew even more during the indoor season, Rizzo was excited about her potential when she’d eventually take the track at Penn State.

“The thing that was most exciting about Marie was her mindset,” Rizzo said. “It’s just her goals and the way she was taught; she was just extremely confident in what she said.”

Nearly two years since her last outdoor collegiate race at the 2024 championships, Leclair took the track for the UCF Knights Invite, setting two of the top marks in the NCAA this season.

Leclair took first in both the 100m and 200m, running times of 11.08 seconds and 22.75 seconds. While both were ruled wind-aided and ineligible for record consideration, her potential was beginning to culminate after just one meet.

“We needed to get her over that hump of breaking 23 (seconds),” Rizzo said. “Now that we’re beyond that, it’s time to see how far the rabbit hole goes.”

One week later, Leclair competed again in Florida at the Pepsi Florida Relays, setting the No. 4 mark in school history in the 100m and 200m with times of 11.56 seconds and 23.14 seconds.

“She’s an open book, and we’re only scratching the surface in terms of what I think she’s capable of,” Gilbert said.

As Leclair is still new to her Penn State debut, she’s become a role model for other athletes on the track due to her recent run to fame.

“Leading by example is what elevates the other ladies,” Rizzo said. “They say, ‘If we want to taste greatness, we need to be like Marie.’”

While Leclair only has eligibility for the 2026 outdoor season, she’s hoping she can leave a big mark on Happy Valley during such a short time.

“A school record would be nice, considering I’ve kind of been flirting with the times around there,” Leclair said. “If I can just leave a mark and be remembered as a Penn State great just for the impact I had on people, that would still mean a lot.”

Even after Penn State, Leclair hopes to continue her racing, including staying in the United States for the upcoming Olympic Games in Los Angeles.

“Part of my decision to come to Penn State was also working toward how I set up the cycle for the next Olympics,” Leclair said. “If I come to the point where I can qualify individually, that would be great.”

While Leclair has thought about what comes next after her short Penn State season, she and Rizzo’s main focus is finishing off this season on a strong note.

“It’s about how far in this season we can get, still keeping the smile, the positivity and the confidence,” Rizzo said. “Then she can go out there, race against the best, and hopefully come out on top.”

MORE SPORTS COVERAGE

title: ‘It's an honor to have this role’ | Clarisa Crowell reflects on leading Penn State softball
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
One of the longest-tenured coaches at Penn State steps onto the dirt every spring and leads a team full of women to constant victories. The Nittany Lions’ softball coach, Clarisa Crowell, has found success in recent years, helping bring the program to higher levels over the past three seasons.

Crowell has been at the helm since 2020 and has had three 30-win seasons in her five completed campaigns. Most recently, in 2024, Crowell led the blue and white to its first NCAA Regional appearance since 2011. With the program, she’s posted 150 wins and a 0.535 win percentage, both of which are the third most in program history.

“I am very fortunate to have this opportunity here at Penn State, and to, you know, to be the head coach here for our softball program,” Crowell said. “There's been a lot of women in all sports, in all really areas of life, that have allowed women like me to be able to be in this type of leadership position here at Penn State.”

In addition to Crowell, 10 other Nittany Lions’ athletic teams are led by a female head coach.

“I love Penn State because when you look at not only our athletic department, but when you look at our school as a whole, there are so many strong women that are in power,” Crowell said. “I think it says a lot just about Penn State just embracing female leadership and also diversity, because I am a person of color, and there've been a lot of women who have paved the way that has allowed me to be in this position.”

Before joining the Nittany Lions softball program, Crowell saw success in her own softball career. Her accomplishments in the college softball world started in her playing days at Virginia Tech, where she was a three-time All-Conference selection and was later inducted into the Virginia Tech All-Sports Hall of Fame in 2013.

After a couple of stops as an assistant at Syracuse and Oklahoma State, Crowell earned her first head coaching gig in 2013 with Miami (OH). In 2016, Crowell guided the Red Hawks to a MAC Tournament Championship, earning a spot in an NCAA Regional for the first time in four years.

In Crowell’s final year with Miami, she was named MAC Coach of the Year after leading the Red Hawks to a 35-16 record and regular-season conference title. This achievement paid off for Crowell as she made the jump to the Big Ten the next year.

“You dream about having this life that I'm able to live right now, where you're living out your dream, because this is what I've always wanted to do,” Crowell said. “It truly has been a blessing for me, this profession, and what softball, the sport in itself, has brought to me.”

Becoming a high achiever and working to reach a high leadership role isn’t easy, but Crowell has passed on the success. Under her tutelage, the program saw many high-character student-athletes bring home accolades both on and off the diamond.

Lexie Black won both the Big Ten Medal of Honor, the highest academic honor in the conference, and the Big Ten Postgraduate Scholarship in 2023, both firsts for the softball program. In 2025, Crowell continued the pipeline of high achievers, with Maddie Gordon bringing home the Medal of Honor, and six athletes achieving Big Ten Academic accolades.

From Black, who went on to study at Iowa State’s College of Veterinary Medicine program, or other players coached by Crowell, like outfielder Haylie Brunson, who, after graduating last season, joined Longwood as a graduate coach, there’s no shortage of players coached by Crowell making changes in the real world.

“The mindset that I have as a professional and just on a personal level, is there's no such thing as failure,” Crowell said. “You're going to be stronger because of the adverse moments that you've had.”

In Crowell’s first season with Miami, the team finished 20-34, and improved by just two wins the next year. However, after that, Crowell and the Red Hawks completed three 30-win seasons and fell just short of 30 in the other two.

With the Nittany Lions, Crowell went 7-34 in her debut season, but that wasn’t a preview of how the blue and white would continue to perform under its new program head.

Following this first season in 2021, Crowell led Penn State to a 25-win increase in 2022, which was the largest single-season turnaround by any team in Division I. The Nittany Lions continued their incline, with 30-plus wins in 2023 and 2024.

“I've been through, you know, some tough seasons that didn't go the way that I had planned," Crowell said. “I find ways to grow and to get better and I think I've been able to do that in my career.”

When it comes to leading a softball team, teaching her players to work through adversity is one of the life lessons Crowell uses to help them become great leaders themselves one day.

The key to that is the support group Crowell assembled. It’s unique and unprecedented; currently, her coaching staff is one of three at Penn State made up entirely of women. In an area like coaching, where underrepresentation is an issue, there’s no lack of representation when Crowell’s players glance from the field to the dugout.

“We are going to prepare you to be elite leaders in whatever profession it is that you have in life,” Crowell said. “A lot of the things that we teach on the field apply to what they do in life, how to overcome adversity, how to get through hardships, how to build your toughness.”

As people go through life, a question that often crosses their minds is how a younger version of themselves would view what they’ve become. This is especially true when achievements and accomplishments have come at a high level and may even feel unthinkable.

For Crowell, her younger self would be nothing short of proud because it’s taken a lot to get where she is.

“My younger self would say, I'm proud of how hard you have worked and where you have gotten, but never settle and continue to get one percent better every day,” Crowell said. “And don't ever forget about where you come from and the people who have helped get you there and be thankful every day for where you're at.”

Those words ‘don't ever forget about where you come from’ are the secret behind Crowell’s success and how she wouldn’t be where she is today without her biggest supporters – her family.

Crowell's family comes from Hawaii, she’s a first-generation college student, and her family values back in the Aloha State have been the most important part of her softball journey.

“My family is everything to me; my parents have had to work really hard their entire lives to support our family,” Crowell said. “I get my work ethic from my parents, and just seeing everything that they gave to our family, and there's absolutely no way I would be where I am today, without my family.”

There’s another importance that comes from being a woman in a leadership position like Crowell is, and that’s being able to lead a group of young female athletes.

"It's an honor to have this role that I have because of the impact that I can have on our student athletes,” Crowell said, “It's important for them to see just the amount of female leaders, elite level leaders that we have here at Penn State, and I want them to always be surrounded by strong women like that.”

title: Penn State baseball energized by dominant offense in win over FDU
author: Dominic Megats I The Daily Collegian
description:
It’s been a cold spring in Happy Valley; however, the bats for Penn State heated up against FDU in its second Dollar Dog Night of the season.

After what was a back-and-forth opening to the game, the Nittany Lions used the home half of the second inning to get five runs across the plate. This led to a dominant 11-5 win over the Knights in front of a crowd of 2,014 fans.

“We are working to make sure that we build a program that does that every night, but especially to stick with us when we’re not playing baseball the way any of us want to be playing right now,” coach Mike Gambino said. “This is not the standard we’ve set over two years and for the people who keep coming out we will reward them.”

Offensive contributions were displayed up and down the lineup throughout the contest. By the bottom of the third inning, seven out of the nine Penn Staters in the lineup had a hit. Ultimately, the blue and white were able to get 15 hits in the win.

Bryce Molinaro earned his sixth multi-RBI game as he continues to climb the program rankings. Jayden Davis and Spencer Barnett joined him with a pair of RBIs. As a team, the Nittany Lions showed efficient hitting throughout the game as they were able to consistently bat in runners.

“(Molinaro’s) having better bats consistently,” Gambino said. “He was having good at-bats, but he’s now consistently having better at-bats. His swing decisions are good. He’s hammering the baseball. He’s looking like (Molinaro).”

Not only were the bats alive, but the runners on base were constantly on the move. Penn State earned six stolen bases, its most in a game on the season. Catcher Joey DeMucci led the pack with three.

The on-base awareness was on full display primarily in the second inning. DeMucci got the party started when he stole third. Shortly after, the Swarthmore, Pennsylvania, native stole home as a double steal forced a throw to second base.

“We want to be aggressive,” Gambino said. “We want to run the bases. It’s just harder to do that when you’re chasing. We had an early lead, so we can go be aggressive.”

The action-packed second inning continued after Michael Anderson was hit by a pitch. With two runners on, Molinaro ripped an RBI double into left field for his second extra-base hit of the game. Barnett followed suit with a shot up the middle of the field, scoring two additional runs. Jack Porter was the last Nittany Lion to earn an RBI in the inning after flying a single into center field.

An 8-3 lead was challenged quickly as FDU was able to answer with a run of its own in the top half of the third. However, a strong Penn State bullpen kept the Nittany Lions in a comfortable lead throughout the game.

Gambino brought in five relievers who allowed two earned runs and struck out ten batters.

The win marks its fourth double-digit victory of the season for the blue and white. Although not a conference win, the Nittany Lions were able to get back into the win column heading into another Big Ten road trip.

“I think the last three performances combined going into Columbus this weekend against Ohio State is huge for us,” Robert Brown III said. “It gives you so much confidence, so much more to play for. You get the taste of winning, and you just want to keep getting it. You get addicted to that for sure.”

title: Penn State baseball defeats FDU on Dollar Dog Night behind hot day at the plate
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Nittany Lions returned home and looked to snap their five-game losing streak against FDU on the second Dollar Dog Night of the season on Tuesday night. A streak would be broken by the end of the contest as the Knights entered Happy Valley riding a five-game winning streak.

Penn State successfully snapped its losing skid and got back on the right foot with an 11-5 win over FDU behind a dominant offense.

“Nobody’s been tremendously happy the last couple weeks. I’m glad that we’re in a spot. For 25 years this program is where it just was,” coach Mike Gambino said. “And now none of us are happy where we are, but we have a long way to go.”

Both offenses broke the scoring open early in the first. Gambino started right-hander Mason Horwat and the junior struggled with his command. Horwat surrendered five walks in just 1.2 innings over his last outing and the theme continued with Horwat walking the first two FDU batters.

Garryn Plummer then reached off a dribbler back to the mound and the Knights quickly had bases loaded with no outs. Horwat recorded a pivotal strikeout, but then walked the next batter and FDU took a 1-0 lead.

However, Horwat limited the damage and struck out two straight Knights to end the threat. Despite FDU striking first, the Nittany Lions quickly jumped on right-hander Chris Mattioli.

Penn State’s hottest bat got the offense rolling. Designated hitter Michael Anderson ripped a one-out double into center and second baseman Bryce Molinaro brought him home off a hard-hit triple to right.

Though the game was tied at one, the blue and white weren’t done scoring in the frame. Right fielder Jack Porter kept the inning alive and snuck a two-out RBI double down the left field line to pull Penn State ahead 2-1.

The Nittany Lions tallied one last run in the opening frame when first baseman Kevin Karstetter pulled a RBI single through the left side and extended the blue and white’s lead 3-1.

Yet FDU would knot the score right back up in the second. Travis Stroh and Johnathan Gomez roped a pair of singles into the outfield and the Knights’ biggest bat, Hunter Ray, plated Stroh off a rocketed double into center.

Plummer smacked a grounder to shortstop Preston Yaucher and tied the game at three. Though the tie wouldn’t last long as Penn State exploded in the bottom half of the second and scored five runs.

Catcher Joey DeMucci lined a leadoff single up the middle and Yaucher then reached with a fielder’s choice chopper to third. Some aggressive baserunning pressured Mattioli on the mound. DeMucci swiped third and Yaucher stole second on the next pitch.

“We want to be aggressive. We want to run the bases,” Gambino said. “We got an early lead so we could go be aggressive.”

Plummer’s unsuccessful throw to second plated DeMucci, which gave Penn State the lead right back 4-3. A few batters later, Mattioli hit Anderson and Molinaro knocked his second double of the contest down the left field line to extend the Nittany Lions’ lead 5-3.

“[Molinaro’s] driving the baseball to all fields. He’s having better at-bats consistently,” Gambino said. “He was having good at-bats, but he’s now consistently having better at-bats.”

Continuing the offensive momentum, third baseman Spencer Barnett sent a two-RBI single up the middle and the blue and white were now up 7-3. Porter smoked a single to center and Barnett scored to make the score 8-3.

The Knights chipped away at their five-run hole when Jeremy Delamota lifted an RBI double to left field to bring FDU closer, 8-4. Yet right-hander Frankie Sanchez stopped the bleeding and surrendered just one run in the third.

However, the blue and white just kept pouring on the runs in its bottom half. Left fielder Jayden Davis barreled a two-RBI single up the middle to make the Nittany Lions’ lead 10-4.

Barring an FDU run in the fourth and a Penn State run in the eighth, both offenses were held fairly quiet throughout the duration of the game. Right-hander Ethan Bauerschmidt closed the door for the Nittany Lions and the squad improved to 9-21 with the victory.

Before Bauerschmidt was called upon in the ninth, left-hander Matthew VanOstenbridge delivered a flawless eighth.

“I did want to come back. I had a conversation with coach in the dugout, and I asked for it,” VanOstenbridge said. “But ultimately, there was no need to, we have Ethan (Bauerschmidt) coming in, another freshman, another talented pitcher and he’s going to come out there and get that close.”

Up Next

Penn State heads back on the road and travels to face Ohio State for a three-game series this weekend. The series opener is slated for 6 p.m. on Friday night and all games will be streamed on Big Ten Plus.

title: Former Penn State men’s lacrosse goaltender Jack Fracyon signs with PLL’s Utah Archers
author: Elliott Inscho | The Daily Collegian
description:
A former Nittany Lion is heading to the pros.

Former Penn State goaltender Jack Fracyon has signed a one-year contract with the Utah Archers of the PLL, the team announced on Tuesday.

Another brick wall 🧱We have signed goalie Jack Fracyon through the 2026 season! pic.twitter.com/aDwaEfIBkY— Utah Archers (@PLLArchers) April 7, 2026

Fracyon served as the blue and white’s starter between the pipes for three seasons, posting a .524 save percentage en route to 35 career wins.

The Nittany Lions made the NCAA Tournament in each of his seasons as the top netminder, including national semifinal appearances in 2023 and 2025.

The Annapolis, Maryland, native will join an Archers squad that prominently features two other former Nittany Lions. Attackman Mac O’Keefe and midfielder Grant Ament, who hold Penn State program records in goals and assists, respectively, have been fixtures in the Utah offense for several seasons.

title: Penn State women’s basketball’s Shaelyn Steele enters transfer portal
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After being just one of two players to not enter the transfer portal from last year’s Penn State squad, Shaelyn Steele is officially out per Talia Goodman of On3.

Steele announced her entrance to the transfer portal on Tuesday, becoming the last of the Lady Lions to depart from Happy Valley after Tanisha Wright was hired.

The Ashland, Kentucky, native spent the last two years on former coach Carolyn Kieger’s squad, seeing action in 46 games with eight total starts.

During her time with the Lady Lions, Steele averaged just 1.8 points and 2.7 rebounds per game, recording 39 steals in her career.

title: Penn State baseball hosts FDU with chance to get back in win column
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
The rocky campaign continued for Penn State over the weekend as Nebraska swept the Nittany Lions and now the squad looks to break its five-game losing streak against FDU on Tuesday night.

While the blue and white hope to get back on track, the Knights are hot and enter the game on a five-game winning streak. The Nittany Lions are currently 8-21, and if they want a chance at another postseason run, they must start stringing together some wins.

Penn State has enjoyed much success against FDU, holding a 3-0 record, with the two squads' last meeting in the 2025 season.

Battle on the mound

The main reason the Blue and White’s season has been so far underwhelming is the poor pitching. The Nittany Lions have posted the Big Ten’s worst team ERA for most of the season and have also surrendered the most hits, so if they want to pull off the victory, the pitching must simply limit runs.

On the other side, the Knights hold a steady team ERA at 5.18, allowing 87 hits and 57 earned runs. Penn State has been outscored by its opponents 260-152 and outhit 318-237, which is a stark difference from FDU, which has dominated most of the teams they’ve faced.

Junior Mason Horwat is likely to get the start on the mound for the Nittany Lions. The Pittsburgh native had a rough outing in his last start against Illinois, lasting just 1.2 innings and surrendering six runs on three hits and five walks.

The right-hander holds a 9.17 ERA and has allowed 22 hits, 11 walks and 19 earned runs over 17.2 innings. He faces an FDU offense that has a team batting average of .341 on 133 hits and 131 runs, and it's heading into Happy Valley.

The Knights rake throughout their lineup, but sophomore Ray Hunter spearheads their explosive offense. The Woodbine, New Jersey, native posts a .407 average and leads FDU in hits (22), RBI (21) and home runs (nine).

It’s unclear who’s getting the ball for the Knights, but Penn State may face FDU’s ace left-hander Chris Messina. The freshman has recorded the team’s second-lowest ERA at 1.86, while allowing just four earned runs, eight hits and four walks over 19.1 innings.

It hasn’t only been a bad start to the season on the mound for the Nittany Lions, but also behind the plate with a conference-worst team batting average of .255. However, the two stars of their lineup have been on a tear.

Senior Michael Anderson ranks among the leaders at Penn State in most offensive stats. Among those are a .366 batting average, 13 home runs and 34 RBI. The Havertown, Pennsylvania, native continued dominating over the weekend, totaling five hits in the Nebraska series, which included two home runs in the series finale.

Alongside him, redshirt junior Bryce Molinaro has picked up right where he left off last season and is a steady force in Mike Gambino’s lineup. The Drums, Pennsylvania, native has maintained a solid .279 average, with eight home runs and 22 RBI.

However, the Nittany Lions will need production from elsewhere in the lineup as well if they want to snap their losing skid.

title: Column | Penn State women’s lacrosse’s postseason potential
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After Kayla Treanor was hired as Penn State’s women’s lacrosse coach in May 2025, high expectations followed her into this season.

Treanor was coming off a stellar career with Syracuse, where she led her squad to four straight NCAA Tournament appearances, including back-to-back national semifinal appearances.

While this year’s Nittany Lions have been consistently ranked in the top 25, they experienced a three-game losing stretch to ranked opponents, with the previous two coming as top-10 losses.

With the postseason approaching in a little over two weeks, here’s a look at how the blue and white stacks up its postseason chances.

Big Ten Tournament

The top seven of nine total teams in the Big Ten earn a spot in the conference tournament, with the No. 1 team earning a quarterfinals bye. The Nittany Lions are fifth in the conference, with a 5-3 record and four games to go.

Penn State officially clinched a spot in the tournament following its win over Ohio State on Sunday, holding the tiebreaker if the Buckeyes win out, the Nittany Lions lose out and both sit on a 5-7 record.

Though Penn State secured a spot, its seed is still up for grabs, as it could earn as high as the first seed and as low as the seventh.

Northwestern currently leads the conference with an 8-0 record, followed by Maryland with just one loss. The Nittany Lions, Johns Hopkins and Michigan are tied with a 5-3 record and could rotate through those three seeds.

Penn State’s current fifth seed faces the fourth seed in the quarterfinals — which is tentatively Michigan — before facing the top seed in the tournament. Two weeks ago, the blue and white was blown out by Michigan 11-6 on Senior Day.

NCAA Tournament

The 2026 NCAA Tournament, which kicks off May 8, consists of 15 automatic conference qualifiers and an additional 14 at-large selections.

Considering Northwestern and Maryland’s dominance this season, the Nittany Lions likely won’t emerge as the conference champion. Instead, they’ll have an opportunity as one of the next 14-best teams in the country.

The current IWLCA poll features several Big Ten and ACC teams in the top-25, with all of them through the rankings.

No. 1 Maryland and No. 2 North Carolina are the highest-ranked Big Ten and ACC squads, giving those schools the automatic qualifications and conference titles in this situation.

Outside of No. 5 Yale, No. 8 Navy and No. 13 Stony Brook, the 14 at-large bids would be selected from the remaining of the top-19 teams in the country.

After being ranked No. 18 in the latest IWLCA poll, Penn State would earn the second-last bid to the tournament.

Even if they make the tournament, the Nittany Lions are 2-3 against ranked opponents, including 0-2 against top-10 teams, and would likely only win one game at most in this year’s tournament.

title: Weekly Recap | Penn State softball closes out Big Ten series with sweep against Maryland
author: Brielle Gari I The Daily Collegian
description:
The Nittany Lions have a 7-5 record against conference opponents after its second consecutive Big Ten sweep.

This past weekend, the blue and white hosted its second home series of the 2026 season at Nittany Lion Softball Park at Beard Field, facing off against the Maryland Terrapins.

Penn State showed out for its home crowd, outscoring Maryland 31-9 over the course of three back-to-back games. The first pitch of the series was moved up to early afternoon on Friday, and the Nittany Lions secured the sweep midday on Saturday after their doubleheader.

Now, the blue and white have a 26-12 overall record. Here are some of the highlights from the past weekend.

Clutch throughout lineup

The biggest takeaway from the blue and white’s dominant performances against the Terrapins is that everyone produced quality at-bats, from the top to the bottom of the lineup.

Freshman Outfielder Breanna Hanik has been the leadoff hitter for Penn State for good reason, as she went 2-for-3 at the plate during the weekend’s first game. Hanik scored a pair of runs, five RBIs and got a hold of opposing pitchers in ways that resulted in homers. She became the second Nittany Lion in program history to have two multi-homeruns in their freshman campaign.

Freshman Allison Oneacre was the first rookie to complete two multi-homer games earlier this season. The shortstop had a perfect outing on Friday, going 3-for-3, getting a single, double and home run and ultimately finishing with two RBIs.

Third-year trailblazer Brooke Klosowicz led the Nittany Lions to victory in both games on Saturday, where the Johnsburg, Illinois, native held a .500 batting average. For the first game of the day, she logged 3-for-3 at the plate and produced a trio of singles, with three RBIs.

But the moment the junior third baseman was known for was closing out the series finale after a scoreless defensive showdown, with a walkoff two-run home run.

Throughout the entire weekend, all of the Penn State offense was able to make contact in the batter's box.

Second baseman Michela Barbanente also hit for a .500 batting average and a .667 on-base percentage during the doubleheader last weekend. Center fielder Natalie Lieto went 2-for-4 in the course of both games, securing a home run, a pair of runs scored and three RBI.

What the past weekend has taught the blue and white is that, in clutch moments, the batting order is able to produce quality at-bats that create momentum no matter where in the order it’s in.

Bullpen steps up

During the three-game series against Maryland, the Penn State pitching staff used the depth of its bullpen very well. At critical moments throughout the game, the blue and white were successful in keeping the score close.

Freshman left-hander Brevyn Kellepouris earned the start on Friday. She allowed five hits and two earned runs across 1.1 innings pitched. McKenna Young jumped into the circle for relief and got the victory, allowing just one hit and a strikeout.

Sophomore righty Abigail Britton and junior Bridget Nemeth helped close things out on Friday, which gave the Nittany Lions the 13-5 win.

Four Penn State pitchers appeared in the circle during Saturday’s doubleheaders.

Nemeth got the start for the first half of games, pitching only 1.1 innings before Mackenzie Duncan, freshman right-hander, entered the game. She shut things down with 3.2 innings, allowing just one single and struck out three batters. Duncan earned credit for the win, improving to 5-1 in her rookie season.

Britton started things off in the second game, striking out three Terrapins, surrendering three hits and a pair of walks, and was able to keep a clean scoreboard.

Young closed out the series with 3.0 innings pitched in the finale. Surrendering a single hit, then sending three Maryland batters back to the dugout, closing out the 2-0 win.

The blue and white used the depth of its bullpen strongly throughout its Big Ten weekend.

title: Recruiting roundup | Penn State earns momentum with handful of commitments in 2nd week of spring ball
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
After a dry spell dating back to the mass turnover on staff, Penn State has gained some momentum on the recruiting trail with a handful of commitments after the second week of spring practice.

Here’s what happened on the recruiting front this past week.

Commitment surge

The 2026 recruiting class was on the smaller side for Penn State, signing 15 players with 10 coming from Iowa State. The lone recruit that committed under James Franklin and the old staff that stayed on board was defensive end Jackson Ford.

For the 2027 group, all four commits that pledged under Franklin backed off their pledges following his firing.

With the process of hiring Matt Campbell and a new coaching staff, it was bound to take a while to build momentum on the recruiting trail. They needed to re-establish prior relationships with recruits and high schools while creating new ones.

After a pair of Junior Days in January, paired with hitting the road and online communication with prospects, the Nittany Lions struck in the 2027 class by landing 3-star cornerback Semajay Robinson on March 28.

He kick-started the recruiting spree, as this past week Penn State added 4-star wide receiver Landon Blum, 3-star cornerback Zachary Gleason Jr., 3-star athlete Ka'ron Ceaser and 3-star defensive lineman Carter Blattner to the 2027 group.

It was a matter of time before the Nittany Lions picked up steam, and they’re trending for several more prospects. They signed just 15 recruits in the 2026 class, so the 2027 class is expected to be large.

If the momentum Penn State has currently continues, it’ll be well on its way to landing a bigger 2027 class.

Notable visitors

As Penn State looks for its quarterback in the 2027 class, a notable visitor showed up in 4-star Will Mencl. He hails from Chandler, Arizona, which is where offensive coordinator Taylor Mouser is from.

The schools to watch with him, in addition to Penn State, are Oregon and Auburn. Miami was in the mix as well, but the Hurricanes picked up a commitment from 4-star Israel Abrams over the weekend. Mencl, along with 4-star Peter Bourque, who’s arriving for a visit on Wednesday, are at the top of the board for Penn State.

A pair of former in-state 4-star 2027 commits also returned to campus in wide receiver Khalil Taylor and safety Gabe Jenkins. It was Taylor’s third time on campus since the new staff took over, visiting for spring practice in consecutive weeks in addition to a Junior Day visit in January. For Jenkins, it was his first spring ball visit but he also made the trip for Junior Day.

A notable re-offer was 4-star interior offensive lineman Tristan Dare, who made his way to campus after backing off of his pledge to Michigan.

Other important names that made it to campus were 4-star cornerback Dhillon McGee, 4-star linebacker Case Alexander, 4-star running back Aiden Gibson and 4-star tight end Cooper Terwilliger.

Terry Smith takeover

Penn State’s first three commitments came from the cornerback room, with Robinson, Gleason and Ceaser. Ceasar is currently listed as an athlete, but projects to play the position at the next level.

It makes sense that cornerbacks coach Terry Smith, who saw his reputation take off nationally after his stint as interim coach, cashed in early with the top targets on his board. While he’s made his name more well-known across the country, Smith has been an elite recruiter for Penn State entering his 13th year on staff.

Gleason and Ceaser are a pair of players who have been on Smith’s radar for a while now, while Robinson was recently offered in January, but a lone visit to State College and an online rapport with Smith did enough for him to commit to the Nittany Lions.

It doesn’t look like Smith is done just yet, as Penn State is trending for 4-star cornerback Kei’Shjuan Telfair to make it a quartet of cornerbacks on board.

While it certainly is a new look for the program on the recruiting front, the one thing that hasn’t changed is how Smith operates on the trail.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Autonomous Robotics Competition Club to compete in GoAERO with emergency response helicopter
author: Neel Ved | The Daily Collegian
description:
Correction: This story initially quoted Adkar as stating the group had been "employing proctor baking techniques." He actually said "rotor braking techniques." The Daily Collegian apologizes for this error.

The Penn State Autonomous Robotics Competition Club (ARCC) was selected to participate in the GoAERO competition, which is partly sponsored by NASA, where they’ll continue building an autonomous emergency response flyer capable of saving lives during natural disasters.

Penn State is currently one of 14 U.S. universities competing in the GoAERO competition with eight out of the 14 being selected as Stage 2 winners, meaning they advance to the final fly-off in 2027. The GoAERO competition is a three-year, $2 million global challenge prize, to design and build teams’ prototypes.

The team is currently building a helicopter called the Soteria Emergency Response Flyer for the competition. In 2025, ARCC won the first stage of the X-Prize Wildfire Challenge, a four-year competition that tasks teams with developing technology to fight wildfires.

According to Rachel Axten, ARCC’s president, the team is interested in working on more wildfire-related projects, so the competition came at the perfect time.

“For us, it was a system that can be deployed to keep pilots and other crew members safe in situations they do not need to be in,” Axten, a PhD candidate in aerospace engineering, said. “Maybe it was delivering supplies or extracting someone that has been stuck in an area that has been consumed by a wildfire."

Axten said two years ago, there were 25 wildfire-related deaths from aerial crews.

Penn State is one of a few teams coming from academia, with the rest coming from the aerospace industry. Axten said being competitive with professional companies that have higher levels of funding is another vote of confidence.

“For me and the team it is really another indication that the work we are doing is not only important from a research standpoint, but also that the industry is interested in the missions that we are pursuing with the helicopter and the total design,” Axten said.

Being the leader of ARCC has been a great opportunity for Axten, she said, as she’s been able to work with her team on very difficult robotics problems. These issues are usually related to research being conducted in different departments, especially aerospace.

“As a graduate student, you are working on a specific research problem and you are the one that owns some part of that problem,” Axten said. “To be part of a team of students, faculty and researchers to pursue a system-level problem with people you know, doing all different parts of a drone or ground robot or whatever it may be has been my favorite part.”

Maxwell Palamarchuk said he joined ARCC because he was always interested in remote controlled aircrafts, and this team was the next step for him.

“I felt like I could offer my services and skills to the team in order to keep trying to push forward in these competitions,” Palamarchuk, a graduate student studying aerospace engineering, said. “It is a great organization that offers a lot to students and that we could try to get valuable, technical experience, as well as even writing, in the case of GoAERO.”

Palamarchuk said access to resources and previous technology will greatly help the group in the competition, as it gives the team an advantage over its competitors.

“Our methodology of our vehicle being a gas-powered helicopter is the most optimal for the weight, endurance and cost,” Palamarchuk said. “This allows us to score the maximum that we can.”

Palamarchuk said having a prepared vehicle that would just need to be modified instead of being built from scratch is another advantage for the team. The developmental side of the project, the amount of work that goes into building this kind of vehicle, gives them a lot of experience.

One of the main features of the Soteria Emergency Response Flyer is that it’s designed to be a fully autonomous aerial vehicle. Though, as with any autonomous vehicle, there is the fear that it may not be safe.

However, ARCC is working to ensure their vehicle is as safe as possible. Ameya Adkar said the flyer has a lidar instrument, which analyzes Earth’s surface, and perception devices to monitor the drone’s safety. There are also cameras and sensors that feed into the helicopter's control system algorithms designed to avoid obstacles, making for a very safe operating environment.

“The GoAERO has safety rules that we are following,” Adkar, a third-year studying aerospace engineering and physics, said. “It includes leeway from obstacles and persons. We are also employing rotor braking techniques to control the rotor blades while the helicopter is on the ground.”

The flyer features autonomous waypoint following and collision avoidance, so it’s vital for the helicopter to be able to land in areas where there may be items from moving debris — like in a chaotic disaster zone — without the human input. For example, it could independently fly over a wildfire without a person directly controlling it.

Currently, there are redundant algorithms that help the flyer determine longitude and latitude, if the flatness of the ground and any obstacles in the diameter of the rotor.

After considering these variables, the helicopter then determines where it can safely land. If any human input is needed, then the helicopter will use waypoints to guide the human input and land by itself.

As ARCC prepares for final fly-off in 2027, they are hoping to improve the overall performance of the flyer by continuously updating the vehicle over the summer.

“As of right now, it is basically implementing that and then just going out and flying and testing and flying and testing,” Palamarachuk said. “So the more that we can run through these practice runs, the more confidence we gain, which will have an impact on our overall performance.”

MORE CAMPUS COVERAGE

title: Reminders through rhymes | 6 poems every college student should read
author: Avery Pelletier I The Daily Collegian
description:
From haikus to limericks to sonnets to found poems, many of us grew up writing poetry throughout our time in elementary and middle school. Our teachers showed us how to create rhyme schemes, play around with words and create stories that traditional writing cannot always convey.

However, aside from the occasional English class, many college students have not only left writing, but also reading poetry to their earlier academic years. Between classes, jobs and internships, many feel as though they don’t have time to spend on haikus. Yet, with all these potential stressors, a short poem to make someone smile could be what many students need.

In honor of April being National Poetry Month, here's a list of six poems all college students should read in order to connect with the writing that raised us all.

“Be Drunk” by Charles Baudelaire

While the title may be incredibly forward, this poem by French poet Charles Baudelaire is more than a story about traditional intoxication, but a reflection on how to live life.

In his writing, Baudelaire explains that for us to feel truly alive and make it through the challenges of life, we must immerse ourselves fully into something and allow the feeling of being “drunk” overtake us. However, he explains this can be done through a plethora of things like art, poetry or music depending on the person.

This poem is perfect for a student who may be feeling uncertain about the future and needs a reminder to soak in every moment they experience.

“Having a Coke with You” by Frank O’Hara

In one of his most famous poems, Frank O’Hara writes about celebrating the simple pleasures in life and the company we enjoy it with.

For many college students, it can be incredibly easy to give up personal time with friends because of responsibilities and deadlines. Though, this poem suggests that when we do this, we miss out on what life truly has to offer.

“Having a Coke with You” is a poem for students who need a reminder to appreciate the little things. College is a short period of time in the long run and is one meant to be savored.

“O Captain! My Captain!” by Walt Whitman

Popularized by the cult classic “Dead Poets Society,” this poem by Walt Whitman is more than just the short quote mentioned in the movie. Even though it originally honored former President Abraham Lincoln after his assassination, this poem is about honoring something that has guided you through hard times to get you where you are today.

In a time of transition where many feel they have to leave their friends and professors behind for their future, “O Captain! My Captain!” is a story that resonates and helps us remember those who guided us on our paths.

“Dreams” by Langston Hughes

In a time where life’s path may seem different from personal goals, “Dreams” by Langston Hughes can offer a short reminder about the possibilities of our dreams.

Comparing a life without dreams to “a broken-winged bird,” Hughes emphasizes how important personal aspiration is to the quality of life. With so many of us feeling the pressure to ditch childhood dreams for stability, Hughes’ reminds us not to.

“Dreams” is a poem, not only for the students trying to pick their majors or careers, but a reminder for all of us that our dreams are valid.

“Wild Geese” by Mary Oliver

One of the biggest struggles college students’ face is perfectionism, making “Wild Geese” by Mary Oliver a must-read.

With its strong imagery and storytelling, this 18 line poem is more than just a collection of words, but a reminder for readers to not strive for perfection, but rather self acceptance.

Since the end of the semester is when many students typically feel the most pressure to reach for perfection, this poem is for those who need a quick reminder that doing their best and being proud of themselves is what really matters.

“The Walrus and the Carpenter” by Lewis Carroll

In this whimsical, absurdist poem about a walrus and a carpenter on a beach adventure, author Lewis Carroll uses both rhyme schemes and clever storytelling to produce a humorous poem.

Even though it may be one of the longer options on this list, this poem creates such an absurd storyline readers are automatically pulled into and need to finish.

This poem is a great recommendation for a student who just needs a distraction from all their responsibilities and a laugh to make them feel better.

MORE BLOGS

title: Weekly Recap | Penn State baseball's pitching goes inconsistent against Nebraska, drops weekend series
author: Dominic Megats I The Daily Collegian
description:
The Nittany Lions return to Happy Valley after being swept by conference foe No. 19 Nebraska. After an unsuccessful trip, Penn State fell to an 8-21 overall record and a 3-9 Big Ten record.

Although coming up short over the weekend, Penn State’s offense managed to keep two out of the three games close until the end. The second game of a Saturday doubleheader was a different story as the Cornhuskers capitalized on poor pitching, ultimately run-ruling the blue and white.

When matched up against an offense that is known to get on base regularly, the Nittany Lions had to respond quickly in order to keep up with Nebraska. Not only was it able to do that at times over the weekend, but Penn State held a lead as high as four runs against a stout Cornhuskers pitching staff.

A name that has been uttered countless times throughout the season shines yet again. Michael Anderson has spent his first year as a Nittany Lion terrorizing opposing pitchers. That didn’t stop against his first-ranked opponent of the year.

The Haverton, Pennsylvania, native returns to his home state fresh off adding five RBIs and two home runs to his name. Anderson is now tied in second in the Big Ten with 13 homers, two shy of first in the conference.

However, due to being a recent transfer portal addition to the team, Anderson isn’t able to say he is climbing program history. Bryce Molinaro used this series to do just that. After a two-run shot in Game 1 of the series, Molinaro tied himself at fourth in career home runs. The Drums, Pennsylvania, native is only three homers away from sole possession of second in program history.

The two heavy-hitting righties led their offense to a 14-run weekend; however, the pitching continued its struggle against a strong Nebraska lineup.

After Friday’s game was canceled due to a storm, coach Mike Gambino had to strategize a bullpen that would have to pitch 18 total innings. The group struggled after a close Game 1 and let up 13 runs in the second game of the doubleheader.

However, Mason Butash continued to be a shining light in what is a dark area on the team currently. The Chantilly, Virginia, native came in to relieve Isaiah Shayter in the final game of the series. After reaching three innings in his second-straight outing, Butash struck out two and didn’t allow an earned run before being pulled.

After allowing six earned runs in his first appearance, the junior has only allowed four in his last 13 innings.

The blue and white will look to end its pitching woes against FDU on Tuesday before heading into a conference series against Ohio State.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Animal rights activists table to protest animal exploitation
author: Jonathon Chiu | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Allied Scholars for Animal Protection (ASAP) set up a table outside the Westgate Building on Monday to promote veganism and combat animal exploitation.

ASAP is a 17-chapter nonprofit that helps college students advocate for human and nonhuman animals through various initiatives, such as promoting plant-friendly diets and educational resources on college campuses.

The table, which was set up around 10 a.m., had animal plushies on plates and signs directing people to Elwood’s Organic Dog Meat, a website comparing public views toward consumption of livestock to those held for dogs. Pamphlets detailing the health and environmental impacts of animal exploitation were also handed out.

Christian Maxey, ASAP’s campus coordinator, said several students had reached out to the organization to potentially start a chapter at Penn State.

He said the table is meant to make people question their beliefs about how animals are treated in the farming industry by presenting them through the same lens as household pets.

“These animals that we exploit are just as capable and just as valuing of their lives as the animals that we love and care about, like dogs and cats,” Maxey said. “We're just asking people fundamentally if they agree that animals should be treated with respect, or if they think animals do deserve to be exploited.”

Maxey said he wanted the table to encourage more students to voice their beliefs on how they want to see animals be treated, as well as to participate in veganism.

“Most students already have the value of wanting to respect animals and so we're just asking students to really evaluate if there's any truly respectable way to exploit someone else,” Maxey said.

Faraz Harsini, ASAP’s founder and CEO, said animal exploitation is often ignored as it’s not prevalent in peoples’ daily lives. Speciesism, the belief that one species is superior to another, is also used to separate pets from other animals, according to Harsini, which should be treated the same as racism and sexism between humans.

“We don't see … all the exploitation that happens,” Harsini said. “What we see is that you go to grocery stores and they put pictures of happy animals on packages. It's already chopped up to pieces, and so it's out of sight, out of mind.”

Harsini said it’s morally imperative for people to speak out against animal exploitation and make changes in their lives to help combat this issue.

“I didn't become vegan because I stopped liking the taste of meat. I started asking questions and making the connection because I always loved animals,” Harsini said. “It's not just about not eating animals. It's about being somebody like Gandhi, like MLK, like Rosa Parks, to speak up, to treat this like injustice to animals.”

Angela Salamone stopped at the table after her boyfriend told her about it . Salamone, a third-year studying psychology, said while she had previously been a vegetarian, there should be a compromise where people don’t have to give up eating meat if they don’t want to.

“Other cultures, they celebrate the animal's life when they use it for their own purposes, so I feel like there's ways that people carry out slaughtering animals in more humane ways than maybe we do it,” Salamone said.

She said she believed people should be able to make their own choices about their diet while still believing that the food industry needs reform.

“I love a salad as much as the next girl, but I think that having a level diet is something that's important,” Salamone said. “I also think that the way that people go about killing animals is not good either; I've seen documentaries and it's quite disgusting. I don't think people should do that at all.”

title: Former Penn State men's basketball forward Hudson Ward enters transfer portal
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
A former Penn State player is headed to the transfer portal for the second time in his career.

Western Michigan forward Hudson Ward decided to enter the portal, he announced Monday via Instagram.

This move comes after Western Michigan saw a head coaching change from Dwayne Stephens to Kahil Fennell this offseason. Ward appeared in 13 games during the 2025-26 season, averaging 4.5 points and 3.4 rebounds per game.

Ward spent just one season in Happy Valley, which was his freshman year in 2024-25. He saw action in eight games, recording five rebounds, one assist and one block.

Ward was a 247Sports composite 4-star in the 2024 class out of Alberta, Canada.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Former Penn State women’s basketball forward Maggie Mendelson commits to Pepperdine
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Former Penn Stater Maggie Mendelson has found her home for her final year of collegiate basketball.

Mendelson announced her commitment to Pepperdine on Monday.

Despite having a professional career ahead of her, Mendelson will play one more season of basketball with coach Katie Faulker and the Waves, who are coming off a 21-13 record last season.

title: Penn State women's volleyball hires Abby Detering as assistant coach
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
A Penn State alum is returning to the team as a staffer.

The Nittany Lions have hired Abby Detering as an assistant coach, the team announced Monday.

Welcome BACK to Happy Valley, @AbbyDetering!📰 https://t.co/QtERFl4KtS#WeAre pic.twitter.com/oRePwBBLgC— Penn State Women’s Volleyball (@PennStateVBALL) April 6, 2026

Detering brings seven years of coaching experience with her to Happy Valley. She was most recently the associate head coach at Rutgers in 2025 after spending the previous three years as an assistant coach.

Detering was a setter for the Nittany Lions in 2016 and 2017, earning an AVCA All-America honorable mention during her senior year.

“Penn State has always held a special place in my heart,” Detering said in a news release. “The legacy of this program and the people who built it have a had a lasting impact on me. I couldn’t be more excited to return as an assistant coach. I’m so thankful to Katie for this opportunity and can’t wait to get to work. We Are!”

author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor

Staying put.

Penn State saw no movement in the AVCA Poll this week, remaining at No. 15.

Despite suffering its first loss of the season Thursday, @UCLAMVB remains No. 1 in the AVCA National Collegiate Men’s Volleyball Poll. @USCmensvolley has won eight straight matches, which is the longest win streak of any ranked team.https://t.co/ixnlNlqyKI#WeAreAVCA #NCAAMVB pic.twitter.com/rKEPLTNrej— AVCA (@AVCAVolleyball) April 6, 2026

title: Penn State women's lacrosse rises in latest IWLCA poll
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After its first victory in nearly a month, Penn State is on the rise again.

The Nittany Lions rose three spots to No. 18 in the latest IWLCA poll following their defeat of then-No. 25 Ohio State.

Penn State was stuck at No. 21 for three straight weeks after falling in three straight contests to top-25 teams, but regained momentum on Sunday.

The blue and white will face unranked Oregon at 8 p.m. on Thursday for its first unranked contest since March 12.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Report: Penn State men's hockey forward JJ Wiebusch enters transfer portal
author: Maggie Hardy | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State is reportedly losing another key forward, as sophomore JJ Wiebusch enters the transfer portal, according to Mike Divver of NHL.com.

Penn State's J.J. Wiebusch to enter transfer portal, per sourceUndrafted winger put up 16-20-36 in 37 games— Mark Divver (@MarkDivver) April 6, 2026

Wiebusch put up 16 goals and 20 assists in 37 games played this season, finishing fourth on the team in scoring.

The sophomore was a part of the blue and white line dubbed the "Behind the Back Boys", with fellow forwards Matt DiMarsico and Charlie Cerrato, that helped drive the team's offensive production.

Wiebusch is yet another Nittany Lion to depart the program, after both Cerrato and DiMarsico, as well as goaltender Kevin Reidler and forward Aiden Fink, inked pro contracts earlier last week.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State women’s basketball freshman Viktoria Ranisavljevic enters transfer portal
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
It was a quick stint at Penn State for freshman Viktoria Ranisavljevic.

Ranisavljevic entered the transfer portal on Monday, per On3. After just one year with the Lady Lions, she becomes the ninth player to depart.

The Massagno, Switzerland, native started in nine of her 26 games her freshman year, averaging 4.6 points per game.

Coming to Penn State as an elite talent for the Swiss national team, Ranisavljevic saw a lot of playing time early in the season, but her playing time decreased as the season progressed.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Report: Penn State men’s basketball wing Eli Rice enters transfer portal
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
A Penn State sharpshooter is set to depart the team.

Wing Eli Rice is entering the transfer portal with two years of eligibility remaining, according to Sam Kayser of League Ready.

NEWS: Penn State sophomore Eli Rice is entering the transfer portal, source told @LeagueRDY.The 6-foot-8 wing began his career at Nebraska before spending the last two at Penn State, redshirting one.He averaged 6.7PPG and 1.7RPG this season. Shot 39% from 3. Repped by… pic.twitter.com/iyZxtKmZYD— Sam Kayser (@KayserHoops) April 6, 2026

The redshirt sophomore has spent two seasons in Happy Valley after transferring in from Nebraska following his freshman year. He missed the majority of the 2024-25 campaign with an injury, so this season was the most he’s played in his career.

Rice was a 3-point shooter for the Nittany Lions, knocking down 39% of his shots from deep. He shot 4-of-6 from the field against Washington to help secure a 63-60 win for Penn State on Feb. 11.

His shooting went on cold streaks at times as he didn’t make a single 3-pointer during the last five games of the regular season. He was then seemingly benched in the first round of the Big Ten Tournament against Northwestern as he didn’t see any minutes despite not having a known injury.

Regardless, Rice is looking to be more than just a 3-point threat.

“I'm known as a shooter, but I just want to expand my game to driving and being more of a playmaker,” Rice said on Feb. 26.

Across 29 appearances and 18 starts this season, Rice averaged 6.7 points and 1.7 rebounds per game.

title: Baseball, yoga, nature | This week in State College
author: Neel Ved | The Daily Collegian
description:
With only about a month left in the spring semester, finals are shifting to the forefront of student’s minds. As students approach finals season, there are still many events in State College to enjoy.

Here are some events that might interest you.

Monday, April 6

Yoga and Mediation

From 12:30 to 1:30 p.m., there will be a yoga and meditation session at the HUB-Robeson Center (HUB) in Room 232.

Students and faculty can join a rejuvenating yoga and meditation practice, with all sessions led by certified instructors. Everyone is welcome to join, regardless of experience level.

There will also be another session from 5:30 to 6:30 p.m. at the Pasquerilla Spiritual Centre in Room 107.

Supplies are limited, so it is recommended to bring your own.

ELEV8: Essential Legal Basics

From 5 to 7 p.m., the Penn State Chaiken Center for Student Success and First-Gen Advocates will be hosting an interactive program led by Penn State Dickinson Law students in Pattee Library’s Dewey room.

During the program, law students will discuss legal issues they want every young adult to know through activities, scenarios and discussions.

Registration for this event is required but free.

Tuesday, April 7

Shinrin-Yoku, Forest Bathing

From 4 to 5:30 p.m. at Sunset Park there will be forest bathing, a Japanese practice of restorative, mindful immersion in nature to improve one's health and reduce stress.

There will be individual and group practice with slow-paced walks, ramblings and lounges while taking in Sunset Park.

Registration for this event is not required, and free food will be provided.

Penn State vs. Fairleigh Dickinson University Baseball - Dollar Dog Night

At 5:30 p.m., the Penn State baseball team will take on Fairleigh Dickinson University at Medlar Field at Lubano Park.

Fans in attendance will be able to purchase hot dogs for $1. The game, like all Penn State baseball home games, is open and free to the public.

Wednesday, April 8

Intersections Film Series - “Sally”

From 7 to 9 p.m., Sustain Penn State will be hosting a free virtual screening of the award-winning documentary “Sally” as part of its Intersections Film Series.

The film dives into topics like sustainability, equality and social change through the life of American astronaut Sally Ride.

Registration for this event is required.

West Coast Swing Dance Lesson and Social Dance

At The Press: Cider & Craft Beverage Taphouse from 7 to 9 p.m., community members can stop by to learn the west coast swing dance, hosted by the Swing Dance Club. There will be two 30-minute lessons for both intermediate dancers and beginners.

The intermediate lessons will start at 7 p.m., and the beginner lessons will start at 7:30 p.m. The last hour of the event will be social dancing.

Anyone is invited to come, as no experience is needed, and can arrive solo or with a partner. Registration for this event is required.

Thursday, April 9

Build Your Career Skills

At 11 a.m. to noon at the Bank of America Career Services Building, the Career Services Team will be leading a workshop to help students learn how to market themselves.

During the workshop, the team will go over what employers are looking for, how to stand out and how to match one’s academic, professional and leadership experiences to the current job market.

Registration for this event is not required.

Friday, April 10

Penn State vs. Northeastern Softball

At 5 p.m., the Penn State softball team will take on Northeastern at the Nittany Lion Softball Field & Beard Field.

All home women’s softball games are free to both students and the public.

Happy Valley Improv Comedy Show

From 7 to 8:30 p.m., Happy Valley Improv will be hosting its weekly Friday night improv comedy show at the Blue Brick Theatre. The show will feature 3 long-form improv sets with a break in between each set.

The price to attend this event is $13.70.

Saturday, April 11

Stone Valley Lake Days

From 10 a.m. to 5 p.m., Nature and Art Rx and Campus Recreation will be spending the day at Stone Valley Recreation Area.

Participants will have the chance to go hiking at Lake Perez, go boating, have a picnic, register for a program and just be outdoors. Boats are available on a first come-first serve basis and start at $10 per hour.

There will also be some lawn games and self-guided hikes available. It’s recommended to bring a water bottle, sunglasses, sunscreen, a small backpack and whatever items you may need for the activities you would like to do.

Students can sign up for free shuttles leaving from the Intramural Building.

MORE CAMPUS CONTENT

title: Column | Long live jazz
author: Emma Strickling | The Daily Collegian
description:
During his monologue during the 98th Academy Awards last month, host Conan O’Brien addressed Timothée Chalamet’s controversial comments about how “no one cares” about ballet and opera. “They’re [the opera and ballet communities] just mad you left out jazz,” O’Brien said jokingly.

If it’s not the butt of the joke, jazz generally tends to fly under the radar. In comparison to other genres, few engage with it. There are modern day jazz artists like Samara Joy and artists that have introduced a jazz- infused style of pop music like Laufey. Regardless, jazz is still a fairly niche genre.

Created by the Smithsonian’s National Museum of American History to celebrate jazz and honor the genre’s history, Jazz Appreciation Month (JAM) is recognized every April.

The genre’s origins can be traced back to the beginning of the twentieth century in New Orleans. It’s primarily a product of the African American community, where African musical traditions, spirituals, blues, European harmonic structures and ragtime were fused into the genre we know today. It eventually spread to major cities like Chicago, New York and St. Louis during the Great Migration and further gained popularity

One of the more defining characteristics of jazz is the use of improvisation, which is when a musician creates their own melodic solo on the spot in accordance with the rest of the band members. With this in mind, it makes jazz one of the freer musical genres. Improvisation allows the musician in that moment to express themselves by being in creative control. The listener is at their mercy.

It’s unpredictable and spontaneous. It’s a nice change of pace from the popular music of today that’s often repetitive and predictable.

Jazz is a collective effort, placing an importance on group collaboration. Jazz lets everyone in the ensemble shine by having many opportunities for multiple players to solo in a song. It’s unlike other genres where normally leading instruments like guitar are the ones to solo and therefore get the most recognition. It’s a more democratic art form.

One of the things that attracted me to jazz is how it can capture emotions within the song. There seems to be a song that can accurately capture any mood.

A driving drum beat with a fast rhythmic pattern on the ride cymbal creates frantic energy. The slow and brooding horn section brings melancholy. The warmth of the alto singer’s voice is like an aural sunshine.

It’s storytelling, regardless of whether the song is lyrical or not. The musicians are telling a story through their instruments and are also communicating with the other instrumentalists of the ensemble during the song.

I feel like jazz turns people off because it gives the impression that you need to have a music background to enjoy it. It’s been labeled “too intellectual,” whatever that means. You don’t need to have a deep understanding of music theory to enjoy it. Like I mentioned above, jazz is able to convey emotion. Chances are if the song is creating or reminding you of an emotion, it’s probably doing the same for someone else.

Therefore, it allows us to connect with others, similar to other genres. It’s only deemed intimidating and inaccessible because someone put that label on it. It requires a higher level of attention than other styles of music, but to label it inaccessible because of an unwillingness to be present isn’t fair.

Similar to anything, becoming familiar with jazz is about finding what you like. There are several subgenres to jazz including swing, bebop, cool, and avant-garde to name a few. They vary in tempo, themes, musical characteristics and ensemble size. But at the end of the day, they all create the diverse tapestry that is jazz.
